item.title: [Lex Clips] How FFmpeg works - explained by FFmpeg & VLC developers | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的发现：ffmpeg 不是工具，是一种"被遗忘者的副语言"

大多数人把 ffmpeg 当作命令行工具，但你仔细听 JB 的描述会发现一个惊人的事实——ffmpeg 实际上是人类目前规模最大的"由死者参与维护"的活体系统之一。"我们有数千名贡献者，其中一些人已经不在人世了。"这句话被轻描淡写地带过，但它揭示了一个奇异的本体论问题：当你在 YouTube 上播放一段视频，你的播放管线里运行着至少几行由已故工程师写下、且因法律上的著作权约束而永远无法被替换的代码。换句话说，全球视频文明的底层，是一座由活人无法单方面拆除的"代码陵墓"。许可证（GPL/LGPL）在这里不是法律条文，而是一种近似宗教的"祖先崇拜协议"——它强制你尊重那些已经无法回应邮件的人。

跨领域连接：ffmpeg 的命令行 ↔ 巴洛克音乐的通奏低音（basso continuo）

你提到 ffmpeg "是一门语言"，几千字符的命令行像程序生成的。这让我想到一个出人意料的类比：ffmpeg 的命令行美学，在结构上等同于巴洛克时代的"数字低音记谱法"。作曲家只写一行低音线 + 数字标记，演奏者据此即兴展开整段和声。ffmpeg 用户也是如此——给定一行 -vf 滤镜链，"演奏者"（解码器、滤镜图、复用器）即兴生成完整的音视频结构。这意味着 AI 现在被用来生成 ffmpeg 命令并不是"自动化"，而是回归——它让我们重新进入一种"指令即乐谱、执行即演奏"的前现代编程范式。Photoshop 和 After Effects 走的是相反的路（所见即所得、肌肉记忆驱动），所以图像领域始终没有出现 ffmpeg 的等价物——不是技术不足，而是视频本质上是时序信号，时序信号天然适合"乐谱式"描述，而静态图像不适合。这或许解释了 ImageMagick 永远到不了 ffmpeg 的广度。

隐藏的系统性真相：开源许可证是"防熵协议"，不是法律工具

普通叙事说 GPL/LGPL 是"保护自由"的法律手段。但 JB 真正在说的是另一件事：许可证是社区唯一能够约定的最低公约数。"社区在许可证之外不会就任何事达成一致。"这一句话颠覆了所有"开源社区有共同价值观"的浪漫化叙述。真相是：许可证不是共识的结果，而是共识的替代品——它是一种把"无法对齐的人类意志"外包给抽象法律结构的工程手法，让数千名互不信任、互不相识、政治宗教语言全不同的人，能够在不需要彼此认同的前提下共同建造一个系统。

更深一层的洞见：他亲身去拜访那位工厂工人父亲、为已故儿子签授权书的故事，揭示了开源世界一个鲜有人讨论的真相——代码的所有权不会因为作者死亡而消失，它会"传染"给继承人。这意味着每一个长期存活的大型开源项目，本质上是一个不断扩张的、跨代的、跨法域的法律生命体，它越成功，越难以改变形态。这与生物进化中的"棘轮效应"（Muller's ratchet）惊人相似：复杂性一旦积累，回退的成本会指数级上升。ffmpeg 的 GPL→LGPL 迁移之所以英雄主义，是因为它对抗的不是法律，而是熵本身。

一个让人意外的观察：ffmpeg 的存在让"专业"与"业余"之间的差距被压缩到了几乎为零，但这创造了一个新的不平等维度

你提到"个人和万亿美元公司站在同一个技术起跑线上"。这是真的，但有一个未被讨论的副作用：当工具被完全democratize（民主化）之后，差距就从"工具占有"转移到了"工具语言流利度"。能写 3000 字符 ffmpeg 命令的人，和只会拖拽 iMovie 的人之间的鸿沟，可能比 1990 年代"拥有 50 万美元剪辑机的电视台"与"拥有 VHS 录像机的家庭"之间的鸿沟更大——因为前者是资本壁垒（可被时间和经济发展抹平），后者是认知壁垒（更难抹平，且会代际固化）。

AI 生成 ffmpeg 命令是这场新不平等的解药，也是它的加速器。它会进一步把"会用 ffmpeg"从一项技能变成一种"知道该问什么的元能力"——这恰恰是开源运动从未承诺要解决的那一层不平等。

📝 转录文本

什么是 FFmpeg

Lex： Uh let's talk about ffmpeg. What what is ffmpeg exactly?

嘉宾： FFmpeg is basically the low-level libraries for codec. So compressions and decompression, mixes and demoxes and filters. It's the core is this. And then you have a several tool which allow you to create a type of pipeline to process any type of video files. Uh and it's used as a library absolutely inside everything from VC to Chrome to your smart TVs to basically any video that you see online um you usually use ffmpeg. And ffmpeg in it has all those type of tools and sometimes depend on other libraries like x264, uh libvpx and others, right? So so it's really now the de facto um tool to process images.

草根与巨头的同一条技术栈

Lex： From a philosophical level I think it's incredible that your home videos, your your grandmother's home videos and trillion dollar corporations effectively are on a level playing field using the same technology stack.

嘉宾： Uh it wouldn't be a surprise, you know, these big companies just have 3,000-line ffmpeg commands. There are some that use the API, but there are some that just have long command lines. So yeah, there's a bunch of tools like literally command line tool, ffmpeg, of course, FF probe. There's libraries lib codec, libby v format, libby v fil filter, but the ffmpeg on the command line is like legendary because you can cut there's so many parameters. You can customize everything to help.

FFmpeg 命令行是一门语言

Lex： It's a language. It's an actual language.

嘉宾： It's an actual—yeah, you could think of it as a programming language.

Lex： Yeah, of course.

嘉宾： I'm sure, because so most of people they're going to take ffmpeg, file in, file out and specify the format, right? But you can—we've seen thousands of characters and we've seen also like people like doing um programming um uh generation of common lines to to to make ffmpeg. There is a ton of people who are using AI to generate common lines for ffmpeg because you have no idea what it is. But you can do specify so many filters right uh on common line right.

So um ffmpeg is this collection of toolbox for multimedia processing that everyone everyone uses, and everyone that is watching your videos are also using, right? You you're on YouTube—well, it's ffmpeg on on the client side. Well, the your server side—on the server side, the client side is probably Chrome—well, you're using ffmpeg also. And and you're using OBS to record—well, it's ffmpeg, right? You're using a ton of important like big box professional boxes—well, it's very possible that inside some part of ffmpeg is running.

实际用例：从片头片尾到字幕烘焙

Lex： I mean there there's like so many—just to give people an idea, like I I use ffmpeg a lot on on everything. Just trivial stuff like uh take a video, add an intro video and an outro video and fade one into the other, like uh what is it called, dip to black, like where it it dips and then shows the next video and does the same thing with the audio. Uh there's like a cross dissolve of the audio. So, it's a quiet—it quiets the audio and makes it loud again. [snorts]

And there's a bunch of stuff like uh showing the captions on screen, hard like baking the captions in. You can customize the font. You can do all kinds of layering of audio and video. There's a million things. And of course, all of that works like magically with basically any codec. Like anything you could shove in on the audio and the video side, it works. But it's like if you if you look like for example you can do things that you would do with Adobe After Effects—

嘉宾： In command line on ffmpeg, right? It's—and it's very interesting because for example for for for images, there is not such tool. There is a few tools but not with the breath of ffmpeg.

Lex： So ImageMagick has a similar kind of—

嘉宾： Yes, but you do some filters, complex filters, you don't have the equivalent of Photoshop in command line, right? But you for video, you have ffmpeg in command—

让复杂的事情变简单：使命与愿景

Lex： Yeah, it's incredible. I mean, it's like it's an example of a thing when a bunch of great people get together and they get a vision and they stick by that vision for many years, which is incredible.

嘉宾： And the vision behind—and the same for VC and FFmpeg—is that we make everything that is very complex easy to use for the normal people, for everyone, right? Our goal is to make something that is insanely complex technically and make it easy to use, right? And people they use VC, they drop a file, they don't realize how complex the file is, but they they play it. Or or people put any type of thing inside FFmpeg with complex filters and it just works like magically, right? And people—

And this is our mission, right? Make very complex things—

Lex： We wouldn't be here and you wouldn't be here if this required, you know, a traditional television studio setup. It's tools like ffmpeg that democratize this—the the podcast and streaming revolution, the YouTube revolution was caused, you know, FFmpeg was a big player in that because it it democratized this technology that was once in the '90s, for example, you needed equipment that cost hundreds of thousands of dollars to do compression. It was the size of a car. And now everybody has that at almost an exact level playing field. And that's something that's so remarkable. It gave voice to a lot of people.

And we just to clarify, we say you you wouldn't be here, not the human, but the podcast.

嘉宾： The podcast, sorry. [laughter] You you as the—

Lex： I was still—

嘉宾： VLC did not have anything to do on a biological [laughter] creating me as a human.

视频成为最强媒介

嘉宾： You realize also everything move from text to images and images to video, right? Look look at social networks. Video is everywhere. It's the most powerful um medium there is, right? And and when you see shorts and and in reels and and TikTok, right? It's amazingly powerful to to to give—video is amazing for that, right? But the complexity is important.

Lex： This—what people don't realize. I mean this is really it gave power to the individual all across the world. It's real freedom. And I think I can't believe it but we still haven't mentioned the actual obvious thing for people who are not familiar, which it's open source. And there's a open-source community of users and developers behind it. So it's really it's a movement. So like we'll talk a bunch in a bunch of different ways about the community behind it, but uh can you speak to the open source element?

开源是什么：巧克力芝士蛋糕的比喻

嘉宾： So when we say what is ffmpeg, it's an open-source project. Yeah. So ffmpeg, VC, x264, video—everything we do is fully open source.

And for the people who don't understand how open source is, my usual analogy is about a chocolate cheesecake. Right? Um usually for you when you want to buy your cheesecake, you go to a bakery, they give you the cheesecake. Um, the other one way of having a cheesecake is have your grandma give you a recipe of how to make that.

When we do open source, we give you the chocolate cake and we give you the recipe to actually remake the same cake, but at the same times tell you how to build the oven, and also how you're allowed to modify the recipe and resell it to someone else. And this is because software is just a very long recipe of small instruction. Computers are not very clever. They go very very fast. So a normal program has tens of billions of instruction instead of the tens when you have your chocolate recipe.

So a lot of the software industry was about selling software like like where you just have like the final cheesecake. In open source we make—we give you everything. And that managed to get a lot of people work together, right? Because then you decide that you're going to make the best program, the best recipe for video, and you create communities.

Um in ffmpeg since the beginning of ffmpeg probably 2,000 to 3,000 people have contributed from the beginning, right? And then it's exactly like the Linux kernel, right? The Linux kernel has probably 10,000 people contributing everywhere. And they get together—well, mostly online, right? So they virtually get together to create the best tool for something. And on ffmpeg and VC is just like, well, this codec doesn't work so I'm going to work on the codec and I'm going to add the support for this file inside ffmpeg so it will be beneficial to everyone. Because again, we work for the greater good, we work for everyone. And that is what open source is.

围绕开源能否建公司

Lex： And we should mention depending on the licensing, uh you could probably build a billion dollar, maybe even a trillion dollar company around basically as a wrapper.

嘉宾： Well, yes—

Lex： People do.

嘉宾： People do, right? Uh there was a lot of uh um problems with uh mostly uh cloud providers who are basically running some open-source tools um in in the cloud and just give you uh the API to access to that. And there was a lot of uh um databases like or Elastic who change their license in order to avoid those type of uh of scenarios.

This is a question we get a lot in ffmpeg is why don't you do that, and you can't. We have we have thousands of contributors, some of whom aren't even alive anymore. You would need all of their agreement to do that. And and JB will go maybe a bit later and talk about how challenging that process was in VC to do the relicensing.

许可证作为社会契约

The license is a social contract in terms of who the de facto of the community. The community does not agree on much beside the license. People go around discuss around because of the license. And that also allow those license fork, right? Sometimes the community splits but it's possible because of the license then to merge back. And we've seen that so many times, right? GCC and GC um and EGCC in the past. We have seen for example all the web browsers, right? They started as web KHTML which becames WebKit and then which becames Blink, right?

So open-source license is like the the core of the community. And um people are coming from all around the world, very different type of uh religion um political borders, they they work in the same way on a project to solve a specific problem. And the specific problem we're working on is to make multimedia easy for everyone.

开源许可证地图

Lex： Uh, looking it up on Perplexity here, looking at the different open source licenses. Most major open source licenses fall into two buckets: permissive (very few conditions) and copy-left (share-alike requirements for derivatives). Below is a brief practical summary of the main ones you'll see in the wild. MIT license, BSD, ISC, uh Apache, uh GNU GPL, GNU AGPL. Where's LGPL? Yeah, LGPL. Let's see. There's the the Mozilla Public License. There's Eclipse Public License. It goes on. There's a lot of variety. I mean, I think I think the really popular ones is MIT, GPL, LGPL—

嘉宾： NBSD.

Lex： NBSD, Apache. Sometimes you see "unlicensed." That's an option—attempts to dedicate code to the public domain with a fallback permissive license.

嘉宾： There are many licenses for many different thing. What people don't understand that public domain is something that doesn't exist worldwide, right? So it's um all the open source licensing use the copyright law, right? The international copyright law in order to give rights on how you use a software or how you modify. It's de facto a a copyright license contract that you give to the end user or to the developer.

And so you have like the first one which are basically very permissive: MIT, BSD. You give the code and basically you do whatever you want, right? You take it, you want, you modify, you do what you want. Um and this is popular for uh uh JavaScript and the type of BSD operating system.

归属署名与 Copyleft 的强弱

Lex： So some of them—one one of the parameters is whether they require attribution, meaning if you use the code you have to say—

嘉宾： Yes. So in those type of permissive license, some you need to say if you use it, which is called attribution, and some you don't.

And then there is a the other part of license which are copyleft where you need to give back to the community your modifications. And with different string attached um, some weak uh copyleft license like um the Mozilla Public License, to some which are a bit stronger uh like GPL, or even very strong like AGPL.

So all of those are different um type of licensing that depends on what your goal are and how you want to structure your community, which is why I spoke about social contract because this is very important to understand.

Um ffmpeg and VC are mostly GPL or LGPL. Uh the Linux kernel is GPL. Uh but Android is Apache. Uh a ton of uh JavaScript framework that are using mostly MIT. Um all the BSD kernels, OpenBSD, NetBSD are of course BSD. Um and and so the it's philosophical change on how you want people to contribute back, basically.

从 GPL 迁移到 LGPL

Lex： So there's uh I think you talked about that [sighs and gasps] uh you've moved at one point from GPL to LGPL on certain parts of the project. What what can you describe the difference between the two and what does it take to move to—I guess a more permissive—so that direction is more permissive. LGPL is more permissive than GPL.

嘉宾： Yeah. So you have to realize that you can always go from more permissive to less permissive, right? Because of course those license are basically statement. So if you restrict, you can always restrict more, right? So in a GPL project you can take MIT code, but you cannot do the opposite, right? Because they are more constrained to to match.

Um indeed in fact I changed the core of libVLC, which is the engine of VC, from um GPL to LGPL. Uh and there were two reasons to do that.

理由一：让第三方应用嵌入 libVLC

The first one is that, so people can use um the VC engine, libVLC, into third party application. So a lot of applications which are playing video on your phone or on your tablet are actually VC engine in it, which is calling ffmpeg in it.

Yeah. Um so that was one of the way to create uh one of the company I created which is doing consulting and integration of those application, where you integrate VC into third party solutions um like inside game engines or stuff like that. With GPL you couldn't do that because that means you needed to open source everything, and those are for a lot of like commercial companies who don't want that.

Lex： So you can create a company with LGPL. You can create a company around it. You can do a commercial thing. You don't have to open source it. So that's a big big leap. So you can play video in your game.

嘉宾： Yes.

Lex： The the problem is I'm a game developer and I want to play some videos. And I don't want to be forced to open source the entire game just to play those videos. So that's where the the consulting business, the libVLC LGPL allows you to do that. The LGPL, the library GPL as it used to be known, allows you to do that.

嘉宾： And FFmpeg is exactly the same. It—LGPL forces you to give back what you change on this component, this library, which is why it's library GPL. And so you can use FFmpeg as LGPL into like any type of application even non-open source, but you need to give back the modification you did on ffmpeg. Same on libVLC.

GPL 是否限制了商业生态

Lex： Is it limiting from an open source perspective to go GPL? Because if you if your uh library, if your code is GPL, it means you're not—you you're basically discouraging companies from building a business around it, right? Is that—is that fair to say?

嘉宾： It depends on—it depends on the company. But the company whose business model requires the source the application to be closed source, yes, it's limited. So that's why for example I moved to LGPL.

理由二：苹果 App Store 的条款

The second reason, um is a bit more obscure, is that the the terms of condition of the uh App Store, the Apple App Store for iOS, makes it very complex to have GPL application on it, while it's easier to have LGPL applications on it. So VC on Windows and on Mac uh and on Linux is GPL. The core is LGPL, but on iOS uh the iPhone version and the Apple TV version is a type of different license called the MPL.

Um and yes, I I went and changed the license, and it was a long story.

重新授权：联系每一位贡献者

Lex： Yeah. So I I think basically to change the license, you have to contact all the contributors.

嘉宾： Yes. It's very important to understand that open-source projects are what we called in the US copyright uh law "joint work" or in um civil law um "collective works" or "collaborative works," is that you work all together in terms of the same goal and then it creates one software which is one release. Um but the copyright is kept by all the individuals. Some open source project don't do that. They force copyright assignment. But this is not what do we do with communities. So everyone has basically copyright on what they changed.

Um and this copyright stays even if at the end your your contribution was deleted, because the new contribution was based on your previous one. Right? So if you want to properly relicense, you need to find all the contributors. And at that time I had to contact more than 350 people. And sometimes well they are just an email, right? So it's you need to actually track down.

一个工厂工人父亲的故事

I actually like traveled to some place to go somewhere that I was like sorry that I had found online to see to go to their job and say, well, you licensed that, can you do—you want to change from GPL to LGPL? Most of the times they don't even care, they wanted to help VLC.

But also it brought me to very complex situation. I arrived to the work of a person who was a factory worker, um and I said, well I need to you to sign that, because it was his son who died who actually wrote the code, right? So I had to explain all those type of open-source meaning, and—no, I was not a company trying to rip out the two lines or five lines that that guy did but was useful, and the whole community agreed on that.

And he had no idea—I was a factory worker, this—and I was a lot younger, right? Like it was 14 years ago. And like like I was almost in tears, right? It's way very difficult, right? We talking about lives of people, and he explaining, and we went to talk about the photo of this guy, right? So, it's important to do it right and to do it correctly.

Um, but yes, that means tracking down everything because every contribution works. There are some projects who don't respect that and we do relicensing a bit like aggressively, but as I said it destroy the heart of the community, because we only agree on this on the license. So, that's important.

社区的多样性与"代码即标准"

Lex： I would emphasize the community is such a wide ranging group of people. There's people in the Syrian war zone with electricity part-time. There's there's all people from all walks of life, rich, poor, young, old. Um, so it's quite remarkable to get, you know, a group of people aligned on something and that that's an achievement in itself.

嘉宾： Yeah, it's incredible. And a lot of them are introverts. So you coming coming to find them and getting them and getting them to answer an email might be quite quite difficult.

Most of us are introverts, right? You you need to be more precise. You have extremely introverts. Extremely extremely introverts, and introverts, right? It's just like a whole spectrum of different people. It doesn't matter.

The important is, is your code good? Is your code great? Is your technology great? We care about excellent code. We don't care who you are. Sorry. It's just like we have no idea to check. We cannot check, right? Like maybe you're a dog. I don't care, right? I don't care where you come from. I need to look at your code.

And this is important because people don't understand that. And they come to the community and send them some patches and they get rejected, and they don't like that because I mean, you're just like, "Sorry, it's not up to our standards." "Oh yeah, but I'm engineer at this very large company in in Italy, in Germany, in the US." We don't care. We care about the quality of your code, because this is what defines our community. And which means that we have a lot of people who contribute who are some very different backgrounds and and and very introverts. Sure. But that's okay, right?

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的发现：ffmpeg 不是工具，是一种"被遗忘者的副语言"
大多数人把 ffmpeg 当作命令行工具，但你仔细听 JB 的描述会发现一个惊人的事实——**ffmpeg 实际上是人类目前规模最大的"由死者参与维护"的活体系统之一**。"我们有数千名贡献者，其中一些人已经不在人世了。"这句话被轻描淡写地带过，但它揭示了一个奇异的本体论问题：当你在 YouTube 上播放一段视频，你的播放管线里运行着至少几行由已故工程师写下、且因法律上的著作权约束而永远无法被替换的代码。换句话说，**全球视频文明的底层，是一座由活人无法单方面拆除的"代码陵墓"**。许可证（GPL/LGPL）在这里不是法律条文，而是一种近似宗教的"祖先崇拜协议"——它强制你尊重那些已经无法回应邮件的人。
## 跨领域连接：ffmpeg 的命令行 ↔ 巴洛克音乐的通奏低音（basso continuo）
你提到 ffmpeg "是一门语言"，几千字符的命令行像程序生成的。这让我想到一个出人意料的类比：**ffmpeg 的命令行美学，在结构上等同于巴洛克时代的"数字低音记谱法"**。作曲家只写一行低音线 +

item.title: [Lex Clips] Linus Torvalds built Git in 10 days | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽略的悖论：伟大软件的"反组织性"

表面上，这段对话在赞美个人英雄主义——Linus 用两周写出 Git，JB 创造 VLC，Brendan Eich 在十天内造出 JavaScript。但真正令人震撼的洞察藏在一个反常识的事实里：这些改变世界的软件，几乎全部诞生在组织最薄弱、流程最少、会议最稀的时刻。Git 之所以能在两周内成型，恰恰是因为 Linus 当时没有产品经理、没有 Roadmap、没有用户调研。如果当年他在一家大公司里提案"我要重写 BitKeeper"，Git 大概率会死在第一次需求评审上。这揭示了一个软件世界的隐藏定律：伟大的工具不是被规划出来的，而是被一个被激怒的天才在周末烧出来的。

跨领域连接：软件的"个人创世"与文学、数学的孤独时刻

Tim Sweeney 说的 "an awesome person decides to do it" 其实并不是软件独有的现象——它与数学史上的"独狼定理"惊人地同构。Grothendieck 重写代数几何、Perelman 证明庞加莱猜想、Wiles 锁在阁楼七年证费马大定理，这些工作都不是团队产出，而是单一意识在一个足够长的不被打扰的窗口里完成的密度爆炸。再看文学：卡夫卡在保险公司下班后写《变形记》，托尔金在牛津的批改作业本背面写《霍比特人》。软件、数学、文学共享同一个生产函数：高浓度的个人意图 × 不被稀释的时间 × 一个具体的"看不下去了"的瞬间。 Git 的诞生不是软件事件，而是这条人类创造规律在 2005 年的又一次显化。

一个让你意外的观察：Linus 真正的"产品"不是 Git，而是"拒绝"

大部分人把 Linus 的伟大归功于他"写了 Linux"和"写了 Git"。但如果你把他四十年的输出看作一个系统，会发现一个出人意料的模式：他真正反复在做的事情，是"拒绝某种被强加的复杂性"。 - 拒绝 Minix 的学术封闭 → Linux - 拒绝 BitKeeper 的商业绑架 → Git - 拒绝 C++ 的语义膨胀 → 著名的 C++ 抨击 - 拒绝 Nvidia 的闭源驱动 → 那个著名的中指

也就是说，Linus 的核心能力不是"创造"，而是"在被恶心到的临界点上把替代品做出来"。这给"个人改变世界"提供了一个被严重低估的反向公式：与其问"我有什么愿景"，不如问"我每天忍受的哪件事，烂到了我愿意花两周亲手干掉它"。VLC、Git、Linux、JavaScript 全是这条公式的产物——它们不是被"梦想"驱动的，而是被精确定位的厌恶驱动的。这或许是这段对话里最不被察觉、却最可操作的一条洞见。

📝 转录文本

关于热情项目

And on that topic of passion projects, uh, Tim Sweeney actually said in a reply to a tweet that was uh complimenting JB. He said:

"Many things in the world only happen because an awesome person decides to do it. This is the case with VLC."

个人创造的力量

And that speaks to something interesting to me. It does seem that a small number of people, sometimes one person, can create something incredible in the software world. Like he said this over and over and over.

I think JavaScript is an incredible thing, created by by uh initially a single person. The — some of the programming languages like Python and C and Java, like there's one person has this vision, has this design, and brings it sometimes over a weekend is the initial spark.

Linus 与 Git

Yes, Linus built Git in 2 weeks.

Yeah. Wow.

Yeah. Yeah, changed the world. Git, I mean, it really changed the world.

Linus' passion project — hey, I'm uploading this tarball to an FTP, like deal with it.

信念与愿景

But for me, it's not just in software, right? And I believe in in in individuals that are going to change the world, right? And it's with a good, as you said, vision, right? I want to do that. Um, it is useful, it will be useful.

And whether it's going to like build train or cars or rockets or something — like I I believe people who believe in themselves and have a vision can have a huge impact for humanity.

观看原视频 →

## 跨领域连接：软件的"个人创世"与文学、数学的孤独时刻

item.title: [Lex Clips] The controversial FFmpeg and Libav split saga explained | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

分叉的悖论：冲突是开源最高级的"版本控制"

大多数人把 FFmpeg/Libav 的分裂看作戏剧性的破裂，但更深层的真相是：分叉本身是开源社区的一种"协议"，而不是它的失败。Git 让"分叉"成为技术上零摩擦的操作，但 Jean-Baptiste 真正在说的是——分叉是把社会冲突翻译成代码空间的方式。当人们无法在一张桌子上达成共识时，他们在代码库里"平行宇宙化"自己，几年后让市场（用户、发行版、合并请求）来仲裁。GCC/EGCS、KHTML/WebKit/Blink、FFmpeg/Libav，甚至 Apple 用 Clang 偷偷替换 GCC——这些不是裂痕，而是开源版的"自然选择"。出人意料的观察是：没有分叉能力的组织（公司、政党、宗教）只能压抑冲突直到爆炸；开源因为可以分叉，反而比传统组织更稳定。冲突在这里不是病，而是免疫反应。

真正的杀手不是戏剧，而是"被关心的孤独"

访谈中最锋利的转折点是从 fork 滑向 maintenance burnout 和 XZ 事件。这里有一个跨领域的对照值得停下来想：XZ 攻击在结构上和"PUA"或邪教招募几乎一模一样——攻击者不是破解密码，而是利用维护者的孤独和疲惫，先制造压力，再"贴心地"接手。这是一种针对人类神经系统的供应链攻击，而不是针对代码的。再延伸一步：AI slop（伪造的安全报告）正在工业化生产这种心理疲劳，把单点的社工攻击变成了对维护者注意力的 DDoS。更出人意料的是——curl 的 Daniel、libxml 的停摆、时区数据库那个独居美国中部的人——他们暴露了一个秘密：人类数字文明的关键基础设施，其瓶颈不是 CPU、不是带宽，而是少数几个人的睡眠质量和情绪稳定性。我们一直以为云在天上，其实云压在十几个人的肩膀上。

"粉末信件"与"谢谢"的对称性：开源是一种情感经济

Jean-Baptiste 因为停止维护 PowerPC 版 VLC 收到了装着白色粉末的死亡威胁，这件事看起来荒诞，但它揭示了一个反直觉的结构：用户对开源软件的情感强度，远超他们对付费软件的情感强度。你不会给微软寄炭疽——你会要求退款。但你会给一个把童年记忆（解码星战游戏、播放奶奶的 DVD）外包给的免费软件作者寄恐吓信，因为免费让用户产生了所有权的幻觉。这是经济学里一个被低估的现象：零价格不等于零债务，反而创造了无限的情感债务——只是这债务的方向是错的，从作者欠用户而不是相反。

这里的"啊哈时刻"是：Jean-Baptiste 主动跑到 Reddit 上回复"谢谢 VLC"的留言并截图发到 IRC——他不是在炫耀，他是在为整个维护者群体校准情感账本。死亡威胁和"谢谢"在他的心理里不是相加的关系，而是相除——他需要足够大的分子（感谢）去稀释那个永远不会消失的分母（仇恨与冷漠）。所以当他说"please celebrate FFmpeg"，这不是客套，这是一种生存所需的能源补给协议。下一次你用 VLC 拖动进度条，记得：那一秒钟的丝滑，背后是有人在 2009 年拆开过一封掺着面粉的信。

📝 转录文本

关于 FFmpeg 与 Libav 的分裂

We're uh taking a non-linear journey through history here, but we're talking about Michael Niedermayer. Uh and I wanted to ask about this — for a time there was a split in FFmpeg and a libav.

Yes. So, um, in open-source projects, um, sometimes you disagree, right?

[laughter]

That's such an S-word putting it.

Yeah. And the good thing is, because of the license, you're allowed to basically do your own, right? Um, and this is normal and this has happened all the time, right? So, at the point there was a GCC — at the time of GCC 2 and EGCS, which became then GCC 3, right? There is what we told KHTML with WebKit with Blink. Um, it is a sane process.

And also, like, when I want to do a new feature today in VLC, I fork, I do my same on my own, and then I merge back to the community. So, there was a split in the open-source community on FFmpeg which become libav and FFmpeg. And after a few years, well, the community merge back and people moved on.

It's a bit, um, drama that is normal in open-source community, but forks are — even they're important — because they change the statu quo of a community. Um, not talking about FFmpeg and and libav here, but all the GCC fork made GCC a ton better, because some people wanted to change the architecture fundamentally to make it faster. And of course, it's always a question of people and so on, but in the end, you realize that FFmpeg today is better than it was uh before the fork. Um, and now, well, we're back all together, right?

And I spent a lot of time in in Kankakee in the in the community. Um, it's not often, to be honest, uh very well explained because a ton of the reasons are not very public, uh but I think that's that's normal. And that's good.

用户视角下的涟漪效应

Yeah, I mean, you're making it sound really nice, but there there is bad — there's pretty heated battles inside open-source projects. I mean, it is a very passionate community and you're kind of in a distributed way have to define the direction of things.

So, here looking at uh perplexity: FFmpeg and libav split in 2011 mainly over project governance, leadership style, and development processes, not because of a fundamental technical disagreement. Uh, FFmpeg effectively absorbed libav's work, uh, while libav withered and most distributions and developers went back to FFmpeg.

Yeah, that was a — that was a weird — that was a, from a user's perspective, that was a weird experience. Cuz, you know, I'm a Linux user, so, you know, whether it's Ubuntu and so on, all of a sudden, I think for a little bit Ubuntu — I feel like, am I remembering correctly — switched to libav. '12, '14, something like that. Yeah. And then they switched back to FFmpeg. I was like, what is happening? So, I don't know — sort of you get to feel the ripple effects of the different internal debates that are happening.

To be fair, on Apple, when you type GCC, you get Clang. Like, they did something like that as well, so.

Yeah, so to me, it's like the fork was like heated drama, but most of the development from libav was merged back into FFmpeg, right? So, the de facto FFmpeg got a superset around libav, and so that gave the user — because in the end, we have to use for the users a larger set of features — and a ton of things that were um discussed. For example, the debate on reviews and on how we push are something that now is completely settled in FFmpeg and is following mostly what everyone in the community agrees, right?

So, the de facto, everyone who was active on libav came back in work on FFmpeg because the disagreement were fixed. And in the end, FFmpeg is stronger than it was before, right? And I know people love drama, but um…

维护者的倦怠与心理代价

Well, my main concern — I understand, I think, looking at the long history, it's all for the good — but I am concerned because there's so few humans that are critical to the success of open-source projects that I have seen it be a psychological toll on folks. And, you know, sometimes leads to burnout.

So, you have these incredible people that are at the core of open-source projects. There's a moment that happens cuz, like, what is the motivation of doing it? Ultimately, it's because you're passionate about it and it makes you happy. And at a certain point, you wake up and it's like, this has been a bit too much heat from the drama. So, like, at the project level, the project continues and often flourishes, but sometimes there's these individual humans that are just like, I've had enough.

Yeah, but it's not just about forks, right? So, what you are referring to is one of the most challenging and most interesting part of open-source today: maintenance burnout, right? And AI is a problem because of that.

And Daniel Steinberg, which is the maintainer of curl, who's probably one of the best promoter of open-source in the world — he's, by the way, a member of the European Open Source Academy with me. So, I'm very, like, humble to be on the same communities as him, right? He's against what he called "AI slop," right? Because it gives a ton of fake reports or bad reports, bad patches. And then a lot of maintainers have a lot of burden to maintain the software. And this is uh straining the mind of open-source developers much more than forks.

XZ 事件与社工攻击

And for example, the XZ fiasco was because there was one guy maintaining it and he got basically hammered by two attackants who were asking him questions non-stop at weird times at night to block him, and at some point, he got fed up and said, "Okay, I can't do that." And gave the commit access to the attackant. Mhm.

Um, so, burnout in open-source community is something that exist, but mostly it's about maintaining things, right?

No, for sure. I wonder how do we help that, cuz those people are so important. You know, the human beings are so important at the core of these projects.

So, for example, now I am maintaining a ton of multimedia and non-multimedia library as maintainer uh because the maintainers got fed up, right? Um, someone VideoLAN, some outside of VideoLAN, uh because it's — sometimes you need a tough skin, right? Because you get like — it's not really attacks, but they: "Oh, this is not working. This is not working." And you feel it personally.

互联网底层那块"小积木"

Um, and this is also why resources — or the Google fiasco — was a problem, right? They don't realize that in the end, you have… you know, it's like the same graph where you see, like, everything, and it's just like one random open-source project that is maintaining the whole driver internet. You see the one, right?

Yeah, this is the meme. I mean, it applies to a lot of these projects, but this is "all modern digital multimedia infrastructure" — and then that thing at the very bottom that everything relies on is FFmpeg. It's true. And then there's usually, like, you know, a handful of folks that are maintaining that.

And FFmpeg or VLC, right? You have a community of 10, 15 core developers. Are not the worst open-source project. XZ, which is even in more installations, is one person, right? There is one guy. libxml is — yeah, libxml, right? There was a big stop. No one is maintaining libxml anymore, which is, like, the only library that is able to parse XML everywhere. All the crazy edge cases of XML under ridiculous circumstances — and they get attacked by security researchers because there's one other crazy edge case that they haven't thought of.

I was like, yeah, but the body of knowledge to actually resolve that is massive. There is one guy maintaining all the time zones for everyone who is in the middle of — I think — was it Nebraska or South Dakota?

Like, the mental health of the open-source maintainers is something that large corporations don't care or don't see, right? It's just like, "Oh, yeah, I'm just doing an open-source report and so on."

财务支持之外，还有"精神支持"

Some of it is financial — and people should definitely support open-source financially all across the board. But some of it is also, like, spiritual, on a basic human level. There's something that happens like with this image of FFmpeg and so much of the internet depending on it, where people almost talk down to the folks who are carrying these projects forward and maintaining them.

In the security they certainly did. That was one of the things, I think, that argument came out is: there was a portion of the security community that said, "No, these guys write crap code. They need to fix their crap code." I was like, "No, no, no, no. This is a guy's hobby project. You have a security bot that's gone and found some AI-generated stuff. That guy didn't write crap code. It's just an edge case to the 99.99999% he didn't think about because it's his hobby project decoding Star Wars games."

Forget the hobby project aspect of it. It's just hard work and it's beautiful. And I think the right approach there is to celebrate people for doing incredible, incredible work. It's just incredible that humans step up — sure, not getting really paid at first or maybe ever — and then they're doing it out of the love of it. And we need to like — human civilization runs on people like that. We need to celebrate them.

死亡威胁与 PowerPC

To give you an idea, I received death threats on VideoLAN, right? And, um…

You mentioned that to me. Like, what is behind that?

So, that must be what, 2009, 2010, right? Um, Apple is moving from PowerPC to Core Duo. Um, that's probably in 2006. And by 2009 or 2010, I decide that we are not going to do new versions of VLC for PowerPC. Mhm. Like, at that time, like, VLC — we were close to the number 1.0, we were four of us, right? Like, just like, no, this is not possible.

So, I received a death threat with some powder in it, right? Remember there was some anthrax threats at that time, right? Um, and it was because I had taken the decision to not maintain the PowerPC port anymore. And of course, it wasn't anthrax, of course. It was some type of flour and so on. But I received that as a — with a letter of, like, "uh, you're piece of — you should die. PowerPC forever," and so on. And was 2009 or 2010, right? I was — I was young. I was just like, why? What did I do, right?

Can break your spirit. It's like, why?

My mother freaked out, right? We had to go to see the police and so on. And now, like, I'm going to say that I'm quite happy that this happened at that time. It forged me a lot, right? I am — I can see I can take a lot of hate on me. I'm okay with it, right? It sucks that that's part of reality.

"谢谢" 的力量

Cuz all the people that love VLC, all the people that love FFmpeg, like me — you know, I legitimately hundred, probably thousands of times in my life had a smile on my face because FFmpeg made me happy. Period. And how many times did I get a chance to say that? Zero. Until I realize there's a Twitter account [laughter] logo. And every once in a while, I'm like, messaging…

One of the things I like on the Reddit meme about me — which I don't like this meme for a lot of reasons — but, and someone says, "Oh, JB is on Reddit." Which I am, right? And I say, "Hello, right?" And then I got so many people who say, "Oh, thank you for VLC." And, like, I take pictures and then I share that to the Signal, to IRC. Yes, we use IRC on different…

I saw you — as a quick tangent — you mentioned IRC is like Slack for old people. So, you still use IRC?

Of course. Yeah. I have it on my phone as well. Of course. Every day. Works fine.

Wow. It works fine. Works fine. You have to power with the crank.

No, but there's no — there's no — there's no tracking. There's nothing. The biggest issue, to be honest, right, compared to Slack, is that it doesn't have threads. That's annoying. It doesn't have emojis for action. Sometimes it would be nice. Yes, V3, but no one does it. And you cannot edit your messages, right? And the rest, it works perfectly fine for me.

You communicate without emojis?

Well, that's why I said it's for old people. Old people.

[laughter]

And we do emojis with, like, you know, the columns and that…

[laughter]

庆祝那些建造者

So, anyway, you communicate on IRC. What were we even talking about?

Yeah, we are talking about death threats. And — but having people thanking you, and sometimes I got people who sent me a message: "Oh, thank you for VLC." And I always answer because I want to validate the fact that you need to thank the open source community.

Yeah, please, everybody listening to this, celebrate FFmpeg. Celebrate VLC. Celebrate all the incredible open source projects — Linux, everything. There's so many. And you know what? I mean, even outside of open source, just celebrate companies that create software that you use a lot and love. Celebrate human endeavor. Celebrate the human effort to not just build something that's okay. Yeah. Build something that is damn good.

Yeah, this is important, right? Like, because as we said, right? We work for technic — we do something very complex for the normal people. Like, we want our excellence in tech to be useful for everyone. And this is why we work, right? This is why I wake up in the morning. It's because I want people to use our stuff because it's making everyone's life easier.

工程师的初心

Want to solve hard problems. Work on something interesting. Work on something interesting — technical challenges. We are engineers. We love to build things, right? When I was young, very early, I knew I wanted to be engineer. I wanted to do cars, right? Maybe at some point I will go back to cars, right? But this is, like, we want to build things that are cool and useful. And they need to be challenging, right? Because you want your brain to turn on.

item.description:
## 分叉的悖论：冲突是开源最高级的"版本控制"
大多数人把 FFmpeg/Libav 的分裂看作戏剧性的破裂，但更深层的真相是：**分叉本身是开源社区的一种"协议"，而不是它的失败**。Git 让"分叉"成为技术上零摩擦的操作，但 Jean-Baptiste 真正在说的是——分叉是把社会冲突翻译成代码空间的方式。当人们无法在一张桌子上达成共识时，他们在代码库里"平行宇宙化"自己，几年后让市场（用户、发行版、合并请求）来仲裁。GCC/EGCS、KHTML/WebKit/Blink、FFmpeg/Libav，甚至 Apple 用 Clang 偷偷替换 GCC——这些不是裂痕，而是**开源版的"自然选择"**。出人意料的观察是：**没有分叉能力的组织（公司、政党、宗教）只能压抑冲突直到爆炸；开源因为可以分叉，反而比传统组织更稳定**。冲突在这里不是病，而是免疫反应。
