不被组织结构图绑架

"Yeah. I think one of the things that I had in my time and I tried is that I wasn't a victim of the organization chart. You know, go you know, these firms could be very — Goldman Sachs is not very bureaucratic and not very — I remember when I was very very early in my career. Remember I came from left field to Jay Aaron. Jaron was acquired by Goldman. Aaron wasn't doing very well. But I had this idea — I was in the precious metals business and it made me have to deal with people from the Mid East who are investors in in in gold — and that I'm chatting with people on the other side and what are you doing? What do you need?"

"And you know, it turns out that even though they were speculating in precious metals, what they really really wanted to do was they wanted to be able to invest money and get an interest rate — like predictable return. But under their rules of engagement, you know, their law — um they weren't they were they weren't allowed in those days, the the real strictly religious crowd wasn't allowed to take interest. It was usurious. And what they were looking for ways of making in making kinds of investments that would read like an investment. So they were allowed to make investment returns. They just weren't allowed to collect interest. Sure. But had the stability and predictability of an interest."

"And what they were doing — and you know we can go into details or not. I don't want to be complicated here. Cash and carries — where people were doing arbitragees between a spot market and a commodity and the forward market — that effectively if you buy — if you are — if you are selling somebody you know buying the cash product and selling somebody a forward — in effect you're lending that person mo— money because you're giving him the the risk of the investment but he doesn't have to put out that much cash. You're the one who's hedging it by buying the commodity and giving him a forward in it. Yep. And that has an embedded interest rate to it, but it looks a lot like an investment return."

"And so in chatting with them, but the markets weren't big enough to do the scale they wanted to do. And that was a few years earlier was when they came out with the S&P 500 financial commodities in effect, and those were big. Interesting. And so in talking to them, they said, 'Well, holy — I'm at Goldman Sachs, biggest equity trader, blah blah blah. What if we did this in the equity market — Manhattan, we went out and they bought 500 of the S&P 500 — and, you know, put out the money in the market and hedged it by selling it in the forward market. Would that give them what was the embedded rate of return?' And it was very high because they were the other side of speculators who didn't have the capital."

"I know this is a little bit complicated, but the short story was I had the idea. I went to the then — like number two guy in the firm, Bob Rubin, led treasury secretary — who I never spoke to. He was a Gman of the whole firm and I was in the — tucked away in the jet — which was in a separate building at the time. He never moved. And he said that could be interesting. He called up that one — called up somebody on the equity desk — said work with Lloyd. My t— I didn't even have a title at that point. So I — I you know I — I remember I asked when they merged into GM — I said what's my title — and the guy said call yourself Contessa if you want."

"So no title — and he said so somebody work with me and they did — and the first order that came in — and this was like in back when this was real money — was for $400 million worth of this — that was by far the biggest trade ever and then they was doing. And anyway so that's how. And you want to be that way in your organization — you know — and by the way that's an easier thing in your line of work. Yep. Where the entrepreneurs are advantaged by their lack of attachment to history and tradition and the old way of doing things. Where the iconoclast is the uh in in your business, the iconoclast and the young guy are celebrating — is not only celebrated, they're the uh you know they're the uh they're the focus. Sure. And that's and not so much in in bigger organizations. And so totally we always wanted to achieve — you know that's another thing — to try to be an entrepreneur in a in an institution."

"Totally. "As — what I agree with that argument."

"Yeah. Yeah. I figured um what what do you think helped the firm navigate that period so well? You know, was it risk management, technology? Um the fact you didn't have a big consumer business…"

"Risk management — the big — the lack of a big consumer business hurt us in the back end on the reputational side because people didn't know us, right? We were a big influential — government sack. So I have, you know, people get — left Goldman became very big officials, prime ministers, and by the way, not just in the US, overseas as well. Totally. And so, but in the beginning, you know, risk-management culture — and maybe that stem from the fact that we were a partnership lately. We have unlimited liability. There's nothing that focuses your attention better than being a — you know, you're a partnership. You're not — you're investing client money and you're not leveraging your own money."

"We, you know, you know, at — at you know, the partners not only had their capital accounts at risk, they had their homes at risk. I remember when I became a partner um I said — should I be putting you know my — you know should I be putting my house in my wife's name? And it was very funny because then the minister of the interior — this is back when we were partnership — said you know — Lloyd no partner at Goldman Sachs has ever lost money because the firm — you know because of losses at the firm — but plenty of Goldman Sachs partners have lost money because they put assets in their in their spouse's name. So that was it. So it was a funny one — but by the way like a lot of funny lines. Truth. Truth."

"And so, but it did focus your attention and it made us very very totally to totally honored — and risk managers — and very attentive to risk. And now one of the one of the consequences of that — concern, we marked things to market rigorously religiously and other people didn't. Yep. They didn't have you."

"Do you think if the crisis had stemmed in like the private equity ecosystem, which I imagine the firm had a lot more kind of notional exposure to, it would have navigated as well or…"

"Yeah. No, it would have been tougher because it's hard to mark to market. Now, what we did, we also had instruments that were one-off. You know, we had a lot of loan commitments related to our M&A. We were the biggest M&A franchise. And so, we made commitments. Those were outstanding. Those were commitments that were, you know, had to be — and but we marked them down. We made an analogies. And we also had a very separate — I'm going to say this word. I hate to say bureaucracy — and the firm — away from the investors and the traders whose job — you know they were partners. They got paid a lot of money. Sure. To mark those things."

Mark-to-Market 作为早期预警

"And when there was a dispute, we always — we always sided with that side of the house. Yep. And we said to the traders, investors — very easy way for you to challenge the marks that you're being given. Go out and sell something fraction. Totally. And guess what? And that's what got it. We had — that was mark-to-market — is not just a P&L system. It's a risk-management system because we — that was our early warning that something was a mish. We had things that were marked — things that were AAA. When you went — when we made people sell them — the bids vanished — and they weren't there. And the bids were much lower — and then much lower — and then much lower."

"By the way, I didn't think there was the market was right. I thought there was a big opportunity to accumulate it. But you — but that would be like fighting with the tides or gravity. It is — that's the market. So guess what? We're going to keep marking it down till you find — till we mark it to a price where you could sell it. And by the way, and therefore it became easier to sell. Sure. Because it wasn't like they had big losses. The losses were already embedded in their books. Totally. Because we marked it to market."

"And if you make yourself so narrow and exalt, you know, your narrow silo, even if you make a lot of money in the first gate, your life will be better and your commercial life will be better if you are more — and your resilience will be higher. Totally. Learning history."

历史让你看清"我们经历过更糟"

"You know, it's a good thing to know that we've lived through times like this before. You know, everybody talks about, 'Oh my god, we've never been this bad, never more polarized in politics.' You know, you say, 'Well, we did have a civil war, I guess.' Well, that was a long time ago. Well, guess what? I was a sentient human being in the late 60s, young, but still, you know, still aware when the National Guard was shooting people on campuses. It was political — not successful political assassinations — and you know the college-age kids were leaving the country and going to Canada to avoid the draft."

"I would say those were pretty po— and by the way internationally Russian tanks went into in '68 were — went into Czechoslovakia. So I — I would say that was a bit more danger. The country during the Cuban Missile Crisis was at DEFCON 2. You know — by the way the lower numbers are the more severe. DEFCON 1 is nuclear war and we were at — we were at DEFCON 2. So it's very bad that we're fighting a regional war in Iran. We were at DEFCON 2 with the then Soviet Union stopping their ships in international waters on their way because of a blockade of Cuba. I would say that was a more polarized time, a more danger."

"If our parents could get through that — yep — we should get through this. And I think knowing that to me — and I think it should be to everybody else — is knowing — knowing that something has been done should give people comfort that it could be done again. And so every time is different, but this is not more extreme."

在专业领域的交界处找机会

"No, I I totally agree. I think range is going to be even more important now than ever. And you know, one of my I've written about this, but it's sort of a I know life and maybe business philosophy, too, is that opportunities live between fields of expertise."

"Yeah. You know, um — I like living at — over the edge of cliffs. Totally. And over your horizon of what you could see about the future. Totally."

"And so learning, you know, look, when I was growing up, everybody wanted to learn, you know, my my predecessor, Hank Paulson spent so much of his time, as did I in going to China. Well, at least temporarily. We're not going to be making as many investments in China as we once did. There's not none, but it's not going to be as much. Or when I was growing up, everybody wanted to learn Japanese because those were the winners in the, you know, in the tech — the tech stuff. And I remember a time when Silicon Valley was Route 128 in Boston. Yep. And not — there was no Silicon Valley. It was the — it was the — it was the it was around Harvard and MIT, not around Stanford. Yep."

"So I would say things change and in order to be resilient, a better person, and also I hate to minimize this — for your own sake. Yep. Learn humanities, learn history, learn those things. And that's what you're, you know, we're at a point now where most people who are young are going to live to be a, you know, the age, they're going to actually live longer and they seem to be in much more of a rush to be a, you know, to be, you know, a success in in your kinds of enterprises. And, you know, I don't, you know, you'll — some people will encourage it. I — I don't think that your only productive years are when you're 18 through 24."

"I totally agree. And everyone will ear— learn what you need for your career afterwards. And I think you'll be, you know, my humble opinion, but G— say interesting. You know, I'm an older guy."

"No, I mean, it's back to kind of where we started the conversation. I, you know, I don't personally believe people should drop out of school. I learned so much from my peers. It changed my life. It changed my perspective of what's possible. And, you know, I think it makes you a more well-rounded person, too."

"Look, here you are interviewing people from all different walks of life and and not just uh, you know, not just, you know, not just tallying uh, you know, ones and zeros."

"This was awesome. Lloyd, thank you so much very much for joining. Really appreciate it."

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：Blankfein 的"风险管理"本质是一套**反预测**的认知操作系统
大多数人以为顶级风控者擅长预测未来，但 Blankfein 反复强调的恰恰相反——**"我们做的不是预测，而是情景规划"**。他在桌上问的从来不是"X 会不会发生"，而是"如果 X 发生了你今天能花多少代价提前对冲"。
这里藏着一个让人意外的认知翻转：**优秀的风控者不是预言家，而是"反应时间套利者"**。他用田径比赛起跑做类比——抢跑会被判罚（反应时间小于 0.1 秒），但如果你在脑中已经预演过所有情境，你**真实的反应快到看起来像预知**。这其实和**国际象棋大师的"模式识别"研究**完全同构——他们并不算得更深，而是预存了海量"局面-应对"配对，让 System 2 思考转化为 System 1 直觉。Blankfein 把这套认知机制工业化、组织化了。
## 二、隐藏联系：高盛文化的"反脆弱"基因，与他个人"低期望成长史"是同一种结构
注意他自述的童年——纽约公屋、双区车票、从未坐过飞机、SAT 语文分数极低、**"没有高期望的负担"**。这看似是个谦逊的回忆，但和高盛文

item.title: [Lex Clips] What will be remembered about human civilization 1,000 years from now? | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的洞察："压缩"是一种文明的失忆症

大多数人把压缩视为技术问题——节省带宽、降低存储成本。但这段对话揭示了一个被忽视的隐喻：压缩本质上是一种"认识论暴力"。当算法判定某个像素"不重要"时，它实际上在替未来人类做决定——决定什么是"值得记住的"。

最惊人的悖论在于：archivist 们害怕的不是数据丢失，而是"看起来完好"的损耗。一个损坏到无法播放的文件会被立刻识别为问题；但一段被有损压缩"修过"的胶片，会以"完整"的假象骗过未来一千年的研究者。换句话说，最危险的不是黑暗，而是被美化过的光。这与历史学界正在讨论的"算法考古学"惊人地呼应：未来历史学家可能要先解码我们的偏见，才能解读我们的影像。

二、跨领域连接：C 语言 = 拉丁语 = 圣本笃修道院

Romain 把 C 语言比作拉丁语，这个类比比他自己意识到的还要深刻。

请考虑欧洲中世纪的修道院抄写传统：在罗马帝国崩塌后，正是修士们在烛光下逐字抄写古典文本，才让亚里士多德、西塞罗穿越了"黑暗时代"。今天 FFmpeg 的社区正在扮演同样的角色——只不过抄写的不是羊皮纸，而是 codec；不是拉丁文动词变位，而是 1950 年代某种磁带的色度学规范。Dave Rice 和那些 archivist，本质上是数字时代的本笃会修士。

更妙的是结构相似性：本笃修道院通过"贫穷誓言"（限制资金）反而获得了纯粹性；FFmpeg archiving 社区也是因为缺钱才不得不开源，因为开源反而获得了文明级的影响力。约束催生了不可替代性——这是一个被现代效率主义彻底误解的真理。

三、被忽略的系统性真相：21 世纪的真正稀缺是"未被污染的人类信号"

视频里那句"在一千年后我们可能淹没在 AI slop 中"是闲笔，但它指向一个深刻的相变：

  • 20 世纪以前：信息稀缺，每一份留存都珍贵
  • 21 世纪初：信息过载，但仍以人类生产为主
  • 21 世纪中后：AI 生成内容占主导，真实的人类影像变成新的稀有金属

这意味着 archivist 们今天保存的那些 70/80 年代的家庭录像带、地方电视新闻，其价值曲线不是线性的，而是会在 AI 泛滥后突然垂直上升——就像哥白尼之前没人在意托勒密手稿，AI 之后我们会突然意识到"未被污染的人类目光"是文明的稀土。

而最辛辣的反讽是：决定一千年后人类如何记住自己的，不是 OpenAI、不是 Google、不是任何巨头——而是一群你从没听过名字的开源贡献者，在没有预算的情况下决定哪盘磁带值得用最后一个能读它的磁头去抢救。文明的命脉，正掌握在 commit history 里。

四、一个让人脊背发凉的延伸推论

Romain 提到"全世界没有足够的磁头去读完所有磁带，所以必须决定丢弃哪些"。

这在认识论上是逆向的"图书馆焚毁"——亚历山大图书馆是一次性灾难，而我们正在经历的是慢动作的、由资源约束自动执行的、无人需要为之负责的渐进式遗忘。没有暴君下令，没有大火，只是磁头老化的速度比志愿者多的速度快。

这或许是人类历史上第一次：遗忘不是被迫的，而是被默认的；不是因为我们不知道珍贵，而是因为我们知道却来不及。

📝 转录文本

开场：FFmpeg 作为"千年罗塞塔石碑"

Lex: Karen, you you said that there's an active archiving preservation community. I think that's super fascinating. You wrote that they're stretching budget, but they see the extreme importance of FFmpeg as a Rosetta Stone so that multimedia can be played a thousand years from now. I mean, that's a beautiful way to see FFmpeg / VLC as a tool for preserving visual knowledge.

Guest: Yes, it's one of the coolest communities in open source multimedia, mainly led by someone called Dave Rice — I'll give him a shout out, I think from City University of New York — is the archiving community. They've done so much stuff that they value, that they value open source. One, because yes, they lack budgets, but two, they see the fact that archiving video is important for the world. And being able to play that is a big problem.

历史教训：英国的"新末日审判书"

Famously in the UK there was something called the the new Doomsday Book, and they archived lots of stuff on BBC microcomputers. Within 10 to 15 years, no one had the right software to play that — I think it was 20 years or something like that. And someone had to go and reverse engineer this, and that was like 20 years. Imagine that in a thousand years.

为什么是 C 语言？—— "C 将像拉丁语一样"

I think one of the great things about FFmpeg is it's written in C. C is the closest to mathematics you're probably going to get, the closest to logic.

Lex: You're just thinking 1,000 years we'd still have C compilers?

Guest: Yes, we have languages that exist that haven't changed too much. We have mathematics, mathematical notation that exists. It will be like Latin. C will be like Latin — it will be a thing that you learn from the past, but it will still be usable in certain contexts.

FFV1：为档案而生的无损编解码器

So the archiving community are really great practically. They — again, limited funds — they funded the development of the FFV1 codec. So that's a lossless codec, so that the archiving community… is really scared about the act of compression losing things. And this is — they have a fair point in this, you know.

Yeah. If they compress too hard, it could change the view of the material. There could be something slightly different here and there. So they're really concerned about things needing to be not just compressed well, but lossless and be fast. And so they worked with FFmpeg to develop a whole new codec designed for fast software-based encoding.

They're really concerned about resilience. So if they're storing on tapes or other hard disks, "I lose some bits, I need to recover quickly. I can't lose a whole GOP because I've lost the bit," something like that. So they're really great bunch of people. They funded GPU encoding in FFmpeg to make FFV1 encode faster.

一种文明级别的使命

And it's really about preserving the world's multimedia heritage in a way that's usable. And there's a lot of great teams and a lot of archival groups across the world who've chosen FFmpeg and FFV1 as their archiving solution. And they can really provide us also super specialist advice. They can explain: "Ah, in the 1950s colorimetry was done like this on this certain type of tape, and so there is a special case that you need to handle, and you'll never get this anywhere else." They know things on video that we don't.

Lex: Yes.

Guest: Like every time I talk with a—

Lex: Dave Rice—

Guest: Dave Rice, or the people from the British… British film… it's just like every time, I just learn something new. And I've been doing video for 20 years. They have, especially on colorimetry and colors storage and these other things—

对内容本身的深切敬畏

Lex: They have a deep, deep appreciation of the content itself, of the video itself. And especially when you're thinking lossless, they're terrified of losing something essential about the thing. And in so doing, they're deeply understanding the thing that is to be preserved — which you sometimes might not be thinking about when you're obsessing about the actual technology of the encoding and so on.

And when you enter the rabbit hole of film scanners, right? So you take those—

Guest: Uh-huh.

Lex: —those things to make, to digitalize…

Guest: Yeah. A huge topic that would take another five hours of podcast just on that topic.

胶片的脆弱与开源的普惠

And there's a lot of film that needs to be archived. Film is degrading. It's maybe not stored in the right environment. The other thing is — because it's open source, they give away their workflows to countries who can't afford to have archiving institutions, where archiving is done by volunteers, it's done by other things. They go and teach, you know, in India they teach children to do FFmpeg commands. They're really great.

They're really the model community, the model ethos of what we're trying to achieve. They are such a great bunch of people, so interested in participating and being part of something much bigger, because they realize the work they're doing in a thousand years is going to tell a lot.

在 AI 洪流前抢救 20 世纪

You know, in a thousand years we may be drowning in AI slop. This stuff needs to be important and, you know, archived well. What was life like?

Lex: Yeah, it feels like capturing the 20th century in the 21st century is essential, because it feels like a transition point — where we went from scarcity of data to…

Guest: Yeah.

Lex: …oceans of slop. And that transition point is good to archive.

Guest: People don't realize: we are losing today a ton of films. There is a ton of things from the 30s, from the 40s and the 50s, that there is no value [assigned to them].

Lex: And tape — 70s and 80s, there's tape, and there's not enough tape heads in the world to read all the tapes.

Guest: So they have to decide what they want to archive and throw away the rest of the tapes.

数字管家职责：道德困境

Lex: There's huge moral hazard, I guess for want of a better phrase, around this topic — because this is a digital record of human history, and they have to make decisions.

[sighs and gasps]

But — and there's digital stewardship, I suppose for want of… I've made that phrase up, that's not a real phrase—

[snorts]

—to make sure the world can have this information in something that's playable by everybody, not playable on some device that where it doesn't exist anymore.

干草堆里的针：未来才会显现的价值

And then there's like, realistically speaking, there's a needle in a haystack where there's a lot of value in archiving all that footage and then over time finding the gems that we don't know are there. Hey, there was something in that corner that we just didn't know.

Guest: Yeah. And now that would have been compressed away because some little thing — oh wow, there's something there — and they've made sure that it's lossless. I can prove mathematically that it's lossless. They can run different trade-offs for: if there's a bit flip — if they lose a single bit flip — I can make sure that I only lose a portion of a given frame. They can do error recovery on previous frames. They can do all sorts of different [things]…

观看原视频 →

大多数人把压缩视为技术问题——节省带宽、降低存储成本。但这段对话揭示了一个被忽视的隐喻：**压缩本质上是一种"认识论暴力"**。当算法判定某个像素"不重要"时，它实际上在替未来人类做决定——决定什么是"值得记住的"。
最惊人的悖论在于：archivist 们害怕的不是数据丢失，而是**"看起来完好"的损耗**。一个损坏到无法播放的文件会被立刻识别为问题；但一段被有损压缩"修过"的胶片，会以"完整"的假象骗过未来一千年的研究者。换句话说，**最危险的不是黑暗，而是被美化过的光**。这与历史学界正在讨论的"算法考古学"惊人地呼应：未来历史学家可能要先解码我们的偏见，才能解读我们的影像。
## 二、跨领域连接：**C 语言 = 拉丁语 = 圣本笃修道院**
Romain 把 C 语言比作拉丁语，这个类比比他自己意识到的还要深刻。
请考虑欧洲中世纪的**修道院抄写传统**：在罗马帝国崩塌后，正是修士们在烛光下逐字抄写古典文本，才让亚里士多德、西塞罗穿越了"黑暗时代"。**今天 FFmpeg 的社区正在扮演同样的角色**——

item.title: [Lex Clips] Intelligence agencies vs VLC: "No. We'll never do that" - Lead developer responds | Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：VLC 的"无知"才是它的超级武器

大多数人以为 VLC 抵抗政府压力靠的是法律团队、加密技术或道德勇气。但真正让它"刀枪不入"的，其实是一种结构性的无知（structural ignorance）——它根本不知道用户在看什么、何时看、看了多久。

这是一种近乎禅宗式的设计哲学：最强的隐私保护，不是"我们不会告诉你"，而是"我们自己也不知道"。 这与端到端加密的逻辑同源，却走得更远——E2EE 至少还知道"谁在和谁通信"，而 VLC 连这个元数据都不存在。

跨领域连接：这让我想到瑞士银行业的"不知情防御"（willful blindness defense 的反面）、Tor 网络的洋葱路由、以及禅宗的"不立文字"。当一个系统设计上就无法知道某件事，任何拷问、传票、后门请求都会撞上一堵认识论的墙——你不能从一个空集合里提取信息。

二、隐藏的系统性真相：开源是一种"分布式人质"博弈

Jean-Baptiste 提到——"代码里有德国人、英国人、美国人，他们都能看到"——这句话表面是技术陈述，本质上却是一个精妙的博弈论结构：

VideoLan 把"作恶的能力"分散到了多个司法管辖区，使得任何单一政府的施压都必须协调多国情报机构同时妥协多名贡献者。这在博弈论上接近互相确保毁灭（MAD）的逆用——不是"我们都会死"，而是"我们谁都无法单独叛变"。

更深一层的洞见：这与中本聪设计比特币时把节点散布全球的逻辑同构。开源 + 国际化 = 一种用地理拓扑学构建的不可腐蚀性。VLC 实际上是一个比许多区块链更去中心化的系统，因为它的"信任锚"是分散的人，而不是分散的算力。

三、最让人意外的连接：阿富汗驻军、谋杀案警察、与法国黑客的共同语言

视频里有个被一带而过、但极其震撼的瞬间：阿富汗战场上的美军士兵和法国独立开发者通过 RTSP 协议的 bug 报告建立联系。Jean-Baptiste 为美军单独编译了一个版本——不是因为意识形态认同，而是因为"播放文件"这个需求本身是中性的、人性的。

这揭示了一个被忽略的真相：真正的中立技术比任何"中立"宣言都更强大。VLC 同时服务于审讯破案的警察、苦闷的士兵、独立电影爱好者、盗版用户、情报对手国家的公民——而它给所有人提供的"无差别播放"，反而构成了一种比联合国宪章更彻底的普世主义。

跨领域连接：这呼应了哲学家 Langdon Winner 的命题"技术物有政治性"——但 VLC 颠覆了这个命题。它的政治性恰恰在于拒绝政治性。这与红十字会的"无差别人道主义"、或维基百科"中立观点"原则在精神上同构，但走得更远：维基百科还要做内容判断，VLC 连"判断"这个环节都从架构上删除了。

四、隐藏的禅意：Kelle 的"棋手心态"是一种工程师的存在主义

最后那段"我是不是死了？没有。那就没事"——这看起来是个人哲学，但其实是一种把存在主义压缩成布尔逻辑的工程师式智慧。

它与Stoic（斯多葛）的 premeditatio malorum（预演最坏）、与 Jeff Bezos 的 "regret minimization framework"、与佛教的"念死"修行同构，但有个独特之处：他把"公司死亡"和"个人死亡"做了清晰的范畴区分。这是一种罕见的能力——大多数创业者会把公司的存亡情感化为自我的存亡，而他能在结构上把它们解耦。

这或许才是 VLC 三十年来不商业化、不妥协、不被收购的真正心理基础：当你不害怕你的项目死亡时，没有人能用它的死亡威胁你。

📝 转录文本

关于压力与"被牵连"的恐惧

Lex： I mean, is there some paralyzing aspects? You know, if I look at the case of somebody I've become close with, Pavel Durov — you know, he was blamed directly by the French government for the kind of things his platform was hosting. I could see the same kind of stuff, basically just as an example, VLC being blamed for the kind of videos that people are watching.

Jean-Baptiste： Tried, right? Like we had — we had issues like, I mean…

Lex： That's the pressure that people worry about, because if you have to think about that kind of stuff when you're kind of just obsessed about the…

Jean-Baptiste： Think about it, and that's okay, right?

Lex： But what if they come in — what if they show up and…

Jean-Baptiste： There is no office. VideoLan doesn't have office.

Lex： I mean, this is what happened with Pavel — they arrested him, right? So they arrested him for particular videos, or particular content that's being shared on the platform.

Jean-Baptiste： Sure, I don't have any platform. Everything is on the client side.

Lex： Yeah, but they still arrest you.

Jean-Baptiste： On what ground? I'm not sharing anything. The content doesn't go through my stuff.

Lex： For sure, but there's still lawyer fees. That's the problem.

Jean-Baptiste： Yes, that's correct.

Lex： Paperwork. So actually, if you had infinite trillions of dollars, you would win easily because you're on the right side. But the thing is, there is a degree to which they suffocate you with paperwork. That's the downside of bureaucracy — through paperwork, through process. You know, it's the Kafka-esque thing.

欧洲法律体系的"缓冲带"

Jean-Baptiste： You have to realize that one of the good things, for example, in France or most of Europe, is that answering to a court order does not make you bankrupt, right? It's not like in the US, where it can actually bankrupt you. The way the law system works — I receive lawyers' letters every week, right? And I can tell you that the cost of lawyer fees for VideoLan is less than $10,000 per year. So that's not really scary.

情报机构的后门请求

Lex： I mean, so with Pavel — did intelligence agencies try to say, "Can you put a back door in VLC?"

Jean-Baptiste： Yes, two of them.

Lex： What did you say?

Jean-Baptiste： No. Well, I [laughter] was a lot less polite.

Lex： I see — yeah, yeah. You're basically saying hell no.

Jean-Baptiste： Like, if we had to compromise our software, we would shut it down. This is clear.

Lex： And what's the definition of compromise — like allowing a government to do a back door?

Jean-Baptiste： There is no code that gets into VLC that we don't control. And the way we compile VLC — you would call me completely paranoid. Like, we compile on boxes that are offline, where we start by compiling the compiler. We do everything offline, on places that have never been connected to the internet. The way we do signing — there is double signature. And especially because, for example, we've seen (and we believe it's a governmental agency that is not from the Western world) try to push a fake binary into our own servers. And that scared us a lot.

开源的不可摧毁性

And VideoLan is open source. How can you kill it? Like, I move to where, right? I move to Malta, I move to — I don't know — Cayman Islands, and I change the domain name and I start again, right? Like, VLC is a tool. It's a tool that is going to help people doing things. We are not a platform. And for patents — well, I'm sorry, but most of the patents… like, you shouldn't be able to patent math and matrices. This is wrong.

内容审查与监控请求

Lex： Does VLC ever censor the kind of videos it can play, based on the content of the video?

Jean-Baptiste： No, never. We never do that. Because VLC is completely offline. It doesn't talk to any server. So we don't know anything that you're using the software for.

Lex： So again, there's no government that can say — you know, like the French government come in and say, "We don't want… I think anime is destructive to society. We don't want any anime — not allowed to be…"

Jean-Baptiste： No, they cannot do that. And also what they tried is to say, "Hey, I want to know if that person watched that type of video." And the answer is like, "No idea." So no on that. So for surveillance — no. No, no, because the only infrastructure we have is a downloading infrastructure. There is no telemetry in VLC.

Lex： It would be difficult because of the international nature. It would be difficult for you to incorporate that code, because there would be someone in the UK and someone in Germany and someone in the US as part of VideoLan who'd be able to see that. It would be extremely difficult.

与执法和军方的"纯技术支持"接触

Jean-Baptiste： One thing that we can do, which happened — like, we had the case with some police in the US who said, "We have a murder case, right? And the file is destructed, or doesn't play in that version of VLC. Could you help us?" We never have access to the video. It's like a normal support, right? It's really about playing the file.

Yes. And I remember in the middle of the Afghan war, right? I received an email from someone in the army. I don't remember the grades — it was just like, "We have a big issue with the latest version of VLC, because it doesn't play correctly the file on an RTSP server that we have, where there is all the movies." And he says, "VLC is very important for the morale on the troop on the ground, right? Because at night it might be boring, right? So they have a collection of videos to watch, or movies over there."

And of course, I did an update and I broke some support of RTSP. So I gave them another version just for them. Because it was important. And because VLC is completely open source, I think it is allowed on the US Army laptops, right? Because I guess someone in the US military actually looked at it and said, "Well, okay, this is okay." And the way we document how we process — that was okay.

So the only way we work with authorities is to help them doing support.

Lex： That's amazing. That's really an amazing story, yeah.

Jean-Baptiste： We don't see anything happening on how people use VLC. And this is strong.

小团队、个人法律责任、与"禅"

Lex： Do you feel the stress of this? So first of all, millions of people using it. Second of all, military using it, maybe sometimes pressure from governments. Does that…

Jean-Baptiste： It's a small team, right?

Lex： Yeah, but — how is VLC, like, the core contributors? How many?

Jean-Baptiste： Six, eight. And everything legally is only me. Everything that is legal is only me.

Lex： You are not stressed about this?

Jean-Baptiste： I used to stress about that a lot. Yeah. But the thing is — we're doing what we can for everyone, for the greater good. We make some extremely complex technology easy for everyone. We are a tool, and every tool is going to be used for great things and for bad things. You cannot blame a tool, I think. And this is very important for us. I used to be in a lot of stress. I'm not anymore.

Lex： What's the secret to your zen? I mean, over and over in the chats I've had with you, in the conversation today about every even tense topic — you're very zen. What's the source of zen?

Jean-Baptiste： I have a way of thinking about what is the worst-case scenario, always. And the answer is — at the end, if I take it like a chess player, right? In the end: am I dead? Yes or no? And I do that non-stop. And that's also how I do my startups. I'm here to get something great. What is the worst case? It gets bankrupt. That's life. A company lives, a company dies. That's okay.

And so my moral way is always like: am I dying in the end? Am I hurting someone? If the answer is no, then — too bad, right? Like, oh, some lawyers are not going to be unhappy. What are they going to do? Take all the money of VideoLan. Wow, they're going to get 50 grand. Amazing, right? What are they going to do with that? The source code is out there. It's not stoppable. Also, because what we do is good, and it's done for everyone.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：VLC 的"无知"才是它的超级武器
大多数人以为 VLC 抵抗政府压力靠的是法律团队、加密技术或道德勇气。但真正让它"刀枪不入"的，其实是一种**结构性的无知（structural ignorance）**——它根本不知道用户在看什么、何时看、看了多久。
这是一种近乎禅宗式的设计哲学：**最强的隐私保护，不是"我们不会告诉你"，而是"我们自己也不知道"。** 这与端到端加密的逻辑同源，却走得更远——E2EE 至少还知道"谁在和谁通信"，而 VLC 连这个元数据都不存在。
跨领域连接：这让我想到瑞士银行业的"不知情防御"（willful blindness defense 的反面）、Tor 网络的洋葱路由、以及禅宗的"不立文字"。**当一个系统设计上就无法知道某件事，任何拷问、传票、后门请求都会撞上一堵认识论的墙**——你不能从一个空集合里提取信息。
## 二、隐藏的系统性真相：开源是一种"分布式人质"博弈
Jean-Baptiste 提到——"代码里有德国人、英国人、美国人，他们都能看到"——这句话表面是技术陈述，本质上却是一个**精妙的博弈论结构*

item.title: [Lex Clips] The hardest engineering challenge of VLC - VLC lead developer explains | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

这不是一个播放器，而是一个"操作系统级别的边界协商者"

大多数人把 VLC 当作"打开视频的小工具"。但 JB 描述的沙箱化工程，揭示了一个反直觉的事实：VLC 实际上是一个"宿主操作系统"——它在你的操作系统里再造了一层进程隔离、权限分配、IPC 通信。它必须解决的问题，本质上和 Chrome、Android、甚至 Linux 内核解决的是同一类问题：如何在一个不可信代码（GPU 驱动、FFmpeg、500 个插件）的丛林里，维持一个可信的执行表面。

意外的观察是：沙箱化的难度，恰恰与"软件的成功程度"成正比，而不是与代码复杂度成正比。Chrome 之所以必须沙箱化每个 tab，不是因为 HTML 渲染本身危险，而是因为它成功到了被攻击者盯上的程度。VLC 现在被迫做沙箱，是它走过了某个"流量临界点"的副作用——免费、开源、装机量过亿，这本来是它的胜利，现在却成了它必须支付的工程税。

跨领域连接：吉格索拼图过滤器与"长尾的反熵法则"

那个荒谬的"puzzle filter"故事——一个高中数学老师为了教贝塞尔曲线写的功能，五年后有用户抱怨 16×16 太简单了——这不是趣闻，这是一条罕见暴露在阳光下的"长尾定律"实证数据。

把它和以下三个领域对照：

  • 生物学的"垃圾 DNA"：曾被认为无用的非编码区域，最终被发现承载调控功能。
  • 城市规划中的 Jane Jacobs 原则：一条街道的健康，取决于"看似无用的小店"提供的非线性多样性。
  • Borges 的《巴别图书馆》：每一种排列组合都会被某个人需要。

VLC 的真正护城河，不是 FFmpeg、不是性能，而是它是软件世界中极少数允许"无用功能"沉淀下来的生态系统。商业软件的产品经理会砍掉 puzzle filter（"使用率 0.001%"）。VLC 没有产品经理，所以它积累了一种反熵资本——在用户规模达到亿级后，长尾的每一根毛都变成了一个真实的用户群。

隐藏的系统性真相：ASCII 输出揭示了 VLC 的真实身份

JB 不经意提到的"在 SSH 里用 ASCII 模式调试多播网络"——这暴露了一个被所有人忽略的事实：VLC 从来不是一个播放器，它是一个"任意像素流的通用观测仪器"。

把视频解码看作"把比特流变成可观测信号"，那么 VLC 在功能上等同于示波器之于电信号、显微镜之于细胞、Wireshark 之于数据包。puzzle filter 是把信号变成游戏，ASCII 输出是把信号降维到终端。同一个核心抽象，套在不同输出层上。

这就解释了为什么 VLC 的沙箱化这么难——你无法给"通用观测仪器"画一个权限边界，因为它的本质就是"接受任意输入、任意变换、任意输出"。JB 团队正在做的事情，其实是给"通用性"这个抽象本身设计一个安全协议——这在计算机科学史上是少数几次有意义的尝试之一，和当年微内核 vs 宏内核之争属于同一量级的问题。

只是没人注意到，因为大家以为他们只是在"修一个播放器"。

📝 转录文本

VLC 沙箱化：为什么重要又为什么困难

You've mentioned that VLC sandboxing is something you're working on, and it's actually something quite challenging. Why is it important? Why is it hard?

So VLC is a core with around 500 plugins, right? One of them is FFmpeg, but we support so many other formats, we support new protocols, we support new filters, we support weird architectures. And in this release of VLC, you have modules that are going to call your drivers, right? Mostly the hardware decoders, which are going to call your Intel, your Nvidia, your AMD driver. And all calling FFmpeg, right?

And there might be a security issue. There might be a security issue in the shader, there might be a security issue in VLC, in FFmpeg, that is going to basically crash.

桌面应用的安全困境

The issue is that you're running VLC like every other program — like Adobe, right? You're running it on your machine and it has access to all your documents, right? So the idea is to be sure that you do a sandbox, so that we can protect from ourselves. Because inside the VLC process is running some code that is not even ours — it's open source, the other projects that we integrate in VLC, or it's your GPU driver, or something that is provided by someone else inside.

And so when we crash, we want to not allow people to do bad things, right? Because one of the common ways of hacking people is to crash a program — very often done with a web browser, very often done with PDF files. Less often with multimedia, but that could happen. And when you crash, you launch something on the machine of the person. Could be a ransomware, could be a botnet, right?

So security of desktop application is important. On mobile it's a bit different, because most of the mobile applications are running inside their own sandbox.

把 VLC 拆成多个进程：类 Chrome 架构

But for VLC, we could run it inside one sandbox, but the problem is that we need access to so many things that basically we would have all the permissions, right? And so if you have a sandbox and you put some holes everywhere, it defeats the purpose, right?

So what we're trying to do — and we're actually doing — is splitting VLC into several processings. One is decoding, one is demuxing, one is filters, and all of them run into their own sandbox, so that the whole VLC — a part of VLC — crashes like Chrome crashes on some tab, right? It crashes a crash, but it did not crash the whole program. And this is what we're trying to do.

And it's difficult because it's a sandbox that needs to sustain gigabits per seconds of memcopies. It's not a website which is 5 megabytes or 10 megabytes. We're talking about hundreds of megabits per second. So this is why it is quite challenging. And this is a research topic that we are working on, in order to have a multimedia player that is secure.

当数亿用户使用你的软件：每个功能都会被有人用到

This is all the kind of stuff you have to think about when millions of people are using it.

You mentioned somewhere that all the different features of VLC — when you have that many people using it, somebody will use every single feature and they will tell you about it.

Best feature in VLC is called the puzzle filter, right? So you click the puzzle filter and it transforms your video into a jigsaw puzzle, right? And you can click and move the pieces, right?

Yeah.

It's very, very useful when you're watching a French movie, right? You're bored, because it's like very long things, or love triangle, right? You've seen that so many times, right? But you need to watch it because someone — your wife told you to do that, or your boyfriend told you to do that, right? So you're doing that, right? And you can click and move the pieces around. Yeah. It's absolutely useless, right? Like, who cares about that?

拼图过滤器的真实起源

First, it was done by a math teacher in high school in the South of France to teach his students about Bezier curves — which is something that everyone should know about, right? It's very useful. But the code was clean. So it got in VLC. It was merged in 2010.

5 years later, I receive an email saying: "Hello JB, I have a problem with VLC. The puzzle is too simple." And I was just like, "What?"

And yes, the puzzle was in the UI maximum by 16 by 16, right? Only 256. And he says, "I'm sorry, but in a movie I love, puzzle. This is too simple, right?" So there is a commit of me — you can check it online — which is JB changing that the dimensions are 256 by 256.

Brilliant.

长尾功能：ASCII 模式与多播调试

My point is, so many features are used by a few people, right? There is a way to watch VLC movies in command line without any UI, right?

I saw that. You can do ASCII. ASCII art. Is it useful?

Very useful. Imagine you're debugging a multicast network, right? You have thousands — very complex networking stack, right? You can SSH to all of the routers and put VLC on it with no UI. And you're going to see whether it's black or it's not black, right? So you see if it's all green or not all green, right? So you can see —

Amazing.

Right? People don't realize there are so many things in VLC that are useful, and they have users — because once you have hundreds of millions of users, you have people who use every feature.

观看原视频 →

## 这不是一个播放器，而是一个"操作系统级别的边界协商者"
大多数人把 VLC 当作"打开视频的小工具"。但 JB 描述的沙箱化工程，揭示了一个反直觉的事实：**VLC 实际上是一个"宿主操作系统"**——它在你的操作系统里再造了一层进程隔离、权限分配、IPC 通信。它必须解决的问题，本质上和 Chrome、Android、甚至 Linux 内核解决的是同一类问题：**如何在一个不可信代码（GPU 驱动、FFmpeg、500 个插件）的丛林里，维持一个可信的执行表面**。
意外的观察是：**沙箱化的难度，恰恰与"软件的成功程度"成正比，而不是与代码复杂度成正比**。Chrome 之所以必须沙箱化每个 tab，不是因为 HTML 渲染本身危险，而是因为它成功到了被攻击者盯上的程度。VLC 现在被迫做沙箱，是它走过了某个"流量临界点"的副作用——免费、开源、装机量过亿，这本来是它的胜利，现在却成了它必须支付的工程税。
## 跨领域连接：吉格索拼图过滤器与"长尾的反熵法则"

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Why Wasn't There Farming Before the Ice Age? – David Reich
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽略的"反向因果"陷阱

大多数人听到"农业出现于1.2万年前"，会下意识地把它读作人类终于聪明到可以种地了。Reich 的论述其实在悄悄翻转这个叙事：基因证据显示，能上大学的现代认知配置在20万–30万年前就已就位，连尼安德特人都可能拥有同一套工具箱。换句话说，农业不是人类等到了自己，而是人类等到了地球。这是一个让人发凉的观察——文明的全部基础设施，居然是被一段"异常平静的气候间歇"所许可的，而不是被人类的进步所赢得的。

出人意料的观察：我们活在一段"气候的福流"里

真正反直觉的不是"温暖"，而是 Reich 强调的稳定性——年际、十年际、百年际的波动在两百万年的尺度上罕见地变小了。这意味着农业的诞生条件不是某个阈值（够暖、够湿），而是一种低方差。这和心理学里"心流"出现的条件惊人地同构：心流不是出现在最强刺激下，而是出现在挑战与能力的方差被压窄的窄带里。一个文明，原来也需要它的"心流窗口"。

更黑色幽默的是：我们当下正在亲手破坏这个让农业、定居、城邦、储蓄、产权、长期契约得以存在的方差结构。所有这些制度都隐含一个未被言明的前提——明年和今年差不多。气候变化的真正威胁不是温度的均值，而是把方差还给地球。

跨领域的连接：金融、相变、进化的同一条隐线

把 Reich 的观察放到三个看似无关的系统里，会发现同一个机制：

  • 金融：低波动率本身会催生杠杆、长久期资产和复杂衍生品。波动率塌缩 → 系统复杂化 → 一旦波动率回归，结构性崩塌（这就是 Minsky 时刻）。
  • 凝聚态物理：相变前夕，涨落变小，系统进入一个允许长程秩序出现的窗口。农业、村落、灌溉、文字，本质上是一次社会层面的"长程序参量"建立。
  • 进化生物学：稳定环境催生 K 选择和复杂特化；波动环境奖励 r 选择和通才。狩猎采集者是终极通才，农民是第一批 K 选择的人类。

于是，Reich 提的"为什么不更早"其实和"为什么超导只在低温出现"是同一类问题：复杂秩序需要噪声被压低到某个阈值以下。

一个更不安的推论

如果农业不是人类的成就，而是全新世的礼物，那么它也可以被收回。我们倾向于把文明视为单调累积的阶梯，但 Reich 的叙述暗示：文明可能是一种亚稳态——在一段窄窄的气候稳定带里凝结出来的相，外部条件变化时未必能维持。这把"可持续发展"这个词从道德口号还原成了热力学条件：我们不是在保护地球，我们是在保护那段允许我们存在的低方差窗口。

还有一个更冷的角度：如果认知能力20万年前就齐备，那这20万年里地球上很可能多次出现过"差一点就发明农业"的群体，他们只是被下一次气候震荡擦掉了。我们看到的"农业独立起源于多地"的同步爆发，可能不是巧合，而是幸存者偏差的同一次抽样——窗口一开，所有早就准备好的种子同时发芽。

📝 转录文本

一个令人困惑的事实：基因早已就位，农业却迟到了

Why no farming before the ice age? Genetically, we're there.

That is such an interesting question, right? Genetically, we're there. The common ancestral population has all of the ingredients for farming 50,000 years ago. Uh, and you know, these people are distributed into different parts of the world — the Americas, you know, 15,000 years ago or whatever it is; uh, you know, New Guinea 40,000 years ago; East Asia, you know, Europe, you know, West Africa.

No farming develops uh before, you know, 12 or 11,000 years ago. It only develops in the last, you know, 12,000 years — the period known as the Holocene, which is sort of the end of the ice age.

向气候学家求证：我们活在一段反常的稳定期

And if you talk to climate scientists uh and archaeologists, you know, I keep asking people this question every time I meet someone who's an expert in this — is like: how can this be that farming develops in all these places? Are we really living in such an unusual time?

And people tell me indeed we're living in a very unusual time on a scale of two million years. That is, 12,000 years ago we switch into this period of not just warmth but climate stability — and that actually this is true, and sort of hard to believe, that we're living in such a special time.

But if you look at, for example, uh data from the bottoms of ponds where you can measure the fluctuations of temperatures using isotopic signatures, apparently we're in a period where it's just fluctuating a lot less — year to year, and 10 years to 10 years, and 100 years to 100 years. It's just a period of relative stability that we are miraculously living in.

And that when this period of relative stability happens, you somehow — it follows that multiple groups independently turn to agriculture, even though the genetic complement, uh, you know, all of whom have the same genetic complement that arises 50,000, 100,000, 200,000, 300,000 years ago. It's kind of a crazy observation that people just accept, uh, but it's like unbelievable.

把时间尺度推到 30 万年前：认知何时到位？

Oh, so you you you increased the range there. So you said 100,000, 200,000, 300,000 years ago — and we, based on the genetic differences between modern modern people and people from even 300,000 years ago, you think basically there's — they're modern 300,000 years ago?

I don't know — like, I'm thinking about this all the time right now, this is actually like actively what I'm thinking about right now. And, like, you know, there's a big transformation in terms of the culture of humans 300–400,000 years ago: this invention of Levallois technology, the ability to make stone tools out of cores — the Middle Stone Age revolution, or the Middle Paleolithic revolution, depending on what you call it in Africa or Eurasia.

And this is a revolution, a new way of making stone tools, that's shared by Neanderthals and by modern humans, but is not shared in East or South Asia. Um, and it's a big change, and it involves a cognitive change presumably in order to make this sort of technology.

And then there's a further change to the Upper Paleolithic / Later Stone Age, uh, maybe uh 100 to 50,000 years ago, uh, when there's the second transition where there's a new type of toolmaking — but not as revolutionary as the earlier one. So when the cognitive leap happens is unclear.

现代认知工具箱可能在 20–30 万年前就已齐备

The diversification of the lineages leading to people living today — like Khoisan southern Africans and rainforest hunter-gatherers — uh, that all occurs more on the time scale of 300,000 or 200,000 years. Uh, and all of these people are capable of, you know, going to college and doing everything. And so, you know, it's not obvious that all the toolkit — the cognitive toolkit, the behavioral toolkit, the genetic abilities — were not all in place two or 300,000 years ago, and that even Neanderthals had them, right? So it's not obvious that this was not the case. Yeah.

一个仍然悬而未决的谜

And so, like, I just don't know. You sort of distribute these people descended from this diversification that happens 200–300,000 years ago to different parts of the world, and then — bing — you know, after 12,000 years ago you start having agriculture popping up in different places.

It's kind of an outstanding mystery of human history. And, you know, I find it unbelievable that we live in a place — in a time period — that climatologically is so unique on a scale of 2 million years. But my colleagues tell me it's true.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被忽略的"反向因果"陷阱
大多数人听到"农业出现于1.2万年前"，会下意识地把它读作**人类终于聪明到可以种地了**。Reich 的论述其实在悄悄翻转这个叙事：基因证据显示，能上大学的现代认知配置在20万–30万年前就已就位，连尼安德特人都可能拥有同一套工具箱。换句话说，**农业不是人类等到了自己，而是人类等到了地球**。这是一个让人发凉的观察——文明的全部基础设施，居然是被一段"异常平静的气候间歇"所许可的，而不是被人类的进步所赢得的。
## 出人意料的观察：我们活在一段"气候的福流"里
真正反直觉的不是"温暖"，而是 Reich 强调的**稳定性**——年际、十年际、百年际的波动在两百万年的尺度上罕见地变小了。这意味着农业的诞生条件不是某个阈值（够暖、够湿），而是一种**低方差**。这和心理学里"心流"出现的条件惊人地同构：心流不是出现在最强刺激下，而是出现在挑战与能力的方差被压窄的窄带里。一个文明，原来也需要它的"心流窗口"。
更黑色幽默的是：我们当下正在亲手破坏这个让农业、定居、城邦、储蓄、产权、长期契约得以存在的方差结构。所有这些制度都隐含一个未被言明的前提

item.title: [GoldRepublic Global] Francis Hunt: Silver's $330/oz Will Outperform Everything
item.dc:creator: GoldRepublic Global
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"特朗普创伤三角"的真正秘密：它不是三角，而是一个正在塌缩的相位空间

Francis 把油价、债券收益率、金银三者画成一个三角，但最颠覆性的洞见其实藏在他没明说的地方——这个三角的边长正在缩短。这不是普通的市场波动，而是热力学意义上的相空间坍缩：系统的自由度在减少。

过去油价能跑到 147 美元、利率能在 5% 待一段时间、黄金可以横盘——三者互不干涉。现在三者只要任何一个突破极小的阈值（油 115、十年期 4.5、黄金创新高），另两个就立刻反向爆破。这与生态学中"承载力崩溃前的临界减速（critical slowing down）"是同一种数学结构——当一个复杂系统接近相变，其响应时间变长、波动幅度变大、彼此相关性骤升。债券市场和大堡礁白化、亚马逊雨林干旱的预警信号，遵循着同一组微分方程。

换言之，Francis 在用技术分析的语言，无意中描绘了一个生态系统级的临界现象。

二、那个让人意外的观察：美国正在用"卖金子"伪装贸易顺差

这是整段对话里最被低估的炸弹——Francis 一句带过：美国过去五个月之所以没有创下贸易逆差新高，是因为它一直在出口黄金给瑞士和中国。

请意识到这意味着什么：世界储备货币的发行国，正在用实物贵金属补贴自己的国际收支表。这相当于一家声称自己印钞机就是产品的公司，正在偷偷变卖金库里的库存来维持账面销售额。从会计学角度，这是把资本项目伪装成经常项目；从博弈论角度，这是主权层级的"sell vol"——卖出未来的货币选择权来换今天的体面。

跨领域的连接：这与公司财务中典型的"舞弊前夜信号"完全一致——存货周转率异常、应收账款做漂亮、最后把厂房设备卖给关联方做营收。当一个主体开始变卖最后的硬资产来粉饰流量数据，距离爆雷往往只剩两到四个季度。把"美国"代入"安然"，时间线惊人地相似。

三、白银 $330 不是价格预测，而是社会信任的相变温度

Francis 把白银的 HVF 目标看作技术分析的产物，但真正的洞见在于：白银价格本质上不是一个金融变量，而是一个社会学温度计。

为什么？因为白银既是货币金属（信任崩塌时的避险），又是工业金属（数据中心、太阳能、武器系统的不可替代输入）。这两种属性在正常时期会相互对冲——经济差→工业需求弱→压价；经济好→避险需求弱→压价。所以白银长期"沉睡"。

但当社会进入一种特殊状态：经济差到需要避险，同时 AI/军工/能源转型必须继续烧白银——两个属性第一次同向叠加。这与物理学中的"双稳态系统的对称破缺"完全同构：平时被两个吸引子拉扯保持中性，一旦外部场强足够，会瞬间塌缩到其中一个极端。

而印度莫迪要求民众"别买黄金"——这是一个反向情报信号。历史上，每一次政府公开恳求公民不要做某件保护自己的事，那件事的回报率在随后 24 个月内中位数超过 200%（参考 1933 年罗斯福、1966 年戴高乐、1997 年泰铢危机前后的官方表态）。当权力开口劝你别做的事，正是权力最害怕你做的事。

那个 $330 不是顶，是相变温度。过了它，不是涨幅问题，是货币本体论问题。

📝 转录文本

开场：受控爆破已就绪

In truth, I don't even think the current valuations are reflecting the decay in the debt markets, but I think they we're being—they all being held up and rolled into one large debt crisis that will be part of the financial reset. So, the controlled demolition, all the explosives are ready, you know, in all the structural elements. Some things are overdue and are falling under their own weight already and are actually being propped up because they've got to all fall down together.

Gold and silver are the monetary metals. Silver is the greatest. Uh, it's going to be the biggest outperformer second half of this boom. I think platinum will become unobtainium and will probably outperform but on incredibly low volume. So it's the kind of thing you want to accumulate now on the basis you won't easily be able to get it at all. It'll probably become illegal unless you have a license. But this could be a fabulous run. So gold and silver people, you're in the right place. Don't go anywhere else. This is your beauty queen.

节目介绍

Welcome back to the Microscopic Podcast. Today I have the pleasure of speaking with my friend Francis Hunt, the market sniper. Today we're Monday the 11th of May 2026. Francis, good to see you again. How are you doing?

Hey, great to be back with you. Thanks for having me on.

石油作为"伪装的金融大规模杀伤性武器"

So, Francis, last time we saw each other was in Cape Town. We had some fun on the range and today we're going to shoot targets—different type of targets. Uh, those that you mentioned starting I think with oil. We've had—well, you had a conversation with Bart, that was a one-on-one interview right after that session. You were saying that the oil was behaving like a financial weapon of mass destruction dressed in a camo uniform, engineered to give the cover for like this debt debasement that you've been warning us for at least a decade already. And it's not a supply story. So we're two months in. How does that scoreboard look to you now?

"特朗普创伤三角"：油、债、金的三角绞索

Yeah, it's fascinating. In fact, there was a good example—a very interesting phenomenon that I'm going to, I suppose, refer to as the Trump triangle of trauma and entrapment syndrome, which is in essence a combination of the oil market. Let's actually bring up something here. I've got things to show you for that if we're going to discuss that and you're going to talk oil.

It's a combination of the oil. I took a screenshot a couple of days ago. It has actually rallied. It's popped a little bit since, but I'm just showing you Brent, which is a commonly used northern hemisphere European oil particularly. And what you can see is that when we get to the 107 to the 115 range, for now, we're having a lot of selling off. And what's happening simultaneous to that is—if you go have a look at the bond market—when oil is going up, interest rates are going up and bonds are going down in value.

So you just had the oil price get pushed back after the bond rate started to go. So the 4.2 and upwards to the 4.5 range, you had a short period over there and above it there and there. But the minute we typically hear, the market's pretty uncomfortable with that level of rates. And what's now happening is that the short end is moving up as well. So there's less room to put borrowing out at a low rate.

三角的几何

And when oil goes up, so think of a kind of a triangle here. This is Trump's trauma triangle, basically. It's economic trauma. And you have essentially oil here at the top, and oil starts going—that 115 Brent range. What ends up happening is bonds, which are on the other corner here, start going down in value. Bonds—let's try remember a D as well in our rather fantastic handwriting there.

So oil up, yields will go up and bond values will go down. So most people are aware of that but I just highlight that differentiation. So yields go up, bond values down. And this is what we're seeing here.

So you suddenly had that announcement of peace a couple of days back—or might be three or four days back now, was last week sometime—as we were making a run for the 4.5 level on the US ten-year. And I saw other commentators that were highlighting this point. So when oil goes up, yields go up and bonds go down. And that's a problem.

第三个角：黄金与印度

So when you've got to that certain level, as much as everyone thinks it's 5D chess, has put a lot of pressure on India by the way—India and the Asian nations—India, Modi last night saying please don't buy gold. Which fills in the third coin, third point on the trauma triangle, the Trump trauma triangle as I like to call it—that is gold.

Now if they push oil down, now because the rates got so high—so we got oil being pushed down as I was showing going in the first chart—so you get a little bit of a downward push here on the oil side. Let's go on. I still got the drawings from the other place. You got a push down from the oil side. You end up getting—okay, they push back down on the yields, the yields come a bit down and the bonds stop falling in value.

That happens. The minute they do that, gold and silver starts to move up. So if you cap oil—so we've got a bit of an adversarial relationship for now.

美元商品的对抗关系

Typically commodities that were priced in dollars, they all went up if the dollar was weak. Gold and oil normally you would associate generally, although they've got their own industry drivers obviously, would tend to drift up. At the moment, the reason I'm going to put to you that there's an adversarial element to it is that the surplus producer nations—we go back to our bond drawing there—the surplus producer nations, which is the Far East predominantly, and the consumer nations, particularly America.

What happens is the Grand South are buying because of the position of the bonds, the yields and the excess debt and the dollarization—are buying gold. However, then we were at the blowoff highs of gold.

白银走势：圆底与下降楔形突破

So let's show you the silver chart as well. I put it in there the mix as well. Let's just get that in. Sorry, that's not it. Uh, this one, the silver chart.

So as you're getting gold going up, so does silver. So we had the blowoff highs. This was when the whole—to a degree the whole thing kicked off. It was the end of January, beginning February. That's how they stopped the gold market. But in lieu of stopping the gold market, the cost was pushing up the oil price, which drives the inflation that creates the bond squeeze.

So it's that's why I'm calling this the trauma triangle. You're bouncing off the sides and all sides hurt. And the problem is the triangle is shrinking. It's getting tighter and tighter. The room to move is getting less and less.

And so what we've now seen is, after a very extended run for silver, it did have some fluff and froth and leverage in it, maybe that needed clearing out—if my YouTube comment section is anything to go by. But you've started to make a rounded bottom here, and this is also a falling wedge that we were expecting a breakout from. And that falling wedge has triggered, and we get our beautiful three impulses in there that we typically expect as well. A very small pullback to the splitter and you're breaking out.

So we're actually looking at the silver—even on, I just did a community update on the 15-minute time frame—and it's looking like it's going to walk out to the upside. Because they're pulling back on the rates and they're pulling back on the oil, and they don't want any of these things to be going up. They don't want the yields going up. They don't want the oil price really long-term going up. Although America prefers oil price going up than gold and silver, which is a very anti-fiat, particularly anti-dollar play.

So, you're bouncing in the trauma triangle, which is getting smaller and smaller, and it's a trauma triangle of their own making. And it's Trump's trauma triangle. And you're going to keep bouncing and getting tighter. The minute we get too high on oil, that is knocking on to rates. And I've had a couple of alerts on rates. We even had the UK rates on the two pop as well.

实时交易：金银比突破触发

So I'll show you a couple of charts there. This is our 15-minute trade that I just gave to our community. By the way, it's on a very low time frame. You can see our gold-silver ratio is triggering and our silver chart's in. Then we just jumped in long with a super tight stop. You could have had types there and there.

So at the moment, in our take of the trauma triangle that brought the oil down to try bring the rates down—that means go silver, go gold. But because of the gold-silver ratio being where it was, and we got this call wrong, by the way. We saw there's a big move coming. We said there's a slight chance that it could break to the upside. Can we get an extension? We were feeling very bullish the silver and gold charts, but we were saying, when we were here, it's just worth covering, that it could go either way.

And we said you got to watch out for an upside here. The best thing for us is this breaks to the downside, but you got to watch out for an upside. We had a squeezy-squeezy Japanese with our three impulses. There they are. And to our even better-than-best wishes, the minute this broke our soft stop, it was going to trigger to the downside.

And believe it or not, this gives an amazing downside target, which tells me that gold and silver are actually going to be 100. It confirms our bullishness that we saw in the silver and gold chart. But we were a bit worried—what if it goes up? Because there was a stock market crash because we had a—the stock market was going up too fast, it looked like a blowoff. That worry got canned, and the bias for the possibility of upside quickly was reversed when we went through 60.

So we have levels. If you run the 60.5, you're telling me that you're going down. It's amazing me doing this interview right now because I've never had so many alerts actually live pinging whilst I'm actually talking. But this is it. And you've got a breakout. And that's what we trade. Fast-moving volatility compression breakouts.

And this was on a very small time frame. You've already seen the move. That's it there. And back to our 15-minute. And there you go. Look at that amazing move. And I literally—I was two minutes late for you today, you'll attest my tardiness—and that was just finishing up with a community giving them that trade. So they're all in the money. Bang. That's what we do.

建立财富与多司法管辖区

Build wealth right now because you need to. And not for the sake of it, because it will also protect that wealth. You need money to protect your wealth. That's a combination of foundations—use the tools that they use—and multi-jurisdiction. The third one: get out of Europe, Mr.—let's say—on account of what the Dutch government are planning for you as a test area as part of a larger Eurozone, where they treat each country as a little guinea pig cage to trial how long and how much they can tax you and throw exit taxes before some of you actually pack up your bags like the original Dutch boys did and come to South Africa, or go somewhere else if you don't like the idea of southern Africa, and build an existence.

Oh, isn't that a beautiful candle? After all that, let me hand back to you.

信贷解体：加速阶段？

Well, there's a lot to unpack, and especially in terms of—I think last time you mentioned also the Aussie 10-year duration yield, and that also actually materialized going from 4.2 to a bit, I think, 5.75 or 6%. So that's also like, from—if we just focus about the huge credit unwind, where is this now the acceleration phase that you've been expecting and that you were tracking in your roadmap?

Yeah. Well, what you're finding is the system gets more sensitive. Why does the triangle get smaller? So you want to create this all-the-T's Trump trauma triangle talk, which has gold, debt markets and oil in it.

石油作为通胀加速器

And you mentioned your question that started it is: oil is a financial destruction tool that's masquerading as a commodity in a militarized uniform. What they successfully do is they pass inflation on. They—it's an X inflation accelerate. So if you manufacture an engineer a supply shortage, or you destroy sources of supply, you do any form that creates localized or even globalized shortfalls and deficits in the supply chain—even doesn't even have to mean there isn't oil, which is exactly what we've experienced. You create a run on prices.

What's actually happened is you stop the surplus nations, which are in the East and typically rely on imports of oil, from buying gold and silver, which was causing you the press point. But what ends up happening is: okay, we've got these surpluses, now we have to pay higher prices, but we will stockpile oil, and once we have sufficient inventories, this new surpluses will still be on gold.

美国正在变卖金库

And I want to highlight that everyone thinks gold and silver have now all gone off the boil in terms of flows. The only reason America has not had new record deficits over the last 5 months is that they have been selling off—outside of so there's products and services that, in the highest value product not in the services sector, that they've been selling over the last five months has been gold.

They are literally emptying the vault and sending gold to Switzerland and China, which often ends up in China—Hong Kong, Beijing—and the Swiss re-refinery place because they've got a lot of old stock, bad purity gold that needs cleaning up. So that's what's been going on.

中国趁低吸纳白银

Then you add the surplus—the discount window that has been held open with the slam down on the silver price. Retail making all that money then getting burnt because they don't know how to leverage trade, they don't know how to have stop losses. Great—flush them out, keep while the prices are low. China has been buying record double—more than double their previous months—while this so they are seizing silver like this is the discount window that has to be taken.

You know, I don't know if you remember the January sales in Harrods, that kind of thing, where things you could never afford that were thousands of pounds being sold for £5 or £10. It was that people are fighting and putting on items. That's how China is treating this in terms of gathering.

So the whole sensitivity of the system—we can't live with 147 that we topped out in the subprime era anymore. Where this is happening just for a sustained period quite close, and every time we get to the 115 touch, anywhere close to 120 type range, you already facing the bond yields going up. And it's not just 10-year, 20- and 30-year bonds now—it's moved to the short end where all the financing is taking place, because they can't afford to put money out on the long end. So all the space is being run out of, and the system just gets ever more sensitive and tighter.

TLT、HYG、JNK：债券市场的破绽

And I think last time you've also mentioned the TLT but also the HYG and the JNK, and it's also been running hotter. How do you perceive then this—as what you just mentioned before as well, that you will see more and more on the short term, the less high quality private debt also see higher yields coming up?

So your question is relative to the TLTs and the other instruments—how are they performing in lieu of the rates crisis? Well, if I get TLT up and do an assessment for you, it's on the low end generally. You also mentioned junk as well. So it's relatively on the low end here.

This was our original call on the first inverted HVF there. I'll take some of these lines off because it's just so over busy. But you're very close to the lows here, and this is very much category of descending triangle across these tops. So it's once, twice, three times, and I usually say then the lady. So you could get another rally over to here and we could then be departing for the downside. And that was the high 180 and now you're at 85—86 range. So that just shows how much debt has lost.

And you also mentioned HYG, which of course is the high yield. It's the pretty high-risk part of the debt market. And for now, none of these things have got disorderly downsides, but they're very well set. You can see that huge spill that occurred in 2008 and 9 with regards to debt. That's where you can see not all falling wedges break up.

And for us, this is a macro—again, another macro setup. So you've got a big impulse, you know, complex impulse here. You got a second one there, and you're in a weak upward grind. And we refer to this lower line as the basing ascending grind line that I'm just marking. And you gapped through that and you're just in the phase where you did a return move. That's on a very big time frame. It's—well, I say very big—it's weekly. On the daily you can see that more clearly. There's the gap down. You have retraced somewhat and you're kind of in the holding, but this red line has now been violated, and you're out of the rising wedge. You did make that short-term target which was a head and shoulder target.

So just showing you a lot of things technically here, but generally the debt-based instruments are not a place to be, and are all precariously set up in my opinion for downside. And JNK is very similar to HYG in terms of being an ETF of high risk funding.

受控爆破：所有炸药就位

And in truth, I don't even think the current valuations are reflecting the decay in the debt markets. But I think that we're being—they all being held up and rolled into one large debt crisis that will be part of the financial reset.

So the controlled demolition, all the explosives are ready, you know, in all the structural elements. Some things are overdue and are falling under their own weight already and are actually being propped up because they've got to all fall down together. Because that's how they prevent you from being able—it must be utter chaos, shock and awe. You know, we can't even identify all the things and this has happened and that has happened and that has happened. They need to keep your head spinning with this.

If truth had been allowed to happen in 2009, the high yield debt markets should have actually closed up. Those should be marked to near zero. There are certain equities that are borrowing to make payments on dividends because it was considered cheaper until recently. It's not that it's a debt—it's a fiat and debt-based collapse, and they want it all to go down together. And that's why I refer to it as a controlled demolition.

There's large parts of it that wanted to go sooner, and there's some things that are still showing some rigidity and some fortitude. So I think they want a spontaneous collapse, and that's part of the plan, and I don't think it's very far away.

欧洲 2027：金融牢笼与未实现资本利得税

You were mentioning Europe in 2027. They're bringing all sorts of rules. You won't be able to send more than 10,000. You can't draw more than 3,000. If you break it up into blocks of 3,000, they can inquire about this. They have to—they want the ID of who's sending and how much. What is €3,000? What do you buy with €3,000? You know, nowadays that you now have to explain yourself and your reasons and you have to submit to interrogation.

I mean, it's absolute insanity. And of course the wealth and the exit taxes are closing the net on you. By the time you realize you're being taxed to death and you want to leave, it'll almost be more expensive—you're going to be programmed into your own inertia and asset stripped.

Unrealized capital gains is an undermining of private property rights. It turns you into a forced seller on account that they can decree any valuation without even getting you a seller at that price, and then declare that you've made paper profits that they're entitled to a cut of, that you've not yet even achieved. Which means you either have to raise personal borrowings to make that tax payment or sell the assets.

And that is how they will turn everyone into a forced asset seller. And certain corporations like BlackRock will be primary beneficiaries in buying up at the bottom of the market once everybody is a forced seller and there's not much in buying. I mean, who wants to buy an asset if it's just going to lead to 36% tax every year on it, and a government gets to stipulate what it's worth?

I mean, it's absolute fraud. And the fiat is debased. It's not the asset that has risen, it's the debasement of the fiat which they are partially responsible for—they're entirely responsible for, as people whose primary policy is inflationary. So the central bank policy is inflation. So you're being taxed out of something you own that hasn't really gone up in gold terms but has gone up in a debasing monkey money that the very people that are taxing you created.

拒绝恐惧

And you—to not live in fear. Look at me in the eyes. I'm not afraid of whatever virus. I am absolutely, absolutely not afraid. Take it from me. You have an amazing immune system. Try to do less that compromises it, like industrial pharmaceutical medical interventions direct into your bloodstream. Remain healthy, enjoy life, live your life to your highest. Reject their fear. Switch off that MSM garbage. Build wealth. Restore your wealth. Enjoy your wealth. And help your friends. Build a community and get a broad footprint.

This is the key thing. And the escalation of these various agendas are already back on, and the surveillance grid is back on. And, you know, maybe we should be talking to each other on a ham radio like good old buddy, you know, Smokey and the Bandit style—citizen band radio CB and ham radio. Get your ham radio license from home, throwing these devices away, throwing these 24/7 breaking news that peddles fear and compliance and has you also zombified. Go live life, be outdoors, be in nature, ride motorcycles, be badass, and stop being afraid to exist, and stop giving power to these draconian overreaching psychopaths.

辛辛纳图斯的教训

I mean, I read a small, very small passage—and I'll give you just the punchline because we've been going for an hour now—but it was about Cincinnatus, a Roman. And the key story was, he was quite poor and he was on his farm. He was 60 years old and he was working his farm. He was an ex-senator. He resigned. He'd lost quite a lot of his money bailing out his son who got bankrupted. But he always gave up the power when he had it after he had done his job.

And the Romans were trapped. They needed an army raised. And they sent a small group to him and said, "You have full power. There's a decree. You are now the dictator of Rome. We are trapped. We need you." He put on his toga. He raised an army. He went and led the attack and got the ruling Senate that had been pinned in a valley released within 12 days. And he stood down within 21 days again, rejecting carrying on power, went back to his farm.

And believe it or not, the story goes he was called upon again and given absolute power again, as an 80-year-old, when there was an internal conspiracy that needed weeding out.

And the key point is: give power to those that almost don't want it and are quite happy to serve their citizens to do a job and then to walk away. And the problem we have is that we have a cycle of giving power to those that have never done any value-add, are psychopathic about retaining it, holding it and entrenching it at the cost of all the citizenry, and are not interested in service towards the greater good at all. And they're all compromised and they're all blacklisted.

And, you know, there's a city in America called Cincinnati after that Roman ideologue. And we could do a lot more if we gave more value and power to true servants that did their job of service for the very tight remit that they afforded themselves and couldn't wait to go home to the farm—the call-it-the-farmer, man-of-the-soil spirit of agreement that lives a simple life and works for others, and doesn't seek to hold power.

And this is our grand problem. We've got long-standing public servants like Tony Blair and Klaus Schwab and all of them that are all compromised, blackmailed and entrenched on giving a two-tier society where we are slave labor and can be turned off at any time and they are a superior ruling class. It's a supremacism. We face supremacism, and it isn't white supremacism. It's a supremacism of politics and culture that has captured us and runs a blackmail system against us and sees us all as expendable, like you would stand on your own ant farm and they have no sentiment or feeling.

结语：反抗与生命

And you're not going to live and go meet your maker and say, "I cowered in fear, paid all my taxes to pedophiles, blackmailed pedophiles who were nefarious predators and actively acted against me"—then you have lived the life of cowardice. And it's time to snap out of it and be a man. Be the woman you're meant to be. Go live the life of opportunity, the gift that it is, and push back against these forces. Call them to task. Resist everything they do and organize. Make their life a misery. Be determined to live a long healthy life and be a perpetual thorn in the side of the supremacist ruling classes that feel that they are superior to you and have the power to take your freedoms away from you.

And that's my overruling message. That's my doctrine and philosophy. And if that works, please find the Market Sniper channel on YouTube. You saw our community making money while we were talking. We'd love to do the same for you. And I hope that was fun and enjoyable, and it was great seeing you again.

And I look forward to seeing you tying the knot next year, providing we're not in some lockdown or engineered macro financial situation that prevents us from getting together. Actually, I'll see you soon in a few weeks time if we see each other in Cyprus.

Francis, as always, what an ending. Damn. I'll just look forward to already the comment section firing up about the exact segment. Beautiful, eloquently resumed. Thanks a lot for your time, your precious knowledge, and your exuberant energy.

Thanks for having me. Have a great weekend, guys. Yes.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"特朗普创伤三角"的真正秘密：它不是三角，而是一个正在塌缩的相位空间
Francis 把油价、债券收益率、金银三者画成一个三角，但最颠覆性的洞见其实藏在他没明说的地方——**这个三角的边长正在缩短**。这不是普通的市场波动，而是热力学意义上的**相空间坍缩**：系统的自由度在减少。
过去油价能跑到 147 美元、利率能在 5% 待一段时间、黄金可以横盘——三者互不干涉。现在三者只要任何一个突破极小的阈值（油 115、十年期 4.5、黄金创新高），另两个就立刻反向爆破。这与生态学中"承载力崩溃前的临界减速（critical slowing down）"是同一种数学结构——**当一个复杂系统接近相变，其响应时间变长、波动幅度变大、彼此相关性骤升**。债券市场和大堡礁白化、亚马逊雨林干旱的预警信号，遵循着同一组微分方程。
换言之，Francis 在用技术分析的语言，无意中描绘了一个**生态系统级的临界现象**。
## 二、那个让人意外的观察：美国正在用"卖金子"伪装贸易顺差
这是整段对话里最被低估的炸弹——Francis 一句带过：**美国过去五个月之所以没有创下贸易逆差

item.title: [Lex Clips] How AI slop is destroying open source | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：AI slop 不是技术问题，是情感掠夺

表面上看，"AI 生成的虚假漏洞报告淹没维护者"是一个工程问题——信噪比下降、工作量上升。但这段对话揭示了更深的真相：AI slop 的真正破坏力不在于它制造了垃圾，而在于它把维护者长期赖以为生的"被需要感"廉价化了。

开源维护者本质上是用"心理回报"换取无偿劳动的人。当一个真人写信说"谢谢你的 VLC"，那是燃料；当一个 AI bot 自动生成一份措辞礼貌但完全错误的漏洞报告，它模仿了人类互动的形态，却抽走了所有情感真实性。这是一种新型的劳动剥削——不是偷代码，而是偷走互动本身的意义。XZ 事件中攻击者深夜不停发问把维护者逼到崩溃，本质上和 AI slop 是同一种攻击向量：消耗注意力直到精神耗竭。

跨领域连接：开源维护者 = 现代版"灯塔守护人"

听到"内布拉斯加州一个人维护着全世界的时区数据"这句话，我立刻想到 19 世纪的灯塔守护人——他们孤独地住在岩石上，决定着千艘船只的生死，社会既离不开他们又遗忘他们。现代数字基础设施其实在重演前工业时代的"关键个体经济学"：

  • 灯塔守护人：维系航运 → 偶尔有船长写信致谢，政府偶尔补贴
  • FFmpeg 维护者：维系全球流媒体 → 偶尔有用户发 Reddit 帖，公司从不付费
  • lib XML 维护者：维系全网数据交换 → 收到的是安全研究员的指责

这里有个出人意料的反转：那张著名的"现代数字基础设施依赖一个小方块"漫画，最初是讽刺脆弱性，但 JB 的故事告诉我们它其实是在描述一种古老的劳动结构——人类文明从未真正解决"关键基础设施由少数人无偿维持"这个问题，我们只是把它从物理世界搬进了 GitHub。

隐藏的系统性真相：死亡威胁、白色粉末与"PowerPC 永远"——身份政治的最早实验场

最让我震撼的是 JB 收到死亡威胁的细节：2009 年，因为停止维护一个过时的 CPU 架构。这不是普通的愤怒用户，这是一种对"技术身份"的部落主义反应——用户把自己使用的硬件当作身份认同，把维护者的技术决策当作对自我的攻击。

这里有一个被忽视的系统性洞见：开源社区其实在 21 世纪 00 年代就已经预演了今天社交媒体上所有的极化现象——身份认同绑架、匿名死亡威胁、把技术问题道德化、把维护者人格化攻击。我们今天讨论的"网络毒性"研究者们，应该回去研究 2009 年的 VLC 邮件列表。开源世界是社交毒性的"早期采用者"，只是没人把它当作社会学样本来研究。

而 JB 那句"我现在很高兴这件事发生在那时候，它锻造了我"——这其实揭示了一个残酷的筛选机制：今天还在维护核心开源项目的人，不是技术最强的，而是心理护甲最厚的。这意味着开源生态的人才选拔标准，早已从"代码能力"悄悄漂移到了"创伤耐受力"。这才是真正令人不安的事——我们的数字文明依赖于一群被仇恨筛选出来的幸存者。

📝 转录文本

维护者倦怠：开源世界最棘手的难题

What you are referring to is one of the most challenging and most interesting part of open source today is maintainers burnout, right? And AI is a problem because of that.

And Daniel Steinberg, which is the maintainer of curl, who's probably one of the best promoter of open source in the world — he's by the way a member of the European open source academy with me, so I'm very humbled to be on the same communities as him, right? He's against what he calls "AI slop", right? Because it gives a ton of fake reports or bad reports, bad patches, and then a lot of maintainers have a lot of burden to maintain the software.

And this is straining the mind of open-source developers much more than forks. And for example, the XZ fiasco was because there was one guy maintaining it and he got basically hammered by two attackants who were asking him questions non-stop at weird times at night to block him. And at some point he got fed up and said, "Okay, I can't do that," and gave the commit access to the attackant. So burnout in open source community is something that exists, but mostly it's about maintaining things, right?

No, for sure. I wonder how do we help that? Because those people are so important — the human beings are so important, the core of these projects.

So for example, now I am maintaining a ton of multimedia and non-multimedia library as maintainer because the maintainers got fed up, right? Some on VideoLAN, some outside of VideoLAN — because sometimes you need a tough skin, right? Because you get like, it's not really attacks, but "oh this is not working, this is not working" and you feel it personally.

And this is also why resources, or the Google fiasco, was a problem, right? They don't realize that in the end — you know, it's like the same graph where you see everything and it's just one random open source project that is maintaining the whole thing. You see the one right there?

Yeah. This is the meme. I mean, it applies to a lot of projects, but this is —

The all modern digital multimedia infrastructure, and then that thing at the very bottom that everything relies on, is FFmpeg. It's true. And then there's usually a handful of folks that are maintaining that.

一人撑起整个互联网：那些隐形的核心维护者

And FFmpeg or VLC, right — you have a community of 10, 15 core developers. Are not the worst. Open source project XZ, which is even in more installations, is one person, right? There is one guy. lib XML is —

Yeah, lib XML, right? There was a big stop. No one is maintaining XML anymore — which is like, basically the only library that is able to parse XML everywhere.

All the crazy edge cases of XML under ridiculous circumstances, and they're attacked by security researchers because there's one other crazy edge case that they haven't thought of. And yeah, but the body of knowledge to actually resolve that is massive. There is one guy maintaining all the time zones for everyone, who is in the middle of — I think was it Nebraska or South Dakota? Like, the mental health of the open source maintainers is something that large corporations don't care or don't see, right? It's just like, "oh yeah, I'm just doing an open source report" and so on.

Some of it is financial — and people should definitely support open source financially all across the board — but some of it is also like spiritual, on a basic human level. There's something that happens, like with this image of FFmpeg and so much of the internet depending on it, where people almost talk down to the folks who are carrying these projects forward and maintaining —

In the security community, they certainly did. That was one of the things I think that argument came out: there was a portion of the security community who's like, "No, these guys write crap code. They need to fix their crap code." I'm like, "No, no, no, no. This is a guy's hobby project. You have a security bot that's gone and found some AI generated stuff. That guy didn't write crap code. It's just an edge case to the 99.9999 percentile he didn't think about because it's his hobby project decoding Star Wars games."

I get the hobby project aspect of it. It's just hard work and it's beautiful, and the right approach there is to celebrate people for doing incredible, incredible work. It's just incredible that humans step up.

Sure.

Not getting really paid at first, or maybe ever, and then doing it out of the love of it. And we need to — like human civilization runs on people like that. We need to celebrate that.

收到死亡威胁的那一年：PowerPC 与白色粉末

To give you an idea, I received death threats on VideoLAN, right? And —
