This happened more back in the day, but you still observe it: when the projects do actually announce a launch on Binance, or Binance announces it, there's a price increase. I think Binance is viewed as almost a distribution channel for the tokens, because it accesses this large customer base and it brings attention to the project in addition to what has existed before.

Oftentimes what ends up happening is the price discovery process and organic volumes — you can count on them being around more for larger tier-one exchanges, whereas for the lower tier exchanges it's tougher. And we also know that wash trading is kind of an endemic problem in the industry. So often times with smaller exchanges, I think people really question whether the volumes are there. I don't know that it helps us calibrate necessarily a price other than directionally favorable, but you can sort of trust what you see more in the data, and you generally have an expectation that things normalize to a steady state that's a bit more reliable on some of the bigger exchanges.

四、商业模式：自营 vs 服务

Corey: I want to talk about the two large business models here, and you referenced it in an earlier answer that there's a distinction between market making for profit, where you've got this open competition — edge comes from a variety of places including speed and understanding order book imbalance and better pricing and forecasting flow — and then market making as a service, where you're getting paid by the project. And I think, to quote you here, "the goal is to not lose your edge in being paid by the service," right? And I was hoping you could unpack this distinction and maybe how it changes how you actually market make operationally, how you think about implementing your market making structures.

自营交易 vs 做市即服务

John: Market making for profit — ultimately, you kind of have a choice of whether to participate and how to participate. I wouldn't even think about it as market making for profit, more like trading for profit. So you could trade either by being a market maker, by being an opportunistic market maker, by being a market taker, or by arbing between different markets. There's a richness of different strategies that you can apply. Almost all of it relies on there being organic trading involved. If you're a taker, you need liquidity there, but the only way the liquidity is there is if there's organic trading.

Market making as a service — I imagine what firms are trying to do is to bootstrap the marketplace. So this is not unlike Uber, where initially they gave very good incentives for people to participate and to ride. So people come for one thing but then they stay for another. That's what these token projects are trying to do. They're trying to create enough organic interest so that people stay and continue holding on to the token, trading the token.

All the problems that we talked about exist. We have KPIs. So we have certain uptime KPIs where we have to be in these markets. We have size APIs where we have to quote some minimum amount of size at various spreads. All of it is meant to give active takers the ability to trade when they want to trade at significant size. And we have to manage this across a lot of different markets, potentially depending on how many exchanges these tokens launch on — and the bigger tokens will launch on multiple exchanges. And then we have to manage inventory risk across these various exchanges. Those are some of the challenges that we face. And all the meanwhile, oftentimes we're compensated with options. We actually have embedded delta to these tokens because of the options that we're trying to manage in a responsible way. These are some of the challenges that we wouldn't have just trading for profit.

留存模式 vs 期权贷款模式

Corey: Let's talk about the business model a little bit. As far as I understand it, there's sort of two big ways in which this business works. There's a structure where the listing project actually takes P&L risk within some bounds, and then there's another where the market maker receives a loan plus an option to buy the token — which you just sort of referenced, and you can, I guess, maybe think about that as a covered call to a certain way. How do the models differ in terms of how you think about quoting, hedging, and ultimately managing your long-term relationship with the project? And are there certain conditions under which you'll accept one of those models and not another?

John: In the early days, I think those are the two structures that normalized. So the loan-option model is more popular and prevalent with bigger projects, and what we call the retainer model is for smaller projects. But before that, I think during the wild days, there were more bespoke deals that actually targeted certain price targets, which, you know, often sort of set quite weird incentives — because market makers shouldn't be able to influence price targets per se, but they were certainly incentivized based on hitting certain price targets. Those sorts of structures are perhaps what make certain TradFi folks quite uncomfortable with crypto.

Delta 对冲的逻辑

But to your point, the way we do it at least — and I think people do approach this differently — there's a few ways in which you can look at the option. One way is that it's basically a VC bet, and you're paying for the option through labor rather than buying it outright from the token project. The way we look at it is that the options enable us to provide liquidity through delta hedging.

Delta hedging — you're basically selling into the market as it goes up and then you're buying as it goes down. So that fundamentally dampens the volatility of the project, which is good, because I think the projects don't realize that what can go up quite quickly could come down quite quickly too. And it gives us a way in which we can deal with the large inventory fluctuations — because otherwise you could really take on quite a bit of inventory without meaning to, and that presents a lot of risk in this model.

Because of the delta hedging nature and the ability for you to manage the inventory using the option, the risk of market making sits with the market maker.

留存模式下的风险归属

In the retainer model, what instead happens is that the risk sits with the project. You would do your market making. You would tell the project what the P&L is, and they would ask you, "Hey, why did you lose so much money? What happened here?" But fundamentally, the market maker takes on no risk and is paid the service fee directly. So it might be something like, I don't know, $2,000–$5,000 a month depending on the number of exchanges and the number of pairs.

A way of approaching that that we sometimes do is we'll just tell the project, "Hey, what we'll do is synthetically hedge." Basically, we'll act as if we had an option. We'll layer liquidity according to that. Basically, that gives you a lot more certainty around what the cost will be depending on the price path. But you can give a more reasonable bound in that structure because the project takes on the P&L risk. There's sort of a lot more degrees of freedom that people apply. The problem with it is it does become a client management question and a relationship management question, where you have to give the project some sense of what might happen, why things are happening the way they are.

五、库存风险与组合管理

库存校准

Corey: Can you talk to me a little bit more about the inventory risk issues? One of the things that sort of naively comes to mind for me here is, again, going back to sort of the two different types of projects that might come to market — one that's very hyped, in which there's a large airdrop that you might expect initially a lot of sellers that you're going to have to absorb, and then the other projects that maybe are less hyped that aren't going to have as many initial sellers, that maybe it's a more balanced initial market, and you might need more inventory at the end of the day. I presume you don't want to just be left holding a lot of inventory. So calibrating that initial amount of inventory to supply and demand seems like a difficult problem, but an important one for the sustainability of the business. Can you talk a little bit about that?

John: One of the things you can do — you're not operating in a vacuum. You sort of understand a bit of the tokenomics of the project. You understand how much supply might be coming online. You sort of understand the thinking of the people, right? Because basically a lot of the people who participate do it on a semi-professional basis in terms of farming the airdrops and whatnot. So you know what they're likely going to do, and you're absolutely right — oftentimes what happens is that there's initial selling pressure and then things normalize.

So for us, there's sort of really limited things that we can do in this dynamic. And I think what it is is about thinking about how you deploy your inventory to be most effective. Do you step in, or do you let it fall and then step in? What we've noticed is that it's not path independent. It's not like the same amount of trading has the same price impact. It's not the case that if you deploy the same amount of inventory, regardless of how quickly or at what price, it results in the same buffering impact. You can deploy the inventory in a smarter way.

In the way that we delta hedge our option, though, initially what we need to do is sell the delta — the embedded delta of the option. We would be sellers along with the other sellers, and we make this quite transparent, but we just sort of space it out in time and we make sure to minimize the market impact to which we can. A lot of this is thinking about building up buffer reserves so that later on, when the prices do drop, we can buy it back, and then similarly if the prices rise, we can continue selling into that price rise.

组合层面的风险

Corey: I want to talk a little bit about maybe the aggregate portfolio construction angle here, because right now we're talking about market making for a single project in isolation, whether it's trading for profit or as a service. You at Caladan simultaneously market make on, I believe, 200-plus assets — different crypto tokens that are going to have various correlation structures and tail risks and these wildly fat-tailed distributions. How do you think about aggregate inventory exposure? How do you decompose what's idiosyncratic to a given token versus systematic? And, you know, again, with all the way these correlation regimes shift and liquidity cascades and the way that DEXes and centralized exchanges are organized, you've got a unique problem on your hand. So how does your risk management adapt to all of this?

John: That's a pretty complex problem, and it's one that we think about a lot and continue to work on.

One thing that you mentioned is that correlation shifts in time, and I think we see that a lot. Oftentimes correlation is a liquidity phenomenon. So if you look at BTC, it's become more correlated with general traditional asset markets, and often that's because the same holders of risk make the same decisions of risk on / risk off. And so BTC becomes more correlated with equity markets as BTC becomes more intertwined with the traditional financial markets.

What we've seen though is really that for small tokens or new tokens, almost all the risk is idiosyncratic, because it's sort of dominated by all these dynamics that we've just been discussing — day one or maybe day 10 dynamics that's still playing out. There's not so much impact of what the overall market is doing, but also we hold usually smaller inventories of these.

务实的风险框架

We just kind of took a more pragmatic approach, which is that we essentially have two risks that we look at, and this will evolve in time. We don't think about factor risks — the crypto factors are still quite new and we can't really decompose them so well — but we think about an overall market risk that we control, and then we think about an idiosyncratic risk.

What we do when we sort of manage our overall portfolio is we skew our pricing differently for different coins depending on how correlated to the market it is. And this correlation is not a numerically estimated measure. It's more of a kind of assigned measure. So I don't know — some of your listeners might be familiar with Barra factors. And Barra factors are derived in such a way where the loadings on certain factors are actually assigned and they back out the factor themselves. So it's a little bit like that.

For bigger tokens like BTC and ETH, there's going to be a lot more, I would say, market-based skew or overall skewing of the price from portfolio-level exposures. Whereas for the smaller tokens, it's almost all of it from their own inventory, and we just have tighter bounds on those. So it's a very pragmatic approach, and it kind of works because our trading goes in and out so quickly. If we were holding positions for much longer, I think you would need to have more sophisticated management of these risk factors. But for us, we're able to turn things around quickly, so generally the risk is pretty tight.

六、对手盘与场所风险

关于 Spoofing

Corey: Historically, there has been a perception, and I think it's probably fairly accurate, that spoofing is endemic in crypto markets. I don't know how true that is today, but for listeners who are coming from traditional markets, this is explicitly illegal. In crypto, it's probably reasonably illegal still, but people have been able to get away with it because it's largely unregulated and difficult to pin down. How does the prevalence of spoofing change the way that you read the order book and think about adverse selection when you're making markets?

John: So I actually started doing a bit of high frequency trading in traditional financial markets before spoofing became explicitly illegal. This was the global financial crisis, right, with Dodd-Frank, that became explicitly illegal. Then people actually prosecuted it. I think the SEC and the FBI prosecuted it under wire fraud statutes. So I think those same statutes might apply to crypto as well, but as you said, people do it anyways.

What's interesting was the spoofing was really targeted toward high frequency trading strategies because they tended to react very quickly. It's quite sophisticated as well. I actually, on a personal note, worked in close proximity to someone who was one of the biggest spoofers — so traded 10–20% of the ES market, one of the most liquid markets in the world, and was able to successfully manipulate that for profit.

And what's really interesting is there's sort of degrees of sophistication where you do this on a primary market where you don't lose much money, where you break even, but you derive the profits from another market that's influenced by it. So an example might be that you trade in ES, the E-minis, but you make your money on SPY and all the other stocks that move with it, because there's effectively a large set of derivative markets — let's call them derivative or influence markets — that takes a signal from a primary market.

And you see a lot of this in crypto as well, right? Where a lot of these economic hacks are because someone references a smaller market and then so much liquidity builds there and people can manipulate the small market and then make profits on the big market. You see that in crypto. You see both the very basic manipulation, which is somewhat easy to filter out, but also the more advanced manipulation. But you also have all of these other problems like insider trading and very tightly controlled supply, and all that.

All of which is to say that we're sort of operating in a very adversarial environment. And this is where the table selection becomes very important. You want to choose the tokens and the venues that have good edge. And there's a lot of different filters and market analysis that we can do which identify these markets. So we pick our — outside of market making as a service which we're being paid to do — for our actual prop trading, we sort of pick our markets very carefully.

FTX 之后的场所与对手盘

Corey: I want to talk a little bit about venues. I think what we saw a couple years back with the rise and subsequent collapse of FTX was a pretty seismic shift in the way people view venues within crypto. All of a sudden this idea of counterparty risk went from maybe theoretical to existential overnight. Curious how the market making infrastructure view of venues, credit relationships, and the ideas of maintaining capital efficiency across venues has been rebuilt and rethought since then. And I'm particularly curious because one of the things that evolved out of the FTX demise was the rise of perp DEXes and Hyperliquid especially, which emulates so much of what a centralized exchange is but is now doing everything fairly transparently on chain. And there's some meaningful trade-offs there in the way that those exchanges are managed with their centralized vaults and ADL being such an important component. So maybe you can talk a little bit about how the landscape has shifted and maybe impacts it's had on your business.

John: So FTX did impact us, but not nearly to the degree that I think it impacted some of the other firms in the space at the time. We had maybe about 5% of our equity on FTX, but I do remember frantically pulling money out of other exchanges just to make sure that there weren't any sort of systemic issues lurking. And I think exactly right, risk became top of mind for a lot of people.

The way I conceptualize it is this is exactly like Adaptive Markets Hypothesis. Collectively, attention shifted from one thing to another. It became much more fashionable and defensible to talk about risk, whereas before maybe you were seen as being the naysayer.

What then happened was a push towards, I think, certain infrastructure being built that can manage that risk. One of the big firms that came out of that was Hidden Road. So they underwrote insurance and counterparty risk. So that became an interesting product for a lot of people. You have Copper and BitGo now offering essentially credit intermediation services. You can post your collateral or trading funds with them, and they'll reflect it onto certain exchanges with whom they have partnerships.

And then the third thing I think we saw is probably more bilateral trading relationships develop. The way I think about crypto is it's quite similar to FX or commodities in market structure. You have a lot of bilateral trading relationships that's established where people can vet each other on a bilateral basis. There's a lot more transparency than with centralized exchanges like Binance, but also you did have more transparency with even those — for example, their reserves being published publicly.

Those are the developments. And as you also mentioned, with perp DEXes, essentially this idea of using smart contracts to hold customer funds and customer assets as opposed to it being a centralized entity into which you have more limited visibility. The FTX fiasco created a lot of these opportunities for new structures and new companies to be formed.

杠杆瀑布与 ADL 风险

Corey: One of the interesting things with — and this is probably true with the centralized exchanges, but you certainly see it with the decentralized exchanges and the decentralized perps that have come out over the last couple of years — is that because you have maybe I'll call it venue sprawl, where there's so many venues that you can trade across, you don't have what you have in traditional finance where you have one centralized, arguably protected, venue where there's a waterfall of ways in which customer assets are protected. And if you're trading something like E-minis, the likelihood of your position being cut against you during a significant market sell-off is very limited. The client comes first.

With all these venues needing to protect their own solvency, they have a waterfall of ways in which they can try to manage this leverage cascade risk. But when you mix lack of centralized clearing plus a huge amount of leverage in crypto, it seems like at least once a year there's a massive liquidation wipeout, and you have these auto-deleveraging effects that then have knock-on wipeout effects for many traders who are maybe trading long-short and trying to hedge their positions and losing one leg of their trade. Curious how you think about that risk, because it is very different on a venue-by-venue basis. They all operate differently and it's very different than traditional finance.

John: I think with any delta-neutral trade — something like a funding rate or anything where you're carrying a long and short position across different exchanges — this becomes quite risky. There's no easy answers here. I think it's one of the reasons why you're paid a premium, right? You're carrying this risk.

There are ways in which you can kind of manage on an individual exchange basis. It depends on your strategy. So one thing is that these ADL risks are proportional to your return. So the more money you make, the more likely you're to get liquidated ADL. I think they do this to give you some sense that you're okay — at least you made money. What you can do is when you've detected that you've made a lot of money on a particular position, you can just trade out of it back in. That's obviously expensive, but it is a way to manage the risk.

Other things are more, I think, at the strategy level, where you just have to look at: well, how big is your notional position in this particular thing relative to open interest? Can you spread that open interest around a bit more? All of it is more trying to limit the impact should it happen. There's unfortunately no straightforward answers here.

七、DeFi：另一个世界的流动性

Corey: With the rise of perp DEXes, I feel like traditional DeFi has been maybe a little bit forgotten in the narrative, but it is something that Caladan actively participates in. Not only do you participate in market making in centralized exchanges, but traditional DeFi pools as well, where you're providing liquidity against these automated market making curves, and you have to manage things like impermanent loss, and you're deploying capital into smart contracts with different trust assumptions and audit needs. It's really a fundamentally different exercise than looking at a centralized limit order book. As I mentioned, you operate across both simultaneously. Can you talk about the structural differences between the centralized approach versus the true DeFi liquidity pools, not just in execution, but how you think about risk and capital allocation and the nature of maybe edge itself?

智能合约风险

John: The way I would think about decentralized venues is: one, there is a counterparty risk, but the counterparty risk is really against the smart contract. So then there's a question: well, how secure is a smart contract? Is there an exploit? That's a very tough question, right? You have audit firms that specialize in this and they still miss it. So our thinking is it's hard for us to effectively evaluate that better than other people can — people who are specialized. So we don't even try. We just rely on existing audits if it's new. Really you rely on the battle-hardening of some of these protocols where they have been hardened over many years. There have been exploits, they've been fixed, and so forth.

AMM 的进化

The other feature of DeFi venues is that it's very expensive to do transactions. That's actually why the Uniswap — their constant product automated market maker — was such an innovation. It was because you could do it actually cost effectively, and it was cost effective from a gas perspective, but it was very inefficient from a capital perspective.

So you started with Uniswap v2, where you had to put in a lot more capital to allow a certain amount of trading with a certain amount of market impact, but it was very capital inefficient. Then Uniswap v3 came out, which introduced this range-based liquidity. But if you think about that in its limit, that's just like a limit order, but then you needed more active management. So you sort of see this transition path back in effect into an order book of some sort.

CeFi-DeFi 套利

For us though, all of these inefficiencies essentially create some opportunity. One that's very well exploited is this CeFi-DeFi arb. Essentially, oftentimes centralized exchanges or limit order book exchanges are where price formation happens, and then the DeFi side lags. That creates an arbitrage opportunity in which you can push those prices back into line. That's when you're on the taking side. You can also selectively be liquidity providers into these pools and then also make money, but you're almost always hedging your position into the CeFi exchanges as well.

It's very much a trade-off between how much fees you earn versus how much adverse selection you experience from the arbs. So, for example, if you have a pool where the only people who trade are the arbs, then there's no way you're making money because they're only trading when they can overcome the fees. But if there's enough noise traders where they're going back and forth and they're paying the fees, then that can wash out where you're actually still making money. Those are some of the challenges. Risk management is entirely separate because the smart contract holds your custody — that's just are you in or out, and then if you're in, how much.

链的"性格"

And then the last thing I would say is from an infrastructure investment perspective, DeFi is quite different because, again, the high costs are such that everything's batched together. And because now there's real economic consequence, the structure of DeFi markets are such that — depends on the protocol — but for Ethereum, there's an auction-like process whereby you essentially bribe the block producers to put your transactions first. So you're often paying a lot of your profits there.

And then for Solana, what's interesting is it's so cheap that people just spam it. The way in which you gain an edge is to just kind of spam the chain with a bunch of transactions, and you hope one of them gets through. So it creates a lot of very interesting incentives for these profit-motivated players. But it is quite different from the traditional game of high frequency. Same ideas, but different implementation.

八、给项目方的建议与代币的反身性

代币是什么？

Corey: In your experience with the breadth and depth of experience you have working with a number of projects to bring new tokens to market, what would your advice be to those who are doing their initial listing and looking to maximize the long-term health of the token?

John: If you take a step back and think about what the token is, depends a bit on what the project founders are going for. Projects — I think the token is a product. Everything else is kind of a marketing support for that token. And in that case, you know, maybe this discussion is irrelevant.

But another way to view it is it's a mechanism by which you're rewarding participants in the ecosystem with economic value that's being generated. So the key is that there needs to be economic value. And the token also has other benefits like coordinating behavior that otherwise might be harder to do, et cetera, et cetera. But the underlying fundamental is that there's real economic value and that the token reflects that.

So oftentimes I think people focus a bit too much on the token itself versus building something that has sustainable value. So the main thing I would say is make sure your project actually has real value and real revenue and real use, and that's what ultimately will drive kind of the token. I think nowadays people are also a bit more sensitive to the actual mechanism and the rights given to the token. Before it was like a "trust me" kind of thing — that's also starting to mature a bit. Nevertheless, I don't think there's a substitute in the long term for the project itself actually being valuable and the token participating in that value.

反身性的特殊性

Corey: Let me push on that a little because I think in crypto especially, the token is the product and the product is the token. There should be ideally a business behind it, and we see that in some cases where they're using revenue to do programmatic buybacks. So there is sort of a fundamental cash flow behind the token. But you see this reflexive relationship that when a token isn't doing well, it seems to almost have a negative marketing impact on the project, which drives revenue lower, right?

So if the token price doesn't do well — and you don't necessarily see that in traditional markets, it's like, well, Nike stock going down doesn't really impact me buying Nike shoes — but for whatever reason in crypto, the price of the token going down seems to impact people's use of certain protocols. So I would argue potentially the price health of a token actually is, for whatever reason in the state of crypto right now, important to the sustainability of the project. Curious your thoughts on that.

John: I think so. I think the token does serve a marketing function. So there is a reflexivity element. But if you look at, for example, Hyperliquid, there's sort of real cash flow. Their actual sort of launch — it was interesting. It did go quite well, but they started quite low, for example, like $3 or $2, and now it's at 40. It's not about going out with a bang.

I hear everything that you're saying. I think reflexivity is not just a thing about crypto markets. If we look, for example, at the WallStreetBets stocks like AMC or GameStop, they were able to raise capital off the back of those pumps, and that really gave them kind of a lifeline to continue doing other wacky stuff like buying mines and such. Or if you think about how stock price may or may not influence employees who have stock option grants, it does have maybe not as dramatic, but it does have real economic impact on the company and people's motivations and things like that.

So that reflexivity is there. And one of the critiques of, I think, crypto is oftentimes the token seems superfluous — just to have one. And for the health of the project, it actually detracts people's attention, the team's attention, to manage now this effectively thing that trades, which is a very different problem often from working on the project and what the project's meant to do.

That speculative option embedded into the token is also what allows a lot of these projects to kind of raise capital. For example, when we evaluate equity-based investment versus token-based investment, the token-based investment have much more near-term liquidity. And there's sort of a sense — at least it used to be — that even if the project fails, there's a kind of a speculative value that remains in the token once it has captured critical mass. You look at certain projects like ETC — who knows where it is. BSV — it's still trading. It's still very non-trivial sort of market caps, but there's not really a team or anyone working on them anymore. It's just become its own little game that persists.

九、新业务：OTC 结构化产品与资产管理

进入结构化产品

Corey: You've recently extended your services to include OTC structured products. You're building out bespoke derivatives, forwards, options, structured notes for token foundations, funds, and family offices. Curious how you came into this line of service and how you're thinking about sort of the transition or differences between your primary line of business, which is market making as a service, to this new line of business.

John: The first one would be they're quite different. I see a few areas of overlap.

One area of overlap is perhaps the hedging capability. So having access to linear capability where you have access to a very wide breadth of places to hedge is a comparative advantage, because you can write the products, you can design the products — and a lot of this follows sort of TradFi design, right? So it's nothing new — but to actually be able to manage the risk afterwards is quite tough because the liquidity within crypto is just much less than a lot of traditional financial markets. So one aspect of it is leveraging the existing market making infrastructure, prop trading infrastructure, to do the hedging.

The other one is, as we pointed out, there's no central clearing in crypto. So a lot of it's based on evaluation of counterparty risk as well as trust. So we've kind of survived through all the ups and downs and blowups, and we've done it with just our own capital, our own balance sheet, never with investor capital. There is some, I think, credibility on our part that we can leverage to be counterparties to a lot of these clients who are looking to benefit from structured products.

And then the third one is we're in touch with a lot of these foundations who can be customers for them. And because we already have that working professional relationship, it's a way into essentially offering them more services from which they can benefit.

代币基金会的财库管理

Corey: Well, let's pause on the client side here for a bit. I suspect again for a lot of my traditional finance listeners, this is all going to be a little bit novel, and I want to create the context. If you're a token foundation, you might be sitting on hundreds of millions of dollars worth of your own token, but it's going to be illiquid, maybe locked up, obviously highly concentrated. Your ability to monetize it and use that capital is limited because people are watching what you're doing with it and/or you might just blow through whatever liquidity pool or order book there is, right, trying to actually convert it into some other asset you can spend. What does treasury management actually look like for these foundations? And maybe you can talk about the core problems they're trying to solve and use this capital for, and how given those problems, the types of structures they're coming to you to request that you're building out for them.

John: So the first thing I'll say is that treasuries are quite a diverse group. There are some treasuries that have been around for a long time and most of their assets are actually maybe in BTC or ETH, and they just haven't done anything with it. There are some other treasuries that have hired very, very professional people from traditional finance and are quite knowledgeable about option structures and the limits they're in. It's a very diverse group with different needs.

And the way that I think about structured products is that you have these Lego pieces. The Lego pieces themselves are quite technical, but you don't really talk to the treasuries about that. You talk to them about their needs and what their constraints are. You essentially use that to synthesize certain payoff structures or ideas to satisfy their needs.

So what are some of these needs? I think yield generation — we hold a lot of assets. They tend to be in crypto, not just their native tokens, but other crypto. And these typically don't have a way to earn assets. So that's one. Another need is, "Hey, we have a runway. We have these operational costs. How do we have a program where we can finance these operational costs or sell our tokens to cover these operational costs without spooking the market?"

信号问题

The needs are quite diverse, and that's where customer education comes in. And that's why we have a team that goes and speaks with these treasuries and understands their needs and then comes up with something custom.

I will say that the concern, the signaling concern that you mention, is very real. I think it would be like if Elon Musk was like, "Oh, I'm selling all my Tesla." Everyone would freak out. So you have the same concerns in TradFi. It's just much more concentrated in crypto. And I think there is room — like in TradFi, there's solutions where you pre-commit. You're not trying to time the market. You pre-commit to a certain program. People understand that. So in crypto, we think there's probably a way to do that as well, where you can kind of satisfy your needs without necessarily spooking the market into, in terms of thinking that you have insider information or you've lost faith or stuff like that.

Some of the structures that we create does help to shield the treasury from having that negative signal to the market. What we're actually doing is monetizing volatility, monetizing convexity, and trading counter-flow to where the market naturally trades. That also helps a bit.

十、关于做市商的误解

Corey: One of the things I've noticed about many of the retail crypto participants is that there's generally this negative view about market makers for whatever reason — that the market makers are just destroying price or playing some nefarious role or that there's backdoor deals. What do you think the biggest misperceptions are? How would you clear the record as to the role of what market makers are really doing in these markets?

真相与虚像

John: That's hard to do because I think there's maybe some truth to some of those perceptions. Because market makers as a group is a diverse group with different actors that have different choices that they make. And as I mentioned, there are even now these active market makers that very much try to paint a picture.

Have you ever read the book Reminiscences of a Stock Operator?

Corey: Absolutely.

John: It paints a story about how you can kind of manipulate markets. It's kind of like pump and dumps in penny stocks, right? And that happens still in crypto. Crypto projects do engage with these active market makers. The attraction of a high token price is very appealing. So all of that does happen, and it's unfortunate that it does happen, but it's there.

When we think about market makers, perhaps the key is not to use such an overloaded term to describe what it is that people do. And that's why we've always chosen to kind of stay away from, I think, these more manipulative or deceptive kind of practices. It's just not really in our DNA, given where we came from.

因果还是相关？

Corey: Yeah, I think I've seen some pictures floating around on Twitter where participants have labeled different key market makers and what the historical price action has looked like for them. You know, when you say, "Oh, this new token is being run by this market maker, that means the price action is going to look like this or XYZ." It's a little tongue-in-cheek to your point. Maybe there is some historical precedent for it — does look like that.

John: There's a difference between cause and effect there too, right? It's sort of like these tutoring services that claim 99% of their people they've tutored made it into Ivy Leagues. Very likely they just chose the people who would have made it anyways.

I think a lot of the initial price action isn't really within the control of the market makers. They can create certain things in the short term, but it's hard to sort of maintain that without a set of conditions being true. One of which is a very tightly controlled spot inventory.

永续合约与现货的"尾巴摇狗"

What's interesting is we actually see that when we trade, because one of the things we do is help people hedge. And one of the very different things about crypto is this idea of a funding rate and perpetuals that don't really exist in TradFi. And the funding rates can just behave really, really strangely. It's super difficult to hedge. It's a place where we've spent quite a bit of time doing research, but it sort of has that flavor of a tail wagging the dog.

So imagine you have a very small spot inventory, maybe even just a few million dollars, but you have an open interest that's like an order of magnitude higher than that. If you manipulate spot inventory, you can affect a lot of economic outcomes for the holders of the perpetuals. So it creates a really interesting kind of market dynamic there that can be manipulated if someone chose to do so. These are the things that make it a bit interesting, but also difficult I think for TradFi players to navigate the crypto markets.

十一、未来展望：加密走向何方？

Corey: Well, from the outside, it does appear that crypto in many ways is sort of speedrunning a lot of the lessons and product development that TradFi has had over the decades and centuries. I'm certainly not the first to have stated that. Curious where you see the market today and where you see it heading, particularly as TradFi institutional participation deepens in these markets.

John: I think a lot of this is credit to the roadmap that TradFi already laid out. If you look at the structures and the things that have been built, it's pretty faithfully replicating a lot of what already exists in TradFi.

I think it has a couple of advantages. One is that it's like a nice regulatory arb, right? You sort of have a Hyperliquid where they can sort of say, "Well, we're not custodying any customer assets." That really does make things faster. And then the other thing of course is you have kind of programmatic money and these relationships that are codified, so you can see them play out without any circuit breakers, right? In a much more raw way. So you can get a lot more learnings from them.

类比：外汇与大宗

But the way I think about crypto — I have a couple of theories. One is that it'll just kind of replicate forex and commodities markets. You have a lot of the same challenges, right? You have essentially these little regional markets that are there for regulatory and other structural reasons that are isolated. You have essentially a lack of central clearing. You have the need to actually settle something. It's not like oil where you have to deliver from one place to another, but it's not quite just risk that you're settling — it's actual physical things that you're settling.

I sort of see it really developing in that direction, where it's a highly, highly distributed, highly fragmented market where you have a lot of different bilateral relationships. And so there's a very central role for a market maker — not in the market making as a service sense, but in the trading principal risk sense — to balance flows across these different markets.

DeFi 的两条路

And then you also have DeFi, which is yet another form of fragmentation and takes its own form of management. The interesting thing about DeFi that I'm not sure about is people have already started talking about permissioning in DeFi. In which case then it's more maybe just a ledger and settlement. It's a technological innovation but fundamentally will be integrated into the traditional financial system.

There's also sort of the non-permission side, which maybe then it becomes this thing that will always exist but is a little bit more nichey and will have ultimately less capital. It'll still be there in its little sandbox, but not to the degree that it's there today.

So that's kind of how I think about the system evolving, but we'll see. That's the closest analog I can get.

十二、收尾问题：你最近在着迷什么？

Corey: All right, John. I always like to end these conversations with a bit of a personal question. And the question I've been asking all my guests lately is: what is something — and this has to be outside of work — that you're currently obsessing over? Could be an idea, a book, an activity, a hobby, music — something that just currently has you completely captivated.

小狗与 AI

John: My kids are advocating for a puppy. So we've been talking about a puppy — how you have a puppy in Singapore where the rooms are quite small.

But also, I've been obsessing a bit over AI, because a lot of people use it for search and it's great for that. But I think you can fundamentally create different workflows for yourself that is ultimately a new way of approaching things.

So I'll give an example: I was talking to a friend and he said what he did was to actually feed in a lot of personal information and preferences into AI to almost build a virtual version of himself, to kind of talk to himself about how he would approach certain decisions. I can also think about feeding in — I'm a collector of mental models. So I really like this idea of feeding in a few guiding mental models and saying, you know, "Hey, what's going on?"

I think it will really change the nature of work and what we define as work very, very quickly. Thinking about: are we even teaching our kids the right things? Like, is it going to be valuable? Like a lot of those questions now sort of sit with me.

变化的洪流

Have you ever watched Interview with the Vampire, the movie?

Corey: I have not.

John: So in there, there's this scene where basically there's this leader of a coven of vampires that have been around for hundreds or thousands of years. And what he says is that one thing that people really struggle with is keeping up with change and the changing of times. And I feel like change is so fast these days, and it's very hard to anticipate what things might be like. It's quite disruptive because you plan for one thing and you spend years planning for one thing and it could change. So I don't envy my kids. Basically, they're going into a world where they'll have to struggle with a lot.

Corey: And, you know, it's something I think about a lot too. And I actually feel like AI has been something that a lot of guests have answered with lately, but everyone with their own unique particular obsession about AI — but certainly those with kids. For all of us with kids, it's a question of: kids that are currently in high school or middle school are going to have a very different relationship with this problem than kids who are toddlers right now, who might just grow up immersed in it, and the world will have already changed before they're aware of it. So life is moving quickly.

John: It is. Yeah.

Corey: Well, John, thank you so much for joining me. I really enjoyed the conversation, and thank you again for taking the time.

John: Thank you for your time as well.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的洞察：做市商的"亏损"其实是一种被精心设计的"负载平衡费"
大多数听众会下意识地以为做市商靠"低买高卖"赚钱。但 John 在访谈中揭示了一个被忽略的真相——**为新代币做市几乎总是亏钱的生意**（"it's almost always a money losing proposition"）。项目方必须用代币期权或现金留存费来补偿这种结构性亏损。
这意味着代币做市本质上不是"交易业务"，而是一种**类似云服务的 SLA 合约**：项目方购买的不是流动性本身，而是"在指定的延迟、深度、点差下，随时可用的可成交性"。把它放进系统视角，做市商更像是为代币市场提供"99.99% uptime"的 AWS——你付的是基础设施账单，而不是商品价格。这彻底颠覆了零售用户"做市商在收割我"的叙事：在 cold start 阶段，**是项目方在补贴你能够卖出**。
## 二、跨领域连接：Andrew Lo 的"适应性市场假说"竟然是 FTX 倒闭的最佳预测模型
John 在 Alpha Simplex 时被 Andrew Lo 的 Adaptive Markets Hypo

item.title: [Lex Clips] H.264 codec vs MP4 container: Difference explained | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个让你意外的观察：编解码器世界其实是一场"信任崩塌"的哲学实验

你以为这是一个关于压缩算法的技术故事，但听完之后你会发现：VLC 之所以能征服世界，不是因为它"播得更好"，而是因为它"不信任输入"。这是一个反直觉的洞见——在软件工程中，我们通常追求"输入合法、契约严格"，但视频播放器领域的赢家恰恰是那个从一开始就假设"文件是坏的、容器在撒谎、扩展名是骗局"的产品。

这其实和现代零信任安全架构（Zero Trust）的诞生逻辑惊人一致——VLC 在 90 年代末因为 UDP 流的丢包问题而被迫"不信任输入"，二十年后整个云安全行业才把同样的哲学奉为金科玉律。换句话说，盗版党在拨号年代下载半截 AVI 文件的"半成品播放需求"，意外训练出了一种早于时代的工程伦理。

跨领域连接：编解码器 ≈ 语言学中的"音位/音素"，容器 ≈ 书写系统

MP4 和 H.264 的混淆不是偶然的命名事故，它揭示了一个更深的结构问题："承载形式" 和 "信息内核" 在人类所有的信息系统里都会被混为一谈。

  • 在语言学里，人们经常把"字母"（书写系统/容器）和"音素"（声音/编码）搞混——英语里的 "ough" 在不同词里发完全不同的音。
  • 在金融里，人们把"美元"（容器：法币符号）和"购买力"（编码：实际价值）混为一谈。
  • 在 MP4/H.264 里，容器只是一个"我承诺里面有东西"的多路复用承诺，而真正决定你看到什么的，是那个无人提及的内核。

更妙的是：MPEG-4 本身是个"元规范"——它是一个包含多个 codec 的容器化规范。所以容器里装着 codec，而 codec 的规范本身又是一个容器。这是递归的命名混乱，类似分形——每放大一层都重复同样的结构性误解。

隐藏的系统性真相：压缩的本质不是数学，而是"对人类感知的赌博"

表面上，codec 是数学问题——熵编码、运动估计、变换、量化。但仔细听 JB 的描述你会发现一个被刻意低估的真相：所有现代编解码器的"压缩 1000 倍"，本质是在赌"你不会注意到"。

这意味着 codec 工程师是伪装成数学家的认知心理学家。他们在做的事情和广告设计师、魔术师、UI 动效设计师本质上同源——研究人类注意力的盲区，然后在盲区里偷东西。

而 AV1/VVC "不是一个 codec 而是一组工具集"这件事更惊人——它意味着编解码器正在变成"内容感知的自适应有机体"。屏幕共享时它一种人格，看电影时另一种人格，看动画时第三种人格。这其实和大语言模型的 Mixture-of-Experts 架构在哲学上完全同构——MoE 也是"一堆专家工具根据输入路由"。所以下一代视频 codec 和下一代 LLM 在架构哲学上正在收敛。

最后一个让你坐直的预测：当 codec 已经演化为"工具集合 + 输入感知 + 路由策略"时，它离一个专用神经网络其实只剩半步之遥。未来 10 年里，AV2 或 AV3 很可能不再是"算法 + 标准"，而是"权重 + 推理引擎"——视频压缩将不再写在规范文档里，而是冻结在模型权重里。届时，"开源 codec" 这个概念本身都要被重新定义。

📝 转录文本

容器与编解码器：常被混淆的两件事

Lex： We mentioned codecs, but I don't think you mentioned containers. So what, what's the actual containers for some of the stuff we're talking about? So, people are familiar with MP4, uh, MKV. So anyway, what what are containers versus, uh, the thing that goes inside?

JB： So the container is what we call also the muxer, right? When I say demuxing, it means de-containerizing, right? So actually if you look, "mux" means multiplexer and demultiplexer, right? Mux and demux are those. And same — codec is actually coder-decoder, right?

Um, and containers are this collection of multiple tracks, right? So it's what normal people call the file format, but it's a bit more, um, subtle than that. But the most known one of course is MP4, but uh, when I started it was AVI, right, as the the video format from from, uh, Microsoft. And, uh, MOV — which became MP4 — was a format from Apple. Um, in the open source community, one of the persons that is still active on VideoLAN is called Steve Lhomme, and started this Matroska format, which is like a bit more complex and and more feature-proof. Um, and there are so many others.

MP4 ≠ H.264：行业命名的"原罪"

JB： So I mean, it's a pretty common thing — and maybe it'll even happen in this conversation — that people confuse container and the codec, right? So confuse MP4 and H.264, for example.

Lex： Is that a horrible violation?

JB： No, it's not. Because technically the name of H.264 is MPEG-4 Part 10, because MPEG-4 is actually a meta-specification which has several things in it, right? There is the Part 2, uh, so there is, like, audio codecs, right — AAC is the factory, is "MP4 audio something." There is actually several video codecs, right, inside the MPEG-4 specification. One of them is MPEG-4 Part 10, called also AVC, called also H.264, right? So it's completely the fault of the industry to to to to make things difficult to understand. So that's very difficult, so that people then don't understand why sometimes you talk about MPEG-4 Part 10 where you mean H.264 and why it's not MP4.

Lex： So you can technically shove in all kinds of different codecs inside containers, and horribly so. But broadly speaking though, MP4 is understood to generally be H.264 plus AAC audio. 99% of the time that's that, and the rest are de minimis — the small effects, you know, edge effects really compared to that. So it's not the end of the world that there are people who do get annoyed by that.

第三方： But also in reality something like VLC, just to point out, the file may say .mp4 but it may be something completely different. And that's one of the challenges both FFmpeg and VLC have — is the real world is a completely different place to a three-letter file format.

不信任输入：VLC 的工程哲学

JB： And this is very important to say, right? Like for example, in VLC and FFmpeg, we discard the file format, right? We look into the file to understand what's in it. Because so many people, like, they say "Oh, it's a video, it must be MP4," but technically it's an MOV, or maybe it's an MKV, right? So we analyze in real time everything that we have, and we don't trust, uh, the format.

Lex： So what information does the fact that it's MP4 give you?

JB： It helps, right? It gives you a hint, right? Just like, "oh, it it's finished by MP4, I will start first by opening — probing it with the MP4 container demuxer to see, well, it should be that, but I don't trust it." And if I'm lost, I say, okay, maybe I'm going to try to — so it bumps the priority of the module.

Lex： So how do you get to — uh, just to take a bit of a tangent there, you know, the dumb thing is, if you try MP4 but it turns out it's a different codec than you would have expected, uh, most players just break there.

JB： Yes.

Lex： Yes. So how do you not break? There's just a philosophically — I'm sure there's a bunch of stumbling blocks along the way where you — it's easy to just break and stop, freak out. That's it. How does VLC not? This is why VLC is popular.

JB： Um, but the reason is because actually VLC was — is just a client of a streaming solution called VideoLAN from from from very long time ago, from the late '90s. And when you're playing video which are on UDP, right, in network, they might be damaged, right? So you don't trust your inputs. And this is very important in today's security, is that you don't trust your inputs. So everything in VLC is prepared to, um, work with broken files. Mhm. And it's a philosophical idea from the beginning, and everything is engineered into that, and it's a culture, right?

盗版时代意外塑造的"破损文件容忍度"

JB： And so for example, VLC became very popular on that, because a long time ago when people were pirating content, um, which they do a lot less today, um —

Lex： And none of us ever have.

JB： No, of course not. Um, the metadata to play some files like AVI is at the end of the file, right? And when you're downloading, you don't have that, right? So VLC was just like, "Hey, this file is broken, but I'm still going to try to interpret it." And this was very useful.

什么是视频编解码器？

Lex： We hinted at the awesomeness of the various different stages. We hinted at the awesomeness of codecs, the depth and the richness and the complexity of everything involved there. What — let's try to define: what is a video codec? What what's involved there? What what does it mean to compress something? You already started to hint at it, but can can we elaborate a little bit more?

第三方： So there's a huge amount of redundancy in any video, uh, both spatial and temporal. And the point of any video codec is to remove this redundant data, use mathematical properties as part of this reduction process. So more often than not, using several orders of magnitude more compute to compress, because that's more costly — versus both costly both financially and in CPU resources — versus the decompression. So it's asymmetric in that respect.

Lex： Often the case, because compression is done once but there could be lots of viewers of another file.

第三方： So to take that information and compress it by 100x, 200x — removing redundant information and using mathematical properties to make that small, but also have properties such as error resilience. So as JB suggested, VLC in the beginning was was used to play UDP network feeds, and UDP network feeds lose packets. And so some of the design goals of a codec is also to be recoverable.

JB： You need to actually be able to join a stream. It's not necessarily a file. You need to join — get on the decoding process and start decoding.

时空冗余：电影里那朵不动的云

JB： And to give a more image to to to to people who are not familiar, right? Like when you're going to see any type of movie, right, you're going to see the camera is going to pan, right, and and and travel, and you realize that for example all the background is the same from for like a minute, right, or 30 seconds, right. So you can reuse the cloud that you see, uh, on the background — you can reuse that from a frame to another, right? And so, the more the more memory you have, the more power, the more comparisons you can make, right, and so the more compressed you can be. And most of the modern codecs are basically doing that.

Lex： So just to make it even more explicit: so what is video? Video is a bunch of pixels — off an RGB have three values, and you have a grid of pixels, and you have let's say 24 or 30 or 60 frames a second, and you just have all these pixels repeating and showing different stuff 30 times a second. And so the question, the philosophical, the technical question is: how can I compress all of that, store all of that, at 100x —

JB： Or 1,000x, right?

Lex： 1,000x.

JB： The target is 1,000x, right?

冗余到底是什么？

Lex： And the goal is — when you say redundancy, what is redundant? Meaning stuff at best that humans wouldn't notice if it was missing. So for example, you have a picture of a cloud, right? And from the next frame there's still going to be the same cloud. So it's redundant. You could just put it once and not do it, right? Or you have a, a [clears throat] black background behind me for example — the black is the same on the whole picture, right? So you can say, well, you know, in this picture, take the pixels that you have on the top left and the one on the top right, I'm not going to give the value, I'm just going to tell you it's the same at the top left. And then you can say for frame one, um, reuse something from the previous frame or the previous previous frame, and so on and so on, right? So you could basically — it's unlimited.

JB： But then it's limited in terms of memory or in terms of compute power. Because for example if you need to compare pixels on 200 frames in the past on 4K resolutions, it's a huge amount of compute. And then when you're showing it, you have to do the decompress of all of that.

编码 vs 解码：不对称的权衡

Lex： So is it the codec — the has the encoding and the decoding is a coupled process that you're developing?

JB： Exactly, right. And those are two different, um, uh, trade-offs, right. Are you going to compress more, uh, but then it might be more difficult to to to decode? Um, are you going to make it a codec that is more complex to encode and easier to decode? Are you going to make a codec that is easier to encode because you need to be fast, but then the client side, the player is going to spend more time? That's why you have so many different types of codecs — is that it's not always easy.

现代编解码器：不是 codec，而是一套工具集

JB： And to make it even more complex, modern codecs like AV1, AV2 or VVC are actually not codecs — they are a collection of tools, right? There are multiple tools, multiple codecs in the same codec, to depending on the image get the more compression.

第三方： So just to elaborate, codecs like AV1, VVC have a much wide — have a wide audience. It could be a screen share content, it could be video, it could be animation. All of these require different coding tools. So what happens these days is a collection of tools are put in and called AV1, called AV2, called VVC, to allow for different use cases. So you may be on Zoom and sharing your PowerPoint, and then you need to show the audience a video — that codec needs to start changing its tool set depending on the content to compress in a different way.

Lex： And like you said, there's a bunch of incredible engineers behind each part of that — each part of the tools that make up AV1, for example.

第三方： Sure.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个让你意外的观察：编解码器世界其实是一场"信任崩塌"的哲学实验
你以为这是一个关于压缩算法的技术故事，但听完之后你会发现：**VLC 之所以能征服世界，不是因为它"播得更好"，而是因为它"不信任输入"**。这是一个反直觉的洞见——在软件工程中，我们通常追求"输入合法、契约严格"，但视频播放器领域的赢家恰恰是那个从一开始就假设"文件是坏的、容器在撒谎、扩展名是骗局"的产品。
这其实和**现代零信任安全架构（Zero Trust）的诞生逻辑惊人一致**——VLC 在 90 年代末因为 UDP 流的丢包问题而被迫"不信任输入"，二十年后整个云安全行业才把同样的哲学奉为金科玉律。换句话说，**盗版党在拨号年代下载半截 AVI 文件的"半成品播放需求"，意外训练出了一种早于时代的工程伦理**。
## 跨领域连接：编解码器 ≈ 语言学中的"音位/音素"，容器 ≈ 书写系统
MP4 和 H.264 的混淆不是偶然的命名事故，它揭示了一个更深的结构问题：**"承载形式" 和 "信息内核" 在人类所有的信息系统里都会被混为一谈**。

item.title: [Zhang Xiaojun Podcast] 140. 对姚顺宇的4小时访谈：请允许我小疯一下！在Anthropic和Gemini训模型、技术预测、英雄主义已过去
item.dc:creator: Zhang Xiaojun Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：Anthropic 的"独裁"为何成了它的护城河

在 2026 年这个所有人都在谈 bottom-up 创新的时代，姚顺宇给出了一个极其反直觉的观察：Anthropic 是三大模型公司里最 top-down 的，而这恰恰是它能 make bet 的原因。

更妙的是，他指出了一个被几乎所有人忽视的微观结构性差异：Anthropic 的技术决策者同时是公司的"口风的"（co-founder 级别的 owner）。Jared Kaplan 和 Sam 既懂技术又持有公司，所以他们说"我们就赌 coding"的时候，没有任何中层敢于、也没有任何中层有理由稀释这个决策。OpenAI 在 Ilya 离开后丧失了这个结构；Google 永远不可能拥有这个结构——因为 Google 的技术领袖不是 Larry Page。

这是一个跨领域的洞察：这其实是"创始人控股的家族企业 vs. 职业经理人公司"在 AI 时代的重演。Anthropic 像一家德式家族企业（Mittelstand），而 OpenAI 越来越像通用电气——技术决策权和股权早已分离，于是出现了 Sam Altman 必须不断政治化以维持权威的局面。

二、隐藏的范式联系：Scaling Law 的"撞墙"其实是科学方法论的失败，不是物理极限

姚顺宇丢出了一个让人脊背发凉的判断：大多数觉得 pre-training scaling law 撞墙的人，撞的不是物理之墙，而是他们自己 bug 之墙。

这个观察的真正杀伤力在于跨领域的类比——他用做物理实验的 ablation 思维来诊断 AI 研究的方法论问题。在量子物理研究中（他本科那段被人忽视的工作），他正是因为发现"理论和数值对不上"才挖出了 Block wave 假设的破绽。他用同样的方法论指控整个 AI 圈：你们没撞墙，你们只是没做干净的 ablation。

这里有一个更深的悖论：AI 时代之所以"个人英雄主义已过去"，恰恰是因为它从"科学发现期"进入了"系统工程期"——但与此同时，能否突破撞墙感的关键，仍然取决于个别研究员是否具备 18 世纪热力学家那种"理论与实验不分家"的科学品味。这是一个典型的"组织上需要集体主义，认知上需要个人科学品味"的张力。

三、最出人意料的连接：程序员的命运 = 18 世纪手工艺人，而产品经理意外地成了"最后的人类堡垒"

这一段是整篇访谈最颠覆性的：AI 首先取代的，是人类智力分配最高的那 5% 做的工作（数学、代码、科研）；而最难被取代的，反而是产品经理这种"没有刻度的工作"。

姚顺宇本人也没有完全展开这个洞察的深层含义，但顺着他的逻辑往下推：人类历史上第一次出现了"反向技术替代"——过去的工业革命都是从体力劳动、低技能劳动开始替代；这次 AI 革命却是先吃掉智力金字塔的顶端。

更跨领域的连接是：这其实在重演 19 世纪英国手工艺行会的命运。那个时代最精湛的织布工人、钟表匠、印刷工——被工业革命第一波吃掉的，正是那些被同行尊敬、需要十几年学徒的"高技能匠人"；而那些"卖艺不卖身"的杂耍艺人、酒馆侍者、街头小贩反而活得更久。因为可被标准化定义的复杂，比模糊的简单更容易被机器吃掉。

姚顺宇说"程序员未来可能只剩千分之一"，本质上是在说：reward signal 越清晰的工作，越早死。这是 AI 时代的"反熵增定律"——清晰是危险的，模糊是护城河。这也解释了为什么 Anthropic 卖给了"美国效率软件"，而豆包这种"中国式间接盈利"反而是 Meta 看不懂的——因为后者的 reward signal 复杂到 AI 训不了。

一个意外的元观察

姚顺宇这个人本身就是他所描述的现象的活样本：他厌恶老灯、推崇 reliable、不相信个人英雄主义、不读人生之书——他几乎是在给自己做 self-ablation。一个理论物理学家进入 AI 领域后，最大的转变不是技能而是"不再相信不可证伪的话语"。这某种意义上是物理学家版的"破除巫魅"——而当他说"AI 不需要脑子，需要靠谱"的时候，他其实在说：这个行业终于变成了一个可以用工程理性而非天才崇拜来推进的领域。这本身，可能就是 AI 进入"下半场"最准确的定义。

📝 转录文本

对姚顺宇的4小时访谈：请允许我小疯一下！

频道：Zhang Xiaojun Podcast
链接：https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Gk_KUg3qED0

开场：两个姚顺宇

大家好,我是小俊。AI 业界有两个姚舜宇,一个从 OpenAI 跳槽到了腾讯,一个从 Anthropic 跳槽到了 Google DeepMind。前一位姚舜宇去年来过我们节目,那今年我们邀请了另外一位。

AI's essence is simple. It's also a form of enhanced learning. Don't be superstitious. The era of AI personal heroism has passed. The most important characteristic of AI entrepreneurs is reliable, etc.

我们的节目录制于 2026 年 3 月。在我们录完这期节目之后,世界上发生了很多意想不到的变化。比如 Meta 撤销了对 Manus 的收购、Cursor 可能被 SpaceX 收购、XAI 将停止独立运营进入 SpaceX 并更名为 SpaceX AI,等等。如果我们的对话内容有一定的滞后性,请大家多多包容。因为 AI 的世界真的变化得太快、太出乎意料。

可能还要特别说明的一点是,技术细节会涉及到一些企业机密,有一些是嘉宾不方便分享的,所以也请大家能够多多包容。我们在最大可能的范围内,和大家一起来学习 AI。接下来就是我对顺宇的访谈。期待 2026 年我们和 AI 共同进步。

一、Anthropic 的 top-down 与个体英雄主义的消逝

安插配作为一个公司来说,它能够实现这种 top-down 的机制,是一件独特的事情。其他模型公司难做吗?是的。比如 OpenAI 做不到,Gemini 也很难。它和创业公司不一样,因为创业公司重要的是要 make a bet,要赌点什么。

我觉得现在大家基本都是冲浪的人,基本是浪在冲,而不是人在冲。

AI 不需要脑子?真的不太需要脑子。需要什么?我觉得这个行业最重要的特质就是靠谱、做事细、然后对自己做的事负责任,这是最重要的特质。

二、自我介绍与两个姚顺宇的区别

小俊：硅谷不是有两个姚舜宇吗?你要不要先给大家介绍一下你自己,然后给大家科普一下两个姚舜宇的区别?

顺宇：可以。我叫姚舜宇,显然也有一个跟我几乎同名的朋友,我们俩主要履历也有一些 overlap,所以说可能看起来非常的难以区分。

我以前是学物理的,本科的时候在清华,做的是 theoretical theory。后来去 Stanford 做 theoretical high-energy physics,以及相关的量子力学和黑洞。从 Stanford 出来后去了 Berkeley,只待了两个星期。之后离开去了 Anthropic,待了一年。去年九月底十月初我加入了 Gemini。

如果一定要做区分,我觉得最大的区别是:顺宇一直在做 CS,跟计算机相关。而我从某种意义上来说是一个半路出家的,以前主业是做理论物理的。

小俊：你们是好朋友吗?在大学认识、同一届对吧?他是个什么样的人?你又是什么样的人?

顺宇:我们是同一届,在清华同年级,但他一开始就是 computer 的学生,所以他是数学班的,我是物理班的。后来他去了 Princeton,我去了 Stanford——这是另一个让人有点困惑的点。在 push 的世界里,我觉得 Stanford 是学计算机的人去的地方,Princeton 是学物理的人去的地方。但我们俩反着走的,所以可能造成了一些麻烦。

我们其实也没那么不同。我觉得他比我有意思,我以前从他身上学到很多。比如他花了很多时间思考 AI,思考人与 AI 的交互,包括一些产品相关的事。我觉得他是一个跟我非常不同的朋友,我从他身上学到了很多。

小俊:你们在硅谷见面频繁吗?现在还经常打电话吗?

顺宇:我们在硅谷的时候确实见面挺频繁的,可能每几个星期吧,但好像见面主要是为了凑一块玩——纯玩,出去散散步,聊聊有的没的,有时候吃个饭打个牌之类的。他回去之后我们也还是经常打电话。最近一次电话好像是前一两个星期。

小俊:他是不是多次想把你拉过去?

顺宇:可能有这个意思吧,但我觉得不关键。我没去任何公司,主要是因为去年八九月份我从 Anthropic 离开,最大的动机就是想学不同的东西。我没考虑去 lead 一个项目,更关注的是学习,所以选了 Gemini。

三、AI 的"下半场":能力被拉平之后

小俊:你们俩老被放一起讨论,对你来说是困扰更多还是享受更多?

顺宇:我没什么感觉。我这个人也不太关注社交媒体,所以真的没什么感觉。

小俊:顺宇之前说 AI 进入了 the second half,进入下半场。你觉得今天的 AI 在一个什么样的时期?

顺宇:对我来说,我不知道什么叫上半场、什么叫下半场。对我来说,AI 现在进入了一个阶段:大家不再担心 AI 能不能做某件事,而是担心这件事是不是被良好定义。

一年前我在 Anthropic 的时候,人们还在担心 OpenAI 为什么这么强,我们有没有机会追上、有多大机会超越。现在至少在 Gemini、OpenAI、Anthropic 三家中任何一家,都不会真的担心追不上了。

现在对大家更难的事是想明白要去做什么。这是一个 bet、一个赌,但也是一个非常需要 insight 的事情。

模型同质化:从纸面到体感

小俊:这是不是意味着模型能力被拉平、商品化了?

顺宇:从用户实际体验来说,三家模型还是能感受出区别的。但难点在于,从纸面上看不出区别。

一年前,数学方面比较简单的像 AIME,难一点的像 AMO,你从纸面上就能看出这个模型 reasoning 强一点、那个模型 coding 强一点。现在纸面上大家都比较相近。比如看 SWE-Bench,好的会比不好的高一两个百分点,但大家都在 80% 附近——这个差异主要是 noise(噪声),不是 signal。

但从使用上确实还是能体现出区别的。据我所知,Cloud 还是最好的 tool-based agent;纯 coding 方面 Codex 最近比较激进,两者差距变小了。

小俊:这些能力是它们有意选择优先发展某个方向,还是单纯的好坏?

顺宇:有意愿的成分在,尤其在过去主要是意愿。因为 Cloud 一直重视工具使用包括 coding,OpenAI 关注 coding 是最近一段时间的事。当你看好一个方向,就能花更多精力构建合适的 infrastructure、合适的 data——尤其是数据,从某种意义上说,是非常耗时间、耗精力的事。

现在两边差不多了,因为纸面上看着差不多,内部测试数字也差不太多。更难的事是:怎么定义你的问题、定义你想要的行为。当问题没定义清楚的时候,模型之间的差异是你无法想象的。

出乎意料的例子:GitHub 的自然抬升

顺宇:举一个出乎意料的例子。如果你回到一两年甚至三年前,看这些 pre-training 数据,你会发现模型写代码会很好。那时候我们也不知道为什么。但意外的原因是:如果你不做任何数据选择,自然分布下 code 数据的质量就比其他的高——因为 GitHub 的质量显著高于其他正常互联网。

有趣的是,这件事在业外的讨论好像比业内更激烈。业内有人讨论,但对于业内来说,这并不是特别令人惊讶的事。

四、OpenCloud、Manus 与"壳"的命运

小俊:OpenCloud 这个产品意味着什么?

顺宇:技术上其实并不能说明什么。OpenCloud 依赖的模型能力,其实并不是到今年年初才准备好。可能在去年 Opus 4.5 发布的时候,Opus 在 tool use 能力上就已经比 OpenAI 和 Gemini 3 都要强一些。在那个时间点其实就可以做这件事了。

它一开始发布也没立刻火,过了一段时间才火起来。所以技术上不是什么令人惊讶的事——它是模型能力的自然溢出。

但它给大家展示了一种可能性——意识到你可以让模型做很多事。

Manus 跟 OpenCloud 的区别,我其实不太理解。说实话,可能是 OpenCloud 火了,但如果你问我 Manus 为什么做不到,我不知道——可能就是他没做到。

小俊:不管 Manus 还是 OpenCloud,都选择了把自己卖了——Manus 卖给 Meta,OpenCloud 卖给 OpenAI。这说明什么?

顺宇:我自己的感受是,一个东西要想长久生存,还是要考虑壁垒的。目前来说,很多壁垒都在模型侧。但未来会不会产生产品侧的壁垒?说不定。

大家老生常谈的所谓"数据飞轮",目前我觉得没有哪个场景真正形成了数据飞轮。甚至 AI 纯粹原生的应用场景,目前除了 agentic coding(写代码)之外,没有哪个场景是 AI 真正原生且变得非常成功的。

因为从某种意义上来说,Chatbot 其实是搜索的延伸。它最大的需求是"我有个问题,问 Chatbot"——这其实就是搜搜做的事。它带来的是比搜搜强得多的东西:很交互,能帮你总结信息、压缩信息。

壳的两条活路

顺宇:壳要在目前这个情况下活下来,我能想到两种方式:

第一种是跑得足够快。在模型公司反应过来之前先拿到大量用户;在模型公司想吃你之前自己开发模型。Cursor 在走这条路。

Cursor 在 AI 原生应用里,几乎是我能想到的创业公司里增长最快的。即使这样的公司,现在也很有危机感。

我的感觉是,对 Cursor 来说,现在和 Anthropic 已经进入了一个非常微妙的关系。曾经他们是亲密无间的伙伴,Anthropic 给自己的产品命名,后来 Anthropic 自己的 Cloud Code 变得非常成功;现在 Cursor 也在尝试自己训模型(composer)。所以他们现在已经进入了竞争关系。

如果竞争失败,我觉得比较麻烦——因为 coding 的事情本质上是一个面向 professional 用户的效率工具,效率工具是一个 winner takes all 的事。这是不管 Cursor 还是 Anthropic 或任何 coding 公司最担心的。

第二种是市场足够小,小到模型公司懒得管。Midjourney 就是这个例子——市场小到张门岚努力做的话也能做,但小到不值得花时间。

小俊:所以哪怕是 Cursor 今天也没逃出模型的收涨性,有谁成功逃逸了吗?

顺宇:大的目前还没看到,小的可能没证明。Larva(拉发的)算吗?我觉得他们有机会。他们有机会做的不是通用场景。

这是 founder 自己要决定的事:你要不要抱着万分之一的生存几率去赌一票大的,还是抱着百分之一的几率先吃另一个小的事情。如果是我,内心肯定想吃一票大的,但真诚地想,我觉得第一步不能一步登天。所以我会先吃一个小的——但会选择一个有想象空间的小的。

为什么巨头自己做不出 Manus 和 OpenCloud?

顺宇:Meta 买 Manus 这件事,最大的用处(抛掉花了多少钱)是获得了一批很好的亚洲产品团队。中国的 AI 人才储备很丰富,虽然纯技术上中国 AI 还没追上美国,但显然有很多好人才,产品上中国人才比美国更强。

为什么 Manus、OpenCloud 不是大公司的 research 团队做出来的?

我觉得一个公司一旦做大,负担也跟着大。作为 researcher,我可以做一个看起来有意思、有很多 feature 的东西,但一旦做成 public 产品,就要承担很多责任。你不能让所有用户买台电脑去跑——它会把用户电脑的算力都吃光、把系统搞坏。Google 不可能这样推产品。

你还要花时间打磨产品、确认法律风险、确保不损害用户和品牌,放出去后还要给它比较固定的资源去 serve 这个模型或产品线。所以对大公司来说有挺多负担,但对个人来说无所谓——我反正是个开源项目,带麻烦又如何?

五、2026 年的预期:有限上下文用作无限

小俊:你对 2026 年怎么思考和预期?

顺宇:我觉得有太多可能性。从模型能力上,我觉得今年是有机会实现"用有限上下文训练、当作无限上下文使用"的。这件事实现之后会解锁很多新应用——比如让模型跟你持续交互、持续获得你的信息,在运行过程中根据当前场景把它觉得不重要的信息扔掉,从而成为大家梦想中的个人助手。

现在更多处于"哪个想法是最后的想法"的探索阶段,而不是"大家没有想法"的状态。

模型进步速度:完全没有放缓

小俊:站在 2026 年 Q1,你觉得模型进步速度在放缓吗?

顺宇:我觉得完全没有,完全没有。

如果你拿某个 benchmark 看每月增长,这肯定会放缓,因为定义上最高就到 100%,越接近肯定越慢。但这不代表用户体验上模型能力增长在变慢:50% 到 60% 可能感觉好了一点;70% 到 75% 反而可能比 50%→60% 提升更显著;但 80%→90% 之后用户可能就感觉不到区别甚至变差。

我的标准是基于一个研究员的个人感觉:我个人感觉模型学习东西的能力越来越强了。

Pre-training 没有撞墙

顺宇:Pre-training 在过去几个月里其实越来越强了。这从某种意义上是一个有争议的事——几个月前很多人在讨论 pre-training 的 scaling law 是不是已经到头了。我的体验是没有,而且我感觉未来四个月也看不到到头的迹象。

觉得到头有三种可能: 一、觉得规律的适用范围到头了; 二、觉得规律的某个条件不能被满足(比如数据撞墙); 三、他没有发现自己工作中的 bug,所以以为到头了。

从我的角度来看,大多数觉得撞墙的人是因为第三种——bug。

bug 是什么样的 bug?

可能是 scaling law 实验中科学性的假设没做对——选什么 token horizon、每种大小的模型期待的训练数据量、数据从哪选,这些科学选择没选清楚。也可能就是 bug。这在业界并不令人惊讶——很多时候 fix bug 带来的进步远大于一些 amazing 的技巧。

这更像是一个信念问题。当你遇到一个 bug,你觉得它不能解决,那你就觉得这条路到头了;当你觉得这个 bug 肯定可以解决,你就觉得还没到头。

但对我来说更重要的是做事的系统性——当一件事和你预测的不一样的时候,你能不能系统性地排除各种可能性。这是 Anthropic 和 Gemini 都做得比较好的事。

驱动力:算力、数据、算法

算力和数据其实是强关联的:算力上去了自然吸到更多数据;数据上去了自然吸到更多算力。

算法上,有一个阶段是你"不知道怎么做",那个阶段非常关键——卡住不能 scale up。一旦找到关键的算法,可能从"不能"变成"能",之后算法会以平滑的方式提升计算效率或数据使用效率。语言模型 pre-training 在算法上的跳跃就是 Transformer 的发现,之后都是慢慢平滑地提升。

但不可否认,可能有其他方向还需要算法的跳跃。比如多模态生成,目前可能还是一个在算法上没想清楚的事——还是个科学问题。

自然语言生成,在这条技术方案撞到头之前,我觉得不会有跳跃了——它在 science 上是清楚的,但 engineering 上还有很多事可以做。

兴奋的氛围

过去几个月里看 pre-training 和模型能力时,你周围是一种兴奋状态吗?

是的,至少在我能接触到的范围内,在 Anthropic 和 Google 大家想的更多的是:"我们会不断提升 AI 能力,我们很快就会被取代,被取代之后我们该做什么?"——而不是担心"如果模型撞墙了怎么办"。

六、为什么 Coding 跑得最快?

Coding 是过去几个月发展最快的场景。从 Cloud 3.5 new(外界叫 3.6)那一代开始,大概去年十月十一月,coding 就一直在高速发展。

Coding 有两大优势:

  1. Reward signal 容易定义。比如对软件工程任务,我经常需要写一段代码实现某个 feature,这个 feature 要某些输入得到某些输出——这是容易测试的。如果输入输出对了就成功,不对就失败。这是一个例子,Coding 相关有很多 well-defined 的 feedback signal。

  2. 数据有天然基础。GitHub 上汇聚了过去几十年大量优质程序员写的代码,从那些代码出发可以构建非常多的环境。

产品上还有一个原因:Coding 的使用比较单调,不像社交软件或游戏,每个人口味不同、很难满足所有人。好的程序员写代码的风格比较像——简单、干净、结构清晰、适合未来发展、有合理的抽象。所以这件事从产品上让 coding 变得更简单了。

你的代码现在多少是 AI 写的?

小俊:你现在百分之多少的工作会用 Cloud Code 写代码?

顺宇:你问了一个我差点会被开除的问题——Google 不能用 Cloud Code。

保守估计 90% 的代码是模型生成的,不保守估计可能 99% 甚至 100%。

剩下 10% 是它不能写的,而它不能写的部分越来越少了。一年半以前,Cloud 是市场上唯一能写软件工程代码的模型,但还有很多 short coming——比如只关注一个文件、不关注文件间联系、找不到藏得很深的 class 定义。现在这些事越来越少了。

作为 researcher,你的实现 idea 的效率比起一年甚至一年半以前,可能是 20 到 50 倍的加速。模型变得"非常 ridiculous":可以同时开几个 idea,模型能帮你 monitor 实验和结果。

但个人工作时间反而变长了——因为开发速度更快,我想试的越来越多,有越来越多 idea 要尝试。过去某个文件你看不懂,要花几小时去找当初写的人;现在拿着问 Cloud,五秒钟告诉你结果就接着干。所以工作时间反而变长,工作密度也变高了。

Google 已经不是那个 Google 了——不是那个可以养老的 Google,不是 Work-Life Balance 的 Google。在 Gen AI 这个领域,没有谁可以养老。

我一般早上九点起,看邮件;十点左右到公司。如果自己在美国就工作到晚上十、十一点;家人在的话回家早点,但还在家里继续工作。

除了 Coding 之外的影响

除了 coding 之外,AI 已经对很多方向产生了影响,只是那些方向不是大的市场方向。比如基础科学研究——数学、理论物理——很多人开始用 AI 工具。

以前你想试一个 idea,要会写代码、打开编辑器,半天就过去了。现在五分钟代码就完成。Gemini DeepThink 发布之后,很多基础科学研究者把数学推导、数学证明,甚至看文章、归纳事实都交给模型。

除非真的发现一个以前没有的妙理论(比如 AI 产生爱因斯坦级别的理论),那可能会变成万众瞩目。但那个时刻还没到来——影响已经在产生了。

反直觉:AI 先做的是人类觉得最难的事

小俊:AI 为什么先做的是人类觉得最难的那部分工作?

顺宇:这是一个特别好的问题。

在过去的人生阶段里,大家往往觉得最有智力挑战的工作反而是那些比较理性、比较客观的事情——数学、写代码、做 AI 研究、科学研究。越是这些事,AI 越容易做好。因为你一旦想清楚怎么评价,你就知道怎么训练。

但未来可能会有变化:有很多 AI 不容易做、人可能做得更好的事。比如产品经理——做一个好的产品经理,我不太理解怎么训练 AI 去做。

为什么?没有标准、没有刻度。什么叫"一个好的产品"?我想不太明白。它一定是做出来给人用了你才知道它好。那个 feedback 信号很不明确,我不知道该怎么训练 AI 做。

程序员的未来

小俊:程序员什么时候会被彻底取代?

顺宇:这一天会来,但不会是一瞬间——一定是个 meeting 的过程,而且大家已经看到了这个过程,因为有些公司已经开始 hiring。

从某种意义上来说,AI 从一个角度是好事,但从另一个角度是非常不幸的事。AI 是一个非常 centralized 的技术,会让少数人变强,但会让大量人失去其独特价值。对传统软件工程来说,最终结果可能是:现在千分之一的人做完了过去全部的工作,然后获得了百倍的工资。

给程序员的建议?接收新事物——未来程序员很重要的能力是怎么跟 AI 有效协作。比如合理设计实现方案、让方案符合公司未来发展——这些目前还很难让模型理解。但很具体的事(如"实现这个项目,下周五前给我")可能不会存在了。

千分之一的程序员的特征: - 技术上一定很强(技术弱没有理由不被替代); - 能理解自己的工作在大组织中的 adapt 方式; - 规划能力强,能同时做多个大型复杂的事,把小事分给不同 AI。

也许 6 月之后 AI 把这三件最后的事也能做了,那我的答案会变得更悲观。但 6 月之后会发生什么,没人能预测。

七、字节、Doubao 与中国的模型

小俊:字节会让 Google 焦虑吗?

顺宇:其实有可能,但这种焦虑还没传到我这里。可能对 Google 多模态团队有些压力,但我没看到反映成格局变化的迹象。

字节的产品表现和数据上的细节做得很好,字节在多模态生成上一直有很强的优势,但还没让人感到压力大到焦虑。

字节的产品力来自模型还是产品?

我没自己做过,但要让我猜,模型是最大的主导。模型能力的好处来自什么?数据。

五永辉的评价

小俊:你怎么看从 Google 去字节的吴永辉?

顺宇:我之前没跟永辉合作过,所以没有特别客观的评价。但加入 Gemini 之后,我反而看到了永辉更多好的一面——通过看他过去 code、leading 的项目,我觉得他是个非常 senior 的玩家,既有大局观又有很强的技术能力。这是非常难得的。

中美差距与"蒸馏"

小俊:中美模型能力的 gap 在拉大还是缩小?

顺宇:如果做快照看过去一年到一年半的发展趋势,中美 gap 显然在变小。但是否会被完全 merge 甚至被中国超越?这是一个不清楚的问题——这也是中国 AI researcher 和研究机构的机会。

中国在实际算力资源上占很大劣势,但这反而逼出了一些有趣的事——比如中国的模型公司对 distillation(蒸馏)很在行。

最近 Dario 点名了三家公司蒸馏他。

"蒸馏存在"是一个心照不宣的事实,但有不同的"蒸"法,有硬蒸和巧蒸两种选择。

硬蒸:拿一堆 Cloud 生成的 token 强行 SFT 训练。这不太道德,智力上也愚蠢——基本意味着公司不知道自己要做什么,只能 copy paste 让模型纸面数据好看。

巧蒸:比如用其他模型作为我自己数据 chain 的 support;用其他模型作为 evaluator。商业上灰色,但技术上很有趣——某种意义上中国的 lab 可能在多 agent 训练上成了 pioneer。

要我猜:DeepSeek 应该是硬蒸比较少的——它的模型有自己的特点。Kimi 早期可能是硬蒸,后来在慢慢转向巧蒸。字节应该蒸馏比较少——豆包的模型还是比较有特点。

豆包的特点

豆包没有 Gemini 和 Cloud 聪明,但有它的特点:

  1. 豆包的语音生成非常非常强——可能是世界最好。
  2. 生成快——很多模型给你展示思维链,但生活上的琐事我不想看思维链。

小俊:你用豆包吗?

顺宇:我只用过一两次。一是美国用中国模型有一些复杂的事;二是我个人在生活和工作上没有太多有趣的困惑可以问豆包,我生活中更多的是技术困惑,问 Gemini 这种聪明模型最好。

我没有半夜去打豆包情感电台的需求。

但豆包手机我看过,觉得是一个很好的想法,效果做得也不错。技术上是否成熟我不知道——你不希望模型订一张高铁票比高铁票本身还贵。

Apple 我相信对 AI 战略很关心,但 Apple 自己的模型没做好,Siri 一直没真正起来,现在通过和别人合作来做。

八、机器人与世界模型

小俊:中国的机器人在春晚很火。

顺宇:我看过一些表演,也在亚马逊上搜过价格,确实很惊讶居然这么便宜。没买,买了也没什么用。以前脑海里觉得人形机器人(硬件层面)成熟可能要几百万美金,现在便宜很多——说明中国硬件产业有优势。

机器人的模型在目前 non-language 世界中是一个相对大的问题。它更多停留在 Feature Engineering 的 2020 时代——你有给定环境、给定场景,优化这个场景,大家都知道怎么做(做 RL、构建虚拟环境)。但它没有强 flexibility。

在很多 AI 方向,"是否有 flexibility"是一个分水岭——是否一个特定或简单场景能做好,而不是十几年都能解决。语言也一样:在 GPT 之前,你可以训练专门的翻译模型、专门的语义分析模型,但你不能在一个 level 上提升所有能力。Transformer 和 GPT 之后,语言模型跨过了那个阶段。我不觉得机器人到了那个阶段。

机器人现在在 Gemini 也是一条线,大家在尝试用语言模型做基底模型、在上面训练 VLA(Vision-Language-Action)这种东西。他们未来会变得很重要,但目前还没找到自己的路。

不管是机器人还是多模态生成,都还没到 scale up 的那个点。

九、从宁夏到清华:underdog 的少年

小俊:你从小在哪长大?

顺宇:我出生在宁夏一个很小很小的城市,叫大武口——这个城市过去存在是因为一个煤矿。

小俊:你是物理竞赛保送的?

顺宇:我不是物理竞赛保送的。我觉得我小时候挺菜的。读的初中和小学都是无名之辈,初中里"竞赛"根本不是你该考虑的事——那个初中叫上南中学东校,也是一个让人一头雾水的学校。

选择 underdog 的中学

上海高中有所谓"四校"——上海中学、华二、交大附中、复旦附中。当时我能上四校但上不了四校里最好的班,而我特别想搞竞赛(因为以前没搞过)。

小俊:你怎么植入了这个概念?

顺宇:我这个人的个性就是,我总爱做那些我做不好的事。我当时知道有竞赛,但没搞过,所以觉得在去大学前必须搞一搞。但成绩不够进四校里最好的竞赛班。

我找了一个跟我成绩差不多的高中,有它的竞赛版,那个竞赛版用现在的话讲就是 underdog,跟当年的我半斤八两,可以一搞。当时上海还有"推优生"制度——可以在考试前跟学校签约,提前预定那所学校的名额。

我去了那所高中——我不能说当时已经能进四校了,虽然分数够。我没参加中考。但当时我觉得就算我能进,我也要去 underdog 玩。

为什么?

因为"竞赛"是一个让男人激动的感觉。它真的有挑战性,起码在没参加之前,大家给我的印象就是"这件事比你课程内学的更有挑战",做这件事的人真的很强。如果你不做,你就只是史川中最圆滑的那个。

去清华的契机:高三竞赛前去清华理科营暑校,最后一天听说他们在招自主招生,但主要招北京学生。我给招生办老师发了短信问能不能去考。

小俊:他答应了?

顺宇:让我们去考了。

小俊:你哪来这么大胆?你当时觉得他们开后门吗?

顺宇:我没觉得他们开后门,我觉得人家有这个机会,凭什么不给我们?大家都在一条线上竞争。

我从那件事学到的最重要的真理就是要大胆。你不争取永远得不到,你争取了也有可能得不到,但不争取就绝对得不到。

当时忐忑吗?

我已经不记得了,但我满脑子想的都是:现在就得争取,再不争取明天就争取不到。我在听说的当天就赶紧疯狂发短信。

最后,清华 say yes,我们一起考了试。我觉得从那一刻起我就对清华一直有好感——它愿意给每个人一次评估的机会。

出来时觉得考得挺差,因为有一半题没做,但发现别人做得更少,就考上了。后来高考没考到清华的分,但除了清北之外的学校都能上。

小俊:网上为什么都写你是保送?

顺宇:大家没在那几年上过学,很难理解清楚那几年发生了什么。我之前两届还是省一就能保送;我们那届是省一进省队、省队代表去考国家比赛、进了国家集训队才能保送。我进了省队但没进集训队——所以我那届没有保送的名额。

家庭与个性

顺宇:我父母对我不太管,从小就管不动。中国家庭里"跟父母讨论"已经算很好了,我一般是知会一声。父母可能我升学考试都没问过——他们觉得"看不懂别人的事情就不要瞎说"。

小俊:你比较叛逆?

顺宇:如果你管我,你不能说服我,我肯定是要反着干的。如果正好你觉得这事很重要、我也觉得这事很重要,那我肯定要干得比你好。

十、清华、量子物理与"挑战难事"

顺宇:我到了清华读量子物理,做凝聚态理论。为什么选这个专业?阴差阳错。

清华物理系当时有个极客班,虽然在物理系但不限制学生学什么——三分之二的学生不做物理。极客班的传统是鼓励学生尽早进实验室,在科研中学习。

王中(那时候是个很年轻的老师)是我的本科导师。他给了我一些 paper,让我读。凝聚态理论(拓扑绝缘体相关)其实是个很适合本科生上手的方向——它需要的背景知识不多,只要会量子力学、统计力学和固体物理,但很考验对这些知识理解的深度。

那时候我们做的开放量子系统的工作,从某种意义上来说,跟我现在做 AI 是很像的:都是有一个 idea、一个理解,设计一个数值实验来验证你的理解是否正确;在 AI 里则是设计训练 pipeline 来实现你的 idea。

非厄米系统(给镜头前画一下重点)

非厄米系统这件事是这样:量子力学一个基本假设是孤立系统按 Hamiltonian 的演化是 unitary 的,这个 Hamiltonian 是厄米的(转置取共轭等于自己)。

但大多数真实系统不是孤立系统——比如你和我都在跟外界做物质交换。真实系统的有效 Hamiltonian 就不是厄米的了。"非厄米"本质上研究开放量子系统的行为。

我们当时发现了一个困惑的事:研究开放量子系统的拓扑相,理论计算结果跟数值实验差出三四个数量级。后来发现,描述厄米系统的基本方法——所谓 Bloch 波——假设物体本质是线性波。但在非厄米系统下,这个假设其实就 break down 了。

后来没继续做的原因:很难再有 a change to the world 那种贡献。后面再写文章可能能让我更有名、引用更多、找到好教职,但作为科研者就没那么激动了。

博士去做了高能物理

顺宇:博士换了方向去做高能理论,也是回到那个话题:总爱挑战很难的事,有时也会带来不好的结果。

我觉得我的博士对我自己来说成长很大,但对世界来说没产生什么贡献。

高能理论足够难,但它的问题在于不太可被实验验证、没有客观评价标准——理论已经发展到实验完全追不上的阶段。

高能理论的进步靠什么? 1. 数学自洽性:你提出一个框架能跟现有低能标下的理论自洽(如弦论低能能否回到量子场论)。这是科学的。 2. 不科学的因素:没有客观标准时,谁做得好不好,依赖于领域内 old lights 的主观判断。

教训:要做事业,就要做有客观评价标准的事,要做对世界能产生影响的事。

离开学界的契机

博士最后两年开始有这种感觉。后来花时间了解不同方向——一开始更多了解量子计算、量子信息,拿到 postdoc offer 后觉得这更进退维谷。

离开学校后是自己的 career,得给自己找路。当时觉得量子计算和 AI 是两条给年轻人、给小灯机会比较多的路。

博士后做了什么?其实就是理论物理方向,Postdoc 很独立,想干啥干啥。我实际可能到 Berkeley 待了两三个月,但官方来说只待了两个星期就辞职了。

那两个星期啥也没发生,我本来都不准备入职——但 Berkeley 人太好,说"没事,事情定了再说,能来多久来多久"。

十一、为什么选 AI 而不是量子计算?选 Anthropic

小俊:物理学训练对你做 AI 有没有帮助?

顺宇:从工具技能上,物理到 AI 转化非常少。但如果非要说,我觉得主要不是能力,而是性格——物理出身的人可能更愿意问问题、更愿意理解一件事、更愿意系统性地做事。但这不是物理独有的特征——化学、生物、CS 出身的人很多也有。

为什么量子计算和 AI 都被你考虑了,最后选了 AI?

量子计算目前的瓶颈在实验上——不是怎么设计算法,而是怎么实现。我不擅长实验。AI 是有 idea 用 data 验证——data 在 AI 中等价于物理实验中的实验。

AI 像 18 世纪的热力学——那个时代理论和实验不分家,你就是搞物理,自己可以做实验也可以做理论推测。所以从理论物理跨到实验物理的距离比直接跨到 AI 要远。

Anthropic 与"物理人"

顺宇:Anthropic 有很多物理出身的人,尤其是理论物理的。主要是因为关系——Anthropic 创始团队里 3-4 个技术较强的人,其中两个还是技术领导,都是物理出身,招的人也偏物理。

我去 Anthropic 是因为前同事——我的第一个 manager 也做过理论物理。当时 Anthropic 正在大规模做强化学习研究。那是 2024 年 8、9 月,o1 还没发布,大家都还不知道怎么做。

我去之前其实不太了解 Anthropic 怎么训模型——只知道 pre-training 和 post-training 的大致流程,不知道工业级 RL 训练怎么做。

面试怎么准备的?

我自己不知道怎么准备,就把能找到的课自己学了一遍、能做的作业做了一遍,然后手搓了 Andrej Karpathy 那个著名的 Nano GPT(在 Google Colab Notebook 里训练一个小 GPT 模型)。然后就去面了,很快拿到 offer。

第一个方向是大规模强化学习——当时有两个组的 manager 来跟我聊,一个做 evaluation,一个做 RL。我选 RL 是因为它更不明朗。

那时候 Anthropic 整个大团队 Horizon 只有 11 个人。公司总共 700-800 人。

十二、AI 的"科学不科学"与黑盒论

小俊:很多人说这一代 AI 是黑盒。

顺宇:我觉得这世界上所有东西都是黑盒。物理大家觉得很理解,其实也并不是真的——物理从微观演化到宏观本来就是黑盒。AI 也一样,黑盒不黑盒是相对的事。

我们不理解到神经元 surgery 那个 level,除非是在很小的网络里(如 Anthropic 的可解释性团队会做)。但这不意味着完全不理解——比如 Scaling Law 描述了模型在 size 和 data 这两个尺度下如何变得越来越好。

如果 Scaling Law 不算理解的一部分,那我们对这个世界一无所知——这个世界都是黑盒。

Scaling Law 是 scientific law 还是 empirical law?

经验律。但经验律和科学律之间界限是模糊的。比如热力学第一定律、第二定律、Clausius-Clapeyron 方程,被发现时是经验律;随着对微观机理的理解,慢慢变成科学律。Scaling Law 现在很经验,但未来可能变成科学律。

智能涌现?

小俊:能否用科学的表达解释一下"智能涌现"?

顺宇:首先这话就不太科学,所以也没办法用科学的话表达一个不科学的事。

智能涌现对我来说更多是一种主观感觉,而不是客观现象。

当大家说"智能涌现",脑子里想的是:以前的语言模型只能做某一方向(翻译/分析),现在模型好像能做所有事。但这件事更多是技术 limitation,而不是 behavior limitation——我们通过研究找到了如何 large scale training,把所有能力一起提升。我觉得这是更 fundamental 的。

十三、AI 的"简单本质"

顺宇:AI 本质上是简单的——它能做实验。它和物理的根本困难不一样:物理在缺乏实验数据时无法理解高能标的理论。AI 没有这个束缚,我可以做我想做的任何实验,只是需要时间拉算力、准备 infra,没有根本困难。

所以我一直觉得 AI 没有给人一种碰壁的感觉。现在不是大家想不出想法可以试,而是有太多想法要一个个试。

未来 6-12 个月,AI 可能开始自己做实验——这件事其实已经在发生了(AI 帮我们实现想法、加快实验速度)。还没做的是从头到尾完成 AI research:不只是写代码,还要跑实验、分析结果、知道哪里做错、提新假设、设计新代码、跑新实验。这条链下一步可能会变得 complete。

十四、Anthropic 的内部、3.5 new 与 3.7 的诞生

小俊:你加入 Anthropic 时,这家公司给你的印象?

顺宇:执行力非常强。它是一个比较 top-down 的公司,做了决定之后会很拼。员工之间气氛非常好,大家不藏着掖着。如果只做语言模型相关的事,那是一个非常非常好的学习机会——你能接触到模型训练的方方面面,都能找到对应的人问。

Anthropic 的 bet 来自哪?

Cloud 3 发布后 Twitter 上有人讨论"Cloud 3 写代码好像比 GPT-4 强"——那个年代 GPT-4 还是巨无霸,能有件事比 GPT-4 强就很厉害了。所以是试出来的——很快就对市场反馈扑了上去。这也是它执行力非常强的体现。

为什么 Cloud 3 的 coding 比 GPT-4 好? - 是技术原因,但不能说细节。 - 你要让我猜,我觉得是随机试出来的,不是故意选择的结果。

top-down 为什么难复制?

对一个企业来说,能 top-down 是独特的事。难点在于:做技术的决策者必须是公司本身的 owner(decision maker)——这样研究员才信服去做,你也才能为公司负这个责任。

Anthropic 有这个条件——技术上的 leader 就是公司的 owner(Jared Kaplan 和 Sam),他们自己做决定。

Dario 作为 CEO 在他们决策层讨论中起到什么作用,我不知道。我只知道做这件事的 majority of the work 是这两个人完成的。

OpenAI 在 Ilya 时代可能有 top-down,但失去了 Ilya 之后失去了决策能力(Sam 不懂技术)。Gemini 是 bottom-up——大公司可以尽量降低赌的成本,各方面都有储备,跟进。

Anthropic 的 founding team 几乎没人离开,都是 OpenAI 老兵——Scaling Law 论文、GPT-3 论文这些关键 paper 上他们都是 contributor。是真正一起战斗过的人。

3.7 与 Cloud 3.5/3.5 new

Anthropic 其实不太擅长产品命名。外界看到的版本是 3.5(6 月)、3.5(10 月,被外界叫 3.6)、3.7。我去的时候是 3.5 new 之后,主要参与 3.7。

3.5 new 已经验证 Anthropic coding 模型比别人好。我去的时候管理层已知道这事可做且重要,但不知道怎么把它做成。我去研究的就是怎么做成——方法是 large scale RL。

Know-how?

NDA 内容很多。员工不能公开谈未公开的技术。但我觉得最关键的是:把简单的事做得比谁都干净。RL 最简单的算法是 policy gradient,但不意味着它是唯一的——其他算法(如复杂搜索算法)带来的效率提升和 infra 困难,怎么 trade off,这是关键。

为什么 coding 重要?

  1. Coding 是 model research 本身的一部分,做好 coding 可能让 research 效率提升一半,变成 research booster。
  2. Coding 是模型使用工具与环境交互的好抽象——feedback signal 好、数据足够,其他场景找不到这两个特征。

Cursor 那时候还是纯产品公司。我去 Anthropic 之前的一段时间,Cloud 和 Cursor 都处于 underdog 状态。3.5 new 让公众(软件工程圈)感觉到 agent coding 这事可能能做。

3.7 的 post-training:分水岭

3.7 之前 Anthropic 的 post-training 比较小规模、修修补补。3.7 是 post-training 的分水岭——大家意识到 post-training 怎么 scale up:找到合适的环境,环境的反馈足够清晰,本身是强数据源,这样训练就稳定了。

OpenAI 的"草莓"项目当时大家也不知道是什么,但都觉得会带来新范式——后训练强化学习。我去 Anthropic 时大家已经清楚怎么做。OpenAI 和 Anthropic 的具体算法和数据使用方式其实差别很大,但大方向都一样:找清晰客观的 feedback signal,数据本身相对干净,模型可学,做稳定 RL 训练。

3.7 我做了什么?

主要是 RL 在 agent coding 上怎么 scale up,准备各种环境和数据,算法上会遇到什么问题。

小俊:有什么 tips?

顺宇:现在看 tips 是没什么用的事。很多算法设计不是独立的算法——它非常依赖于 infrastructure。

举个例子:RL 中你 sample 出 trace 时跟用 trainer 实际训练时,这两台机器是不同的(算子质量、训练结构等原因)。不同公司不同 lab 这个 difference 不同——你算法可能最大部分就是控制这个、让训练稳定。如果某公司 infra 特别好,这个 difference 不大,他就能在训练上花更多精力。

所以 know-how 没有用。本质上回答这个问题就是在误导——现代 AI 训练是个大系统,你得了解方方面面才能有全局认识:什么事是因为什么变得有用,而不是这件事本身有用。

十五、文化的转变与离开

小俊:Anthropic 文化经历了什么变化?

顺宇:我从 700-800 人时进入,刚踩在小公司的尾巴。3-4 个月后人就多起来了。离开时近 2000 人。

最后那段时间文化经历了一些混沌——外面来了一些跟原文化有冲突的人。原本 Anthropic 文化很简单,像小作坊,大家是朋友,知道对方在做什么,没人推销自己,也没人做无用功——大家被工作逼着,挺多事要处理。

后来我个人不喜欢一些类型:喜欢在 Slack 里讲很多想法的人——ideas are cheap,很多 ideas 大家都知道,难的是怎么实现,把它拆成小步骤做起来。

离开的原因:Dario 反华

离开的一个方面是,我不太认同 Dario 反华这件事。作为公司 CEO 个人立场无所谓,但作为公司 CEO 把这个观点推到这么极端是一个非常情绪化的体现,会影响公司文化。

反华占百分之多少?我公开场合说 40%。不是控股因素,但是 majority holder 的因素。

更大的原因是想学不同的东西。Anthropic 很 focused,做 agentic coding 那块非常好,但很多事不做——比如 Anthropic 完全没人做多模态生成,你想学没地学;Anthropic 在更底层 infra 上花精力也不太多。

为什么去 Google?

小俊:大多数人在 underdog 阶段加入会更有感情,你反而跳去 Google,这个行动好像也反了。

顺宇:取决于你想要什么。

如果想要 clear scope、对最终模型直接影响,Google 不是好地方——研究员和组织多,流程复杂。但如果想要研究自由、探索自由、向更广泛的人学习,我觉得世界上没地方比 Gemini 强。

很多人离开一家去另一家会不舒服——因为不知道自己想要什么。比如去 Google 觉得要为自由学更多,去了之后还想要 product impact,就会很难受。

你不追求 impact?

分阶段。在 Anthropic 经历了太多和产品息息相关的事,我可能想换脑子学不一样的东西。但未来某天可能又换回来想产生产品影响——也可能。

十六、Anthropic 之后的反思:产品的"巧思"

小俊:你离开 Anthropic 时对它的期待?

顺宇:离开时我对这家公司比较悲观——因为离开时 Anthropic 的主要收入是 API。API 是个 bad business——这个生意只对一家公司好,就是 Google,因为生意最后都要打价格战,价格战必须有完整链条。

但后来 Anthropic 在产品方面做了很多巧思——Cloud Code 变得越来越好用,Cloud Code、Cloud Code Work,各种和工作效率相关的事慢慢汇聚起来了。

我以为你会问"OpenAI 和 Anthropic 谁先死",但其实不会死,问的是"谁先变得不那么重要"。

我以为是 Anthropic 先变得不那么重要,但后来先是 OpenAI 被 Google 揍了,然后 Anthropic 顺势而上。现在看 Anthropic 更有优势。

后悔吗?对想做的事来说,这个选择没什么错。

Cloud Code:个人英雄主义的产品

顺宇:Cloud Code 我觉得产品上可能真的还有一些个人英雄主义的机会。

Boris 是这个事的开端——他自己想做这个事提高自己和同事的工作效率,最后变成对所有人都重要的事。

Anthropic 做产品有专门部门吗?
