Claude Code — the person — might really have some opportunities for individual heroism. Boris Cherny. Claude Code almost — at least the beginning of this thing — was him wanting to do this thing himself to improve his own or colleagues' work efficiency. Finally became something important to everyone.

I didn't have too much personal contact with him. He's mainly on the product side. So Anthropic does have a dedicated product department.

This is why product managers might still be quite hard to replace with AI currently. He's not the kind who arranges features. He seems to know how to collaborate with AI — some kind of product manager. The previous generation of product managers might also have some interaction-level changes. But every interaction-level change actually brings a very big product. Maybe Douyin is a product with interaction-level change. Maybe Claude Code is also a product at this level.

加入 Google DeepMind：研究自由

Many researchers, once they enter Google, feel Google doesn't give enough scope. Your move seems to be the opposite.

It depends on what you yourself want. If what you really want is a very clear scope, and this thing is closely related to your final product model — I must get one of my ideas into this model — then Google might be a very bad place. There are so many researchers, so many already mature organizations, doing this thing has a very complicated process.

But Gemini is — if what you want is research freedom, freedom to explore, and want to learn from broader humanity, I think in this world you probably can't find a second place stronger than Gemini.

Many people, when they leave one place for another, the main reason they feel unhappy is because they didn't figure out what they wanted.

不再追求 product impact？

How do you quantify product influence?

Hard to quantify. When publishing papers there was still first author, lead author. Now actually there's no way to quantify. This is also why I think in this era, actually talking about each individual's influence is a very ethereal thing. Essentially it's still the organization that did such a thing, or the world needs this.

在 3.7 上的具体工作

Mainly related to agentic coding and the environment around it, and some algorithmic work. On the algorithmic side, mainly about making training more stable.

But I do think there were definitely some algorithmic improvements that didn't achieve particularly ideal results. To be honest, it's definitely better than previous algorithms. But I don't think that was my personal contribution. It was a collective effort.

The era of individual heroism for language models has probably passed. The Transformer moment — at that point when the technology hadn't yet reached the scale-up stage — the person who discovered it might be a hero. After that, it's been more about collectivism — whether this group can work together toward a common goal.

AI 本质上是简单的

The reason I say collectivism: I think AI as a field is fundamentally simple. Except maybe that leap moment where the idea might require some really deep insights, in the process after that, many ideas are actually very trivial. Anyone could think of them. Anyone could do them. It's just that you got lucky and happened to seize the opportunity.

Before large-scale language models emerged, a lot of things were not inevitable. There was a lot of uncertainty. But after entering that stage, especially now — any organization that wants to stop AI progress can't do it. Anthropic is very concerned about AI safety. But does Anthropic have the ability to stop AI development? It doesn't. If you stop developing, others will continue. Your voice will only get smaller.

The world is pushing us forward rather than us pushing the world forward.

AI 安全：Anthropic 的天真假设

Anthropic's very important motivation is so-called AI safety. The motivation when it was founded.

Anthropic's explanation: First, I need to have the most cutting-edge model. Only then do I have a voice to push my AI safety agenda. So actually its thinking all along has been: I want to build the best model in the world. Everyone will have to listen to me to push forward my safety policies.

But from my personal perspective, I think this idea is very naive. It's not going to happen. What's more likely to happen: everyone will have great frontier models and you won't be able to stop anything.

If you really want to avoid AI bringing about some crisis, what would be a more self-enforcing approach? Like nuclear weapons — multi-party control. Many countries with nuclear weapons. They all have the ability to destroy each other. Stability is maintained through this balance of power.

If you want to stop AI from doing bad things, maybe ultimately you'll need a similar mechanism — rather than pinning your hopes on one company setting a law.

AI 为什么"本质上简单"？

You can run experiments. Compared to things fundamentally difficult — like physics — where without experimental data at that energy scale, you simply can't understand the theory at that energy scale. But AI isn't bound by this. It doesn't matter if you don't understand it. It can still move forward.

I can do any experiment I can think of. It's just that possibly I need some time to scale up the compute or get the infrastructure ready. But there's no fundamental difficulty.

This is why I feel AI doesn't give people the sense that it's hitting a wall. First, you can try many things. Second, it's not that everyone has run out of ideas. More often, there are too many ideas — need to try them one by one.

AI 自己做研究：6-12 个月内

Very soon AI might start doing experiments itself. Within 4 months. In the next 6-12 months AI will do experiments itself.

AI improving itself, speeding up its own development process — this is already happening. It's already helping us achieve some things and speed up our experimental pace.

What it currently can't do: from start to finish complete an AI research project. Not only write code, but run experiment, see results, analyze results, know where it did wrong, propose new hypotheses, design new code, run new experiments. This chain is not yet complete. But this chain might be the next thing to gradually become complete.

离开 Anthropic 后对它的判断

When I left, I was actually quite pessimistic. But later I was overly pessimistic.

Anthropic's API selling tokens — this is a bad business. Only a good business for Google. Eventually leads to price wars.

But later Anthropic on the product side did many clever ideas — Claude Code, Claude Cowork, work efficiency stuff. So now it has become more than what I thought.

If you ask me which of OpenAI and Anthropic would die first — at that time I would think maybe Anthropic. But later, first OpenAI got punched by Google, then Anthropic itself got on track. So now Anthropic has more advantage.

Gemini 转折点：Nano Banana + Gemini 3

I had pretty high expectations for Gemini at the time. I joined at the end of September last year, before Gemini 3 was released.

I think people's perception probably shifted with the Gemini 2.5 generation. 2.5 was a generation where people actually started using the model.

Why did you join Gemini? Mainly because I knew what kind of atmosphere Gemini had — a lot of people doing different kinds of research. And many Gemini engineers — their technical skills are extremely, extremely strong.

In terms of actual impact, two things created a major turning point for Gemini: Nano Banana and Gemini 3. Two things back to back.

If there were only Gemini 3, it probably wouldn't have had such great results. Because when your market share is less than 10%, whether your model is slightly better or worse — it just spreads too slowly. What Nano Banana did: first, it went viral in the market. Then a ton of people downloaded the Gemini app. And then Gemini 3 was released right after — retaining those users.

If Gemini hadn't thrown this punch, OpenAI's position would be really comfortable. When ordinary people use models, their perception of the model's capabilities is actually very, very weak. Most people don't even use the o-series models. Most people just use the regular ChatGPT.

OpenAI 救了 Google 一命

As an outsider, in a sense I think OpenAI saved Google's life. Because everyone used to worry: this chatbot would completely replace search. If this really happened, Google would actually be in a tough spot. But fortunately OpenAI did this thing first, then made Google realize this thing is important. But it didn't take this thing all the way — didn't completely kill off search. Maybe just ate some market share. As a result, let Google itself catch up on chatbots.

If a company in a fictional world not only made a chatbot but also marched forward triumphantly, doing better and better, really just ate up your search in one go — completely didn't give you a chance to fight back — then it would be very tough.

Why didn't chatbots eat up search? Because current chatbot interaction methods actually won't completely eat up search. The one point it's stronger than search: strong interactivity, follow-up questions, condensing complex information. So this portion of usage scenarios will steal people from search.

But there are still some very simple scenarios in search. Like I just search "buy rice" — search and it's done. Do I have to ask ChatGPT which one is good? It's still spinning there.

Google 的产品困境

Google has traditionally been a bit slow with products. Has always been relatively slow.

What's Google good at? Finding an extremely simple product form. Everyone looks the same. Then it just competes with you relentlessly on technology. And you can't outcompete it. Search is a classic example. Everyone has the same search box. One button — but it just searches faster and more accurately than you. There's nothing you can do about it.

All along, Google has been in this state of doing very well but Wall Street never really bought into it. Everyone always wondered where this company's moat really is. There's no product ingenuity. No retention mechanisms either. But it has survived until now.

It's the culture — particularly valued engineers in the past, later particularly valued research. Very well suited for products where technological capability spills over.

"聊天框"是终极形态吗？

I don't think anyone's position is secure right now. The form of AI still has a long way to go. We're not at any endgame yet.

Conditioned on the chatbot being the super app, then maybe there's something to fight over. But the problem is: is this form the super app form? What if someone comes out with a completely different form one day, and your functionality becomes a subset of that thing?

This model clearly has so many capabilities. But the way we use it is a chatbot. It just doesn't quite make sense.

(Note: This video was recorded over 2 months ago, when the agent paradigm was not yet clear.)

We need a product manager to unlock the model's capabilities.

Google 内部的变化

Google's technical reserves have always been sufficient. Organizationally, it became increasingly clear later on — having a better framework to let everyone work together. There might slowly be some progress.

Especially pre-training has become very, very clear now. Who is responsible for what, every node, who is the responsible person. These things are very clear. It was very chaotic in the earliest days.

Pre-training at Google is a very, very controllable thing — predictable. You can know the next generation won't be bad. You might even know how good it will be.

Compared to Anthropic, it's still more bottom-up. Different cultures can both work. Big companies have big company ways. Startups have startup ways.

Google more says — for relatively deterministic things like pre-training, which is already a relatively deterministic paradigm — Google will be more like making it into an engineering project. Google's engineering management ability is very strong.

Post-training of course has more uncertainty. So maybe post-training currently is still more bottom-up — everyone can try more broadly.

Pre-training 也是一种 RL？

It's hard to say from a pure technical perspective. Pre-training, or supervised learning — what is the essential difference between SFT and RL?

Pre-training and SFT are essentially not that different. You just take the data you get as your ground truth, treat that as your expert output, then you align toward the distribution of that expert output.

Reinforcement learning might be a broader level: this original output is not a given expert but something I produced myself. Among them there are good results and bad results. So you want good results to move closer and bad results to move away.

In a sense, pre-training and SFT are a subset of reinforcement learning. But these two things do have differences. The biggest difference lies in the data.

For pre-training data, what matters more is having a good distribution — broad enough, aligned well enough with the scope you want. But data quality doesn't need to be extremely high.

For post-training, it's the opposite. Distribution may be much narrower. But quality requirements are very high.

不同公司如何组织 pre/post-training？

Anthropic and Google are pretty similar — both have one team for pre-training and another for post-training.

OpenAI might be more chaotic. Initially they had three teams — pre-training, the Strawberry team (reinforcement learning), and a post-training team. My understanding: its post-training wasn't really its RL team. Strawberry and its post-training are actually what other companies call post-training and product. They treat the later stages as product work — their post-training is intertwined with product.

Most companies, the product team doesn't really train models anymore. They communicate the desired traits to the team training the model. But OpenAI's post-training in a sense is its own product team but it can also train models.

近期模型发布的洞察

I think I can only say I was fortunate to be involved.

Every time I see it I feel like — how am I going to face my colleagues in the office tomorrow. At the office it's fine. Probably they don't care too much.

Every project I've been part of, whether at Google or Anthropic, would happen even without me. Everyone now is a surfer. Essentially it's a wave — not you the surfer. The wave is AI. This thing itself will move forward. Whether you surf this wave or not, this wave will crash on shore.

Benchmark 饱和与新方向

Some things people are competing over — actually looking at it now, in this era, already not that important. Because of inertia from the past, everyone would compete for first place on various Benchmarks to prove basic capability is strong. But by now, those Benchmarks are somewhat maxed out.

Earliest everyone paid attention to SWE-bench — randomly everyone hit 80-something. Fortunately no one exceeded 83 (recently OpenAI said they exceeded 83). Some problems are not well-defined.

Before everyone reasoned with AIME, then IMO. After IMO — RKGI. Before Gemini 3, everyone probably forgot — the highest was maybe level 10 — everyone was like "hard as climbing to heaven." Then Gemini 3 made it 30-something. Then Claude 4.6 became 60-something. Then Gemini 3 Deep Think hit 80-something. So this is also maxed out.

Releasing fast also shows this problem has become easy for everyone. Everyone knows the know-how now. There are no secrets anymore.

下一个范式级变化？

The two things I just mentioned — ML coding and long horizon — I think might be something that hasn't reached paradigm-level change but is very valuable.

ML coding is valuable because Google itself is a major player in AI research and the most full-stack — has hardware design, parts connecting hardware to models. If this entire system can be accelerated or better managed, that could be very valuable.

Long horizon goes without saying. Everyone knows it's very important.

Paradigm-level might still be those more uncertain things like multimodal generation. There might be a hero or group of heroes.

Continue learning 与 World models

People used to think continue learning and long horizon were very different. Continue learning changes some of the model's weights. When you do this context management, it doesn't change model weights. But actually, if you think about it, there's no fundamental difference. Because those tokens in the context — their own KV cache is also a kind of weight.

Which one will be more useful? It's unclear. But essentially they're both for doing long horizon.

Ten thousand people have ten thousand world models. The definition isn't clear. First of all, I don't know what a world model is. When everyone talks about world models, they might be talking about different things. Gemini's world model might be different from labs like Fei-Fei Li's.

Gemini's world model is more of an end-to-end level of training. Result: not only can I generate a video, I can generate a scenario — the state at this moment, and given an action condition, the next moment state becomes a function of previous state and action. End-to-end training this capability.

xAI：commentator 视角

xAI I don't understand. They've always been quite turbulent.

I don't have that much contact with xAI. Some people I contacted have also left now. Actually I don't know what happened to them.

Google 内部的英雄

I think heroes might be different people at different stages. But behind every hero there is one person — Sergey Brin, Google's cofounder. Many big decisions might not be decided by him on how to do them, but in the end he has to be the one to make the final call.

The person who appears more on the front lines is Koray Kavukcuoglu (DeepMind CTO, now also Google SVP).

Demis Hassabis — I think Demis might manage more things leaning toward science, like drug design, Isomorphic Labs. Gemini — he doesn't manage much. At least from my perspective, the person I see more is Koray.

系统性做 AI 的方法

Several aspects:

One: from the whole system perspective, it needs a relatively scientific attitude. You need to clearly understand Scaling Law. You need to clearly understand what assumptions you have made. When I make a change, what factors are actually related and what factors are not.

From the organizational perspective: requires people to be very reliable, very responsible. Every system, every evaluation framework is very easily hacked. You can always do something to make your metrics look good. But a trustworthy person would think: if the thing I did works well, is it really effective at large scales? Did I miss some factors?

Actually doing things systematically — sounds like one sentence but actually very complex. Many details, many resistances. It actually goes against human nature. Every individual's human nature might be to make their own things show up better. But for a company, the most beneficial thing is to make the entire system very solid. This is actually the best for you personally. Because once this system is solid, you can leverage this system to produce more output.

The bad thing: this system will make your individual heroism not shine. But you can rest assured that others' individual heroism also won't shine. If you are in a system where individual heroism can shine, then this system might not be particularly stable. Because one person leaving might cause the entire thing to collapse. For example like OpenAI.

重复做简单事的难度

The most difficult thing in this industry is actually doing simple things cleanly. Why? Because everyone can do simple things. If you can't do them cleaner than others, then there's no value.

需要研究员自己对于这个系统怎么运作有一个好的理解，然后以及对公司负责任才能做到。否则就是你很容易做到一件事——你可能比如说你在考虑training的时候是比别人好的，但你考虑training加sampling时候比别人差。你总可以选择你只是有training，但这就很糟糕。

所以这个就是既需要你个人的负责任，又需要说组织所建立的这个体系里能够能尽量的发现这些有意的或者无意的这种边界的事情。

但是你作为个体的话，你不知道怎么样是对全局最好的呀？其实是需要——我觉得如果一个研究员做不到对全局去考虑的话，他就不是一个好的研究员，在现在这个时代。

这和你就是在学术界做research是很不一样的事。因为在学术界做research，本质上是一个人吃饱全家不愁的状态——我为我的项目负责，我为我的可重复性负责。但是在一个公司里，你其实更多的时候是——我得为这个公司负责。这是两种完全不一样的心态。

那你这种自觉性从哪里来的？不知道。我觉得我可能就是拉不下脸。

拉不下脸是什么？就是——你对一个公司负责任是你和这个公司的契约的一部分。其实我觉得没什么道理不这么做。这么做是没有原因的。

组织：top-down vs bottom-up

If you're just doing it for personal heroism, it's very likely to undermine the bigger picture.

The traditional view: bottom-up was a necessary condition for fostering innovation. Because everyone needs freedom — only with freedom can there be innovation. But purely bottom-up — you find it doesn't actually work either. Because it just becomes chaotic. That's what Google was like before.

You probably need someone or a small group who can blend these two approaches. That's why I think whether an organization runs well or not — it looks like an organizational issue but ultimately comes down to the tech leader.

Two qualities for a leader: 1. Can fight fires themselves — when something runs into trouble, can step in and lead the team to solve the problem. 2. Can understand others — even if it's something they wouldn't do themselves, they can understand why what others are doing matters. They can tolerate and accommodate others.

TPU vs GPU

From a purely hardware perspective, it's hard to say which is truly better.

The biggest difference setting aside hardware: GPUs have a better open-source ecosystem. TPUs don't. But this actually isn't an issue at large-scale commercial deployment. If you're running a cluster of hundreds of thousands of cards, building out infrastructure isn't really that big of a deal.

These two have differences in design philosophy. Hopper GPU: inside one pod, maybe just eight cards, all interconnect with one another. NVLink is extremely fast. Within one pod, basically no communication bandwidth bottleneck.

TPUs: abandoned pairwise interconnection between cards. They try to fit as many cards as possible into one big rack. 3D Torus design — each card only connects to its three nearest neighbors in three directions, but the entire cluster can be connected into one big Torus. If your compilers or sharding logic is written well enough, you get more memory capacity and reduce a lot of communication bounds.

Downside: at small scale, more of a rigid structure. General versatility might not be as strong.

Neo Labs：大部分会死

My feeling is that the vast majority of neo labs will die. Some labs genuinely have good people. Some labs might actually be starting to do some real work — for example, Thinking Machines is delivering some new things. But some neo labs — I have absolutely no idea what they're trying to do. These two have actually been away from the field for a long time.

中国消费应用 vs 美国生产力软件

In 2026, China will place a lot of emphasis on the consumer-side narrative. Who becomes that super app.

Nobody in Silicon Valley talks about this. Because American enterprise — the productivity software market is just too big and profit margins are too high. So now everyone will probably focus first on productivity software or enterprise.

What China is really strong at is the consumer side. It can come up with really complex product features in a very indirect way to snowball that profit.

Douyin — not "watch a video, charge 20 cents per video." You can watch videos for free, but I can quietly slip in ads, do live streaming, do e-commerce.

But that doesn't work for productivity software. Productivity software is very straightforward: I help you write code, my cost is 150 a month, I sell it to you for 200, I make 50.

The US in the past — with very straightforward products it can push technology to the extreme. But there's never been a product that felt so sophisticated that you can't live without it, yet you don't feel like it's taking your money, but it's actually making money from you.

Should Meta copy ByteDance? Meta isn't as strong as ByteDance. Meta can't find its own niche. No American company doing this. No one has found the niche that Doubao occupies.

The Americans making products — the people doing consumer products aren't good enough. Far behind China.

The positive feedback loop in the US over the past decade all came from B2B. A lot of enterprise stuff. Or it's just too easy to make money in the US. When it's too easy to make money, you won't rack your brains over how to make money.

招聘哲学：可靠 > 聪明

I'd benefit from this. But sometimes — I don't know if this thing is a good thing.

The price has become so high. On one hand, everyone thinks this thing is scarce. But it might not be that scarce. Because training a person — although this thing isn't that hard — but training a person requires an environment. You need to have that opportunity to be exposed to this thing. Without that opportunity, no matter how smart you are, it's useless. Maybe in the past, people who could encounter that opportunity weren't that many.

Another aspect: maybe the hype about people is excessive. Really like to mythologize individuals.

The most important trait is being reliable.

我设计的面试题

This interview question: this person needs to, within 24 hours, complete a reinforcement learning project from scratch. They choose what model to use, what data, what algorithm. After 24 hours up, they'll have a one-hour discussion with me.

Without AI, this would be impossible. No one could do it in 24 hours. With AI, it's actually quite easy. AI can do the whole thing for you.

Two reasons for this design:

  1. In this era, evaluating whether they write good code is useless. What's more important: whether they can effectively leverage AI.
  2. There's a trap: if you let AI do everything but don't really try to understand what AI did for you, you'll be exposed during that one-hour discussion. That's where people fail. Tests whether you've truly formed a collaboration with AI.

Also, why 24 hours: to see how much this person values this opportunity. Can they stay up all night? If willing to pull an all-nighter, they can survive these 24 hours.

AI 是否还是蓝海？

Purely working on language models is no longer a blue ocean. The last train has already left. I feel like I got in on that last train. New people won't have the opportunity to encounter such good opportunities in a relatively small team.

But AI is a very vast field. Language models are just a tiny part. Multimodal generation — many opportunities. Robotics — even more opportunities. Even more extreme: using AI to help with real scientific problems, quantum control. More blue ocean.

For people young enough, doing the hottest thing right now is not the right choice. Doing things no one has done now might be a better choice.

未来规划

I think probably not (be at Google for a long time).

I will still try to challenge myself. Need to torture myself. But I just might need to find something worth torturing myself for.

If AI is not fundamentally difficult, won't you find it boring? Where is your challenge?

Although it's not difficult, knowing and not knowing — there is still a gap. From completely not knowing the details to slowly understanding the details. These things still require spending time and effort. After you understand, this will be helpful for your future — whether product-related or other AI directions.

I haven't figured out how to torture myself yet.

两段经历的对比

Anthropic is where you can understand one thing — one line, language model, every aspect — very thoroughly. It gives you that opportunity.

Google is more horizontal. Many different aspects, many different people. You can see different perspectives, different research directions.

Anthropic, because it bets firmly enough, you can understand more vertically.

不会去用 AI 做物理

Currently it's not the highest priority. If one day I solve the highest priority thing on my hands and haven't found anything else to do, I might go do this thing.

My highest priority now: push ML coding and long horizon to a relatively stable state. That's my highest priority.

Using AI to do physics — many people are already trying. One more or less of me doesn't matter. Might as well let others do it first.

物理偶像

In physics, actually there are many who are really quite strong. People from 100 years ago — Einstein, Heisenberg — let's not talk about. Yang Chen-Ning — let's also not talk about.

When I was doing topology — there was someone who later won the Nobel Prize: Haldane. These people have some abnormal foresight. They seemed out of place in their era. When Haldane first did Haldane model and fractional Quantum Hall effect related things, it was decades away from when everyone finally figured out these topological states. He could feel this thing was important and kept pushing it himself.

In AI, maybe Geoffrey Hinton. When everyone felt this thing was optional or not that certain, he kept working in this direction. He might be a hero-level figure.

After him, in AI, there might also be some heroic collectives — like Transformer (Noam, Ashish, Niki and them).

直率：不再压抑的表达

I don't have any mentors in this industry. Don't have any old friends. I can criticize whoever I want. This might be the benefit of not coming from AI.

I was quite restrained when I was a student. Later I found restraint useless — no benefit to myself, no benefit to others either. Better to be more direct.

Directly expressing your own ideas — in the short term people will definitely hate you. But in the long term, everyone will appreciate it.

XXX has always been quite stupid. And consistently stupid.

In Pauli's words: "not even wrong." Because it's not well-defined. Hard to say whether what he says is right or wrong. One day, maybe a different paradigm happens — he can jump out and say "hey, I said this back then." But you discover: if the paradigm were another state, he could also say the same thing. This is why I hate this kind of very vague people.

XXX is still a well-defined thing. As for whether it's right or wrong — the future will test.

老与少：变化的姿态

When people get old, they don't necessarily turn into old geezers. People get old, they split into two types: 1. Venerable elder: stop nitpicking so much, actually put effort into mentoring young people. 2. Old geezer: don't know what they're talking about, yet love to nitpick and boss people around.

Why did I get so direct? When you develop more judgment of your own, stupid people just look even stupider. But they haven't hurt you. So why hate them? It's just stupidity intolerance.

This field is objective enough. You don't really have to worry too much about offending people with your opinions. As long as your views are internally consistent — you have a coherent framework — you're not just randomly trashing people. People will actually respect you for it. Because ultimately, how well you do in this field is judged by objective standards.

推荐：影响深远的书

This is the hardest question of the day. I feel like you're overestimating my cultural sophistication.

Honestly, I don't really have a life-changing book.

The book I recently read is Yukawa's autobiography — Hideki Yukawa's (1949 Nobel Prize in Physics winner) "Tabibito" (The Traveler). You get to see a scientist who later seemed so successful struggling in his youth. Very authentic.

A novel I really like — "From the New World" — it's a Japanese novel.

其他偏好

Favorite food: sushi probably.

Favorite place: if you really force me to choose, probably Hawaii. Because I really love the ocean. But it's hard to say for sure — after I visit more coastal places I might have a new favorite.

Something not many people know but probably should: Don't trust old timers.

Ever been superstitious? Fundamentally no. But sometimes superstition can be a way to comfort yourself.

Superstitious about old timers? Never. But I probably didn't hate old timers this much before. Then I started hating them more and more. When you develop more judgment, stupid people just look even stupider.

MBTI? No idea.

影响 AI 进展最大的论文

Sequence-to-sequence is one. At the peak of the feature engineering era.

Scaling Laws is one — Jared Kaplan's Scaling Laws paper at OpenAI. A paper that introduced this systematic research methodology into the field. The actual methods may not have been exactly right, but it was the first to introduce this idea.

Key important bet: long horizon.

关于"语言即世界"这个名字

This name is a bit too normal, too mediocre.

Ten years ago, this was a very unique perspective. But now there's too much consensus. Around 2014, 2015, everyone thought vision was the most important thing. Back then, realizing that language is an important carrier of intelligence was probably different.

But I don't think your name was meant in an AI context.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：Anthropic 的"安全使命"正在反噬其自身逻辑
姚顺雨用"被对手救了命"这个角度切入 OpenAI 与 Google 的关系，其实揭示了一个更深的悖论——**Anthropic 的存在本身也救了 AI 安全**。
公司创始时的逻辑是"我必须造出最强模型才有话语权推动安全"，但姚顺雨直接戳破：这是一种 naive 的政治幻想。真正能约束 AI 的从来不是"一家公司立规矩"，而是像核武器那样的**多方制衡（self-enforcing mechanism）**。
这里的反讽是：**Anthropic 越成功，它最初的使命就越无效**——因为它的成功恰恰证明了 AI 安全无法通过"一家良心公司领跑"来实现，而只能通过它最不愿看到的"全员军备竞赛"达成均衡。这是组织自我目的的解构。
## 二、跨领域连接：18 世纪热力学 × 当代 AI × 量子非厄米系统
姚顺雨多次把 AI 比作"18 世纪的热力学"——经验定律先行、微观机制后补。但他更深一层的隐喻其实埋在他自己的物理工作里：**非厄米系统**。
非厄米系统的核心发现是：**当一个系统不再"孤立"

item.title: [Lex Clips] Shocking performance boost of assembly code: ~100x faster than C code | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、隐藏的悖论：最"过时"的技艺，正在守护最"未来"的设备

表面上看，David 项目（24 万行手写汇编）像是一群恋旧者在抵抗时代。但真正令人震惊的反转在于：正是这群"恋旧者"在用最古老的手艺，让最前沿的格式（AV1）跑在最普通的硬件上——你的旧 iPad 2、没有硬件解码器的廉价安卓机、低功耗的边缘设备。

这里有一个反直觉的事实链： - 大厂（Google/Netflix/Amazon）说"AV1 太复杂，必须靠硬件解码" - 这意味着用户必须换新设备才能享受新格式 - 而 VideoLAN 的人写了 24 万行汇编，让旧硬件解码新格式

汇编不是怀旧，而是反消费主义的技术武器。 每一行手写 SIMD，都在延缓一台设备被扔进垃圾填埋场的命运。这是软件工程领域罕见的、可以用"环保"二字描述的工作——而它居然是用最难、最不性感的语言写的。

二、跨领域连接：David 团队 = 高频交易员 + 米其林厨师 + 文艺复兴时期的匠人

Kieran 自己提到了高频交易作为类比，但这个连接可以再往深推一层。考虑三个看似无关的群体的共同模式：

领域 共同行为
HFT 工程师 绕过 OS 调用约定，把网络包直接读到 CPU 缓存
米其林厨师 拒绝预制酱料，用最原始的食材榨取最大风味
文艺复兴金匠 拒绝铸造模具，每件作品手工锤打
David 团队 "我们滥用机器（we abuse the machine），用加密指令做视频处理"

共同的元模式是：当抽象层（compiler / 预制品 / 模具）变成主流时，少数人通过"故意违反约定"获得几个数量级的优势。Lauren 说"我们用密码学指令做完全不相关的事"——这和爵士乐手用错音演奏出全新和声、料理人用甜品技法做主菜，是同构的创造行为。

真正的洞见：汇编大师不是"低层程序员"，他们是"违反抽象的艺术家"。 抽象的本质是契约，而最大的性能（或美感、或味道）藏在违约里。

三、对 AI 时代的预言性观察：vibe coding 的天花板就在这里

你提到自己在按摩浴缸里用自然语言编程。这里有一个非常尖锐的、被访谈中一笔带过但极其重要的论断：

"你不能 vibe code 的核心东西，就是针对硬件的极致优化。"

把这个论断放进 LLM 训练的现实里看，一个意想不到的发现浮现了：

LLM 在汇编上表现极差，不是因为汇编"难"，而是因为汇编是少数"训练语料和真实最优解严重错位"的领域。

GitHub 上 99% 的 C 代码是"还行"的 C，所以 LLM 学会了"还行的 C"，这对用户来说已经够用。但 GitHub 上 99% 的汇编是教学示例或自动生成的，真正一流的手写 SIMD 汇编只存在于 FFmpeg、David、x264 等极少数仓库里——这是一个被语料分布严重低估的知识领域。

进一步的预言： - 短期（2-3 年）：AI 推理成本爆炸 → 量化（FP8/FP4/1.58bit）成主流 → 量化 kernel 的手写汇编将成为价值最高的代码之一 - 中期：当所有"能 vibe code 的"都被 vibe code 了，剩余的稀缺价值会集中在"AI 写不好的地方" - 长期：汇编程序员的稀缺性可能在 AI 时代不降反升，因为这是少数"AI 自己都跑不快、需要人类来加速 AI 本身"的领域

也就是说——访谈里这群"守着旧技艺"的人，恰好站在让 AI 自己跑得动的咽喉位置。摩尔定律之死，把汇编从博物馆请回了战场。

一个意外的尾注

注意一个细节：Karpathy 因为这些推文去学了汇编。一个深度学习领军人物，在 LLM 已经能写代码的时代，回头去学最低层的语言。这不是怀旧——这是一个顶级智者对"价值会在哪里残留"的赌注。

📝 转录文本

公司缘起：把 FFmpeg / VLC 的精神带入商业

JB and I have started companies broadly speaking around the FFmpeg / VLC ethos. So that's really low-level work. So in most companies this wouldn't be written in assembly. It would be accepted that C is fast. Um, as you can see from that, C is not fast. Uh, so here [snorts], it says 62 times faster than C.

Yeah, so it's taking the ethos of doing low-level programming, real-time programming, and using that for commercial applications. And JB and I have started companies around that. In many cases hiring developers from the open source community to use that ethos. And so that's a great example of some of the things we're doing. In most companies it would be, "I'll write this in C and it's fast and we're done." But actually, you can get a lot better.

跨 OS 支持的噩梦：从 Windows XP 到 OS/2

For me, like some of the headaches we have is around some OS that are difficult to support, right? Because um, if you look at VLC — and thanks to FATE and FFmpeg — we run on the last version of VLC runs on Windows XP and still runs there and runs on Windows 11. Um, we work on Mac OS 10.7 to the latest Mac OS, whatever it is, right? 26. Um, we work on iOS since iOS 9. Well, we are actually iOS 26, right? We support — um, we [clears throat] — support many types of Linuxes, BSD, Solaris. The last version still runs on OS/2, right? Like there is maybe 10 users of OS/2 in the world and one of them is maintaining VLC.

Then you realize that this very small team around VLC — and using FFmpeg codecs and all the other ones — support more OSes than Microsoft or Google or Apple. And they have infinite amount of uh of power and resources.

But for example, the worst is iOS. Um, in order to build on iOS 9, we need to do some very clever mixing of several version of the Xcode IDE and SDK from Apple from several versions, and do a type of Frankenstein version of that so that we can still support iOS 9, which is not supported at all by the compiler of Apple — in order to still run on 32 um, on iOS 9. And you've seen on FATE that it was still supporting iOS 9, right?

So my headaches are mostly related to the support of so many OSes. And it's important because we receive so many people saying, "Hey, thank you. I still have my iPad 2 to watch movies and it still works on iOS 9, right?" And it's also an impact of like not forcing people to buy new hardware when it works fine. If you optimize it correctly — which brings us to what we were saying about assembly — it's also fighting like the fact that you need to buy something new non-stop while you could optimize more, which is a lost art.

失落的手艺：什么是汇编？

You got to tell me about this lost art — or the carriers of the flame of assembly. What is assembly? Why is it beautiful? Why is it challenging? How does it work?

So when you write assembly code, you write this using the instructions the actual processor is using directly. So most of the time you would write in a language — let's take C as a good example — the compiler would use that to create assembly language and machine code instructions for you based off your C code.

And there's a specific flavor of assembly that we use in FFmpeg that's called SIMD — Single Instruction Multiple Data. So this means, for example, say I want to add five to a number in scalar assembly — this is what's known as, you work on an individual element. So I have the number 10 and I want to add five. I use the add instruction and I add five to 10 and I get 15.

With SIMD, I can have a whole vector of 16 different numbers. They could all be different. If I want to add five to that, I can run one instruction and that one instruction sums all 16 elements. And that, as you can imagine, lends itself very well to video — video is, you know, a pixel grid. So I can perform operations on multiple pixels at the same time.

FFmpeg 的特殊路线：拒绝 intrinsics

The key thing that we do differently in FFmpeg is we don't use any abstractions, or any major abstractions, on top of that. So there's a part of the world that uses what's known as intrinsics. So these are C functions that behave very similarly — but not quite the same — to writing assembly by hand. So the registers that data is stored in on the CPU, the compiler allocates those for you.

And the key thing to understand when we write SIMD is we have a 10x — and not percentage, 10x to 50x — speed improvement. That function is 62x. That's nuts. On the FFmpeg account, as you know, posts and tweets a lot about that to try and say, "Hey, we're doing this stuff."

You are a person who sees the beauty in assembly, but it's also extremely useful for these kinds of applications to actually significantly outperform even C, which is crazy.

David 项目：30,000 行 C，240,000 行手写汇编

It is necessary, right? Because one of the projects that we need to talk about is called David, right? So David is a decoder for the format that was done by Alliance for Open Media, which is a video decoder called AV1.

For people who don't know, we've been talking about H.264; AV1 is another hugely popular standard and codec that is increasingly taking over the internet. And when this format was launched, many people said — especially even from the Alliance for Open Media, right? which is Google, Netflix, Amazon, Mozilla — they say, "Well, this format is so complex, it must be done in hardware to do decoding."

And well, I arrived with a few other people — mostly Ronald, Henrik, and Martin — who said, "We need to have an extremely good software decoder, because it's going to take time to have hardware." And so we wrote this project, which is beyond insane.

Um, we are talking about 30,000 lines of C, but 240,000 lines of handwritten assembly. Right? Handwritten assembly. 240,000 lines. That's incredible.

That means some of the stuff we're talking about is probably the biggest assembly code bases. To give you an idea — and Kieran can correct me — but I think FFmpeg has 100,000 lines of assembly for all the codecs. And just this one has 240,000.

Um, it's a VideoLAN project, of course. And it is optimized at the maximum because the motto when we started the project is every cycle matters, right? Every cycle matters because David is used in VLC and in some software AV1 playback stacks. We are talking about probably 3 billion devices, which are going to decode video non-stop. Because, for example, 30% of the video from Netflix are now in AV1, 50% of YouTube, right? And you often don't have a hardware decoder, because not many devices have hardware decoders. And with David, we realized that with one or two cores, you were able to decode 720p correctly. So it is literally —

[laughter]

— incredible, right? It's David.

一条引发争论的推文

Look at that. Yeah, so this is another spicy tweet from you. "This is what peak video codec should look like. 79.9% assembly, 9.6% C, and 0.5% other."

And what's incredible is with those tweets, which is factual, people get crazy. They're unhappy, right? They say —

For the last two years they go crazy. "No, intrinsics is fine. The compiler is —" Oh, they go crazy. "You cannot optimize your compiler. Auto vectorization is your fault. You don't understand."

And we've tried that forever, right?

For two years. And two years later, showing hundreds of examples of handwritten assembly. "No, no, no, you're doing it wrong. The compiler can do this."

为什么编译器赢不了：直觉 vs 数量级

So what should — actually just articulate a little clearer. The intuition there from the software engineering folks, when you have code like — okay, let's just take an example, C++. There's a compiler that's doing a lot of the optimization. Yes. And the presumption is, if you have a good enough compiler, if you continue to improve the compiler, you're going to generate code —

Yeah.

— that can perform like optimal performance. You cannot possibly beat it. And you're consistently challenging that thought that if you —

By orders of magnitude. By orders of magnitude, hand-crafted assembly can outperform C.

The two things that they tell us is, "Yeah, but modern compilers have auto-vectorization, right?" Because SIMD that we're doing is vectorization. And it's not even close, right? It's not even close. It's not like 5%, 10% slower. It's multiple times slower.

哲学层面：摩尔定律之后的世界

So can we — I don't know if you can say something philosophically — because there's a lot of great software engineers, great engineers, great machine learning people. Karpathy will listen to this and say, "What's the intuition he's supposed to get from this?"

Karpathy learned assembly because of the tweets, by the way. He's like, "Oh, I think this is a movement." And you know the way he documents his work, and so on.

Philosophically, what's important to realize is that we passed the time where hardware was going so much faster, right? We are at the end of Moore's law. We have limitations for AI, for memory. You need to go down in the stack and optimize more to get more power from what you have. Because requests for power — CPU power, GPU power — are exploding, while the hardware is not exploding in speed, right? So what people do is that they add more cores, right? But at some point you can't have 250 cores, right?

So what we do is to take every inch of the machine.

滥用机器：用密码学指令做视频处理

Not just that. Not just that. We abuse the machine. We use the machine in ways that the creator didn't expect. Sometimes we use an instruction that's completely unrelated to what we do. We use a cryptography instruction in video processing to do nothing related.

And one of the other things that we do, for example in David, which is a bit crazy, is that we don't use the function calling convention from the operating system.

We should explain that. That is extremely complex, but basically usually when you do move from one function in code to another, there is a way to save the registry — the state of the CPU — to enter another function. And this is standard. It's a bit complex.

I would simplify this a bit. So David does things to abuse the calling convention. You could define the calling convention as: I've written a function and I want to call another function. How is the data shared between the functions? Because there's a convention — what's known as a calling convention. And what David does, for optimal reasons, is create its own calling convention sometimes.

So if I want to call Lex Fridman's library, we've got to agree on a convention so that I can share data with you in the assembly language space. And one of the challenges in assembly is that — well, not every operating system, but there are at least four that I can think of on x86: Linux 32-bit, Windows 32-bit, Windows 64, Linux 64. They all have their own calling conventions.

And so one of the amazing things Lauren Merritt did, who we talked about before, was create a very lightweight abstraction layer so you could write your assembly code once and it handled all the calling convention stuff for you. Which is always a problem because you had to manage four different variants.

But David takes this even further — for speed reasons, it does its own calling convention within itself to bypass the rules of functions, and say, "Okay, actually I'm going to call a function this way because I know it's within my library."

每个指令集都要手写：x86、ARM64、RISC-V、SVE、SME

Does that have to be special to every single operating system? Well, if it's custom, no. But the challenge is, in general, yes. And in terms of each instruction set — so the thing to always emphasize is: we do this on every instruction set. So every instruction set has its own handwritten assembly. Which is even more crazy, and that matrix has got bigger in recent years because of RISC-V, because of ARM64, because of the new SVE, there's SME. x86 has AVX-512, AVX.

So we do runtime processor detection. We see what the machine FFmpeg is running on — or David's running on — is capable of, because you could be on a laptop from 2008 where this isn't there. Runtime detection, we set function pointers accordingly. And then from then on off you go. Or you could be on a machine with RISC-V.

Yes. And in all that, we don't even respect the calling convention of the operating system in order to be faster, because we know that we are going to be called from within our binary. So we can share data without saving all the registry in the common way, because that can lead to loading and saving registry on the L1 and L2 CPU caches, and gets us faster.

So that's why I said that understanding CPU architecture, computer architecture, is key. And this is also why it's handwritten. I don't know anyone — I've never heard any other project than David doing that.

这是一门艺术

This is what Kieran calls an art, right? It is an art. I think in the mass world there isn't something on billions of devices. I know there are some specialist industries. I know in high frequency trading they could take this really seriously, where they're receiving feeds from a market and they need to react within X number of microseconds and so the instructions matter. But that's not a mass-produced thing that's on a billion devices. That's hyper-specialized running on hyper-specialized hardware. We're running on all hardware.

反直觉的革命性想法：为什么还要回到低层？

Sorry to linger on it, but that's a really counterintuitive, almost revolutionary idea here — that there's a huge amount of value to assembly. Like, what are we supposed to take away from that?

There's a bunch of people listening to this — myself included — you know, I programmed for many many years in C, C++, going up to the standards of C++, found love of C++ and metaprogramming, and so on. And then transitioned more and more, because of machine learning about 15 years ago, to Python. And so for me in this Python world, JavaScript world, now vibe-coding world where I'm just using natural language sitting in my jacuzzi drinking a drink and just talking to the computer — like, record stops — why is the value to go back all the way down to the low level?

答案：每一美元换更多算力

Because you can get more power per dollar invested, right? And sometimes it's going to be a problem that is limited by your hardware.

A good analogy is what you see in quantization in LLMs, right? People are doing, "Oh, I'm going to do that in FP8, or FP4, or some crazy things like Microsoft who did in 1.58." Because you're constrained by memory, because you're constrained by the machine you can run, because at some point we are doing real time — and I believe this is going to happen on AI inference also — at some point you need to get faster and you cannot always get more powerful hardware, right?

So you need to analyze code and see where is the mission critical? Where are the things that are called non-stop? And for example, David is a good example. It's going to be run billions of hours per day. Yeah. That makes sense. It doesn't make sense to be on the glue of FFmpeg CLI. It makes sense over there.

通向 Kyber：每一毫秒都重要

Yeah, and this has to do also — we'll talk about it more — but your new effort in your company Kyber is doing that kind of thing for ultra-low latency. So the slogan being every millisecond counts. So when you're actually extremely highly constrained in some dimension.

We are also arriving at a point where we've done so many great things, but the hardware is getting back to us, right? Because cost is increasing, because we need more power. And so you're limited by either your CPU, your RAM, or your networking. And you need to optimize — and this is where value is going to be.

Especially because doing AI is going to help do the programming of business, right? And so the core thing that you will not be able to vibe-code are optimizations for the hardware to be as fast as is possible.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、隐藏的悖论：最"过时"的技艺，正在守护最"未来"的设备
表面上看，David 项目（24 万行手写汇编）像是一群恋旧者在抵抗时代。但真正令人震惊的反转在于：**正是这群"恋旧者"在用最古老的手艺，让最前沿的格式（AV1）跑在最普通的硬件上**——你的旧 iPad 2、没有硬件解码器的廉价安卓机、低功耗的边缘设备。
这里有一个反直觉的事实链： - 大厂（Google/Netflix/Amazon）说"AV1 太复杂，必须靠硬件解码" - 这意味着用户**必须换新设备**才能享受新格式 - 而 VideoLAN 的人写了 24 万行汇编，**让旧硬件解码新格式**
**汇编不是怀旧，而是反消费主义的技术武器。** 每一行手写 SIMD，都在延缓一台设备被扔进垃圾填埋场的命运。这是软件工程领域罕见的、可以用"环保"二字描述的工作——而它居然是用最难、最不性感的语言写的。
## 二、跨领域连接：David 团队 = 高频交易员 + 米其林厨师 + 文艺复兴时期的匠人
Kieran 自己提到了高频交易作为类比，但这个连接可以再往深推一层。考虑三个看似无关的群体的共同模式：

item.title: [Lex Clips] New video codecs explained: AV2, H.265, H.266 | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、专利地雷阵：编解码器其实是一场"数学的圈地运动"

最反直觉的观察是：视频编解码器的演化，本质上不是技术史，而是法律史。当 Jean-Baptiste 提到 HEVC 里"99.9% 的场景都用不到"的特性被故意塞进规范——只为多挂一个专利——你会突然意识到：H.266 的规范文件不是一份工程文档，而是一张专利地契图。每个"矩形分块"代替"方形分块"的小创意，都是有人在数学的虚空中插了一面小旗子说"这块地是我的"。

而 AV1/AV2 的真正创新，不是压缩算法的创新，而是"如何在已被插满旗帜的草地上跳舞而不踩到任何一面旗子"的创新。这就是为什么 AV 系列里出现了"golden frame"、"S frame" 这种看似古怪的概念——它们是 B 帧的"替身演员"，在功能上几乎一样，只是为了法律上的不一样。所谓 "I can't believe it's not butter"（人造黄油）的玩笑，恰恰道出了整个开源编解码器世界的悲喜剧。

二、跨领域连接：编解码器 = 制药 + 电信 + 殖民地法

这里有个出人意料的三方类比： - 像制药：每一代新编解码器精确地提升 30%——这不是技术规律，这是专利续命经济学。就像药企为延长专利保护把分子结构改一改、再申一个新专利一样，HEVC → VVC 的本质是把已有专利重新组合 + 加一些边际收益的新专利，让付费时钟重新归零。 - 像 5G/RF：访谈中明确指出"专利雷区只有两个领域最密集——蜂窝通信和多媒体"。共同点是：两者都把连续的物理或感知现象数学化，于是给了"用数学公式圈地"的空间。 - 像殖民地法：欧洲（尤其法国）拒绝软件专利，于是 VLC 才能在法国合法存在。这意味着同一行代码在大西洋两岸有截然不同的法律身份——代码本身有了"国籍"。Jean-Baptiste 算过：如果在美国合规，VLC 每个用户要付 200 欧元——这个数字本身比任何技术参数都更能说明问题。

三、深层洞察：递归命名背后的"开源人格"

最容易被忽视的细节是 Dav1d → Dav2d 这个命名游戏。"Dav1d is an AV1 Decoder" 是一个递归首字母缩写——这是 GNU（GNU's Not Unix）传统的延续。一个团队选择递归缩写，不只是 nerd 玩梗，它透露了一种文化基因：递归缩写本身就是反权威的——它拒绝"权威赋名"，转而用自指性语言把自己嵌入历史脉络。

跨领域看：这与数学家哥德尔不完备定理的精神同构——自指是开源运动绕过封闭体系的元工具。AV1 用"自我描述的编解码器"（Dav1d 解码 AV1）、用"自我命名"（D 在法语里是 2，所以 David→Dav2d），在符号层面就在演练它在工程层面要做的事：绕过封闭体系，从自身内部生成合法性。

更惊人的预测：下一代编解码器的差异化竞争，可能不在压缩率，而在"硬件加速器的可获得性"——因为 30%/代的红利正在递减（你不可能永远 30%——香农极限就在那里），而专利成本是指数增长的。当 HP 因为 HEVC 专利费过高而在 Windows 笔记本上移除支持时，这其实是一个信号：编解码器战争的胜负手正在从"谁压得更小"转向"谁的法律风险最低"。这意味着未来的编解码器，可能会专门针对"司法管辖区"做优化设计——一种前所未有的"地理感知工程学"。

📝 转录文本

引子：聊聊多媒体的未来

Let's talk about the future of multimedia, FFmpeg, VLC. But some of the codecs we didn't really mention — AV2. So can we just lay out what is AV2? What is the hope for it? What is H.265, H.266?

一、AV1 与 AV2：开放媒体联盟的产物

So AV1 is this codec that is done by the Alliance for Open Media, right? Where there is Google, Netflix, Amazon, Apple, VideoLAN — we try to make a royalty-free, very good codec, right? And now it's being deployed, but actually the codec was finished in 2018, but a codec takes years to be used in wide scenarios, right?

So AV2 is the next generation of this codec. It's 30% better, right? So if you keep the same quality, you got 30% bandwidth reduction compared to AV1.

二、Dav1d 与 Dav2d：递归命名的小彩蛋

What's the connection with the Dav1d and AV2?

We're going to do a Dav2d, right? That I called Dav2d because D is "deux" in French.

Well done.

And you have to know that Dav1d is an actual — what we call — recursive acronym, right? Because it means "Dav1d is an AV1 Decoder," right?

Oh, nice. Nice. I didn't even think about that. And people should know that Dav1d is spelled with a "1."

Yes. And so Dav2d —

it's gonna be spelled with a "2."

It's going to be D-A-V-2-D. Sorry, I don't know how you pronounce that. And again, we did a demo at the CES of VLC running the first demo of AV2.

三、规范、编码器与解码器：术语澄清

So can you clarify to me the specification of AV2 and then the encoding and the decoding?

Sure. So the specification is like the document who explains how the codec is supposed to work, right?

And that's really AV2.

That is AV2, like H.264.

Right.

Then you have an encoder. The current encoder is called AVM, and there will probably be other encoders — probably one called SVT-AV2 — and those are the encoders. The same way x264 is an encoder to H.264. The same way that x265 is an encoder for the H.265 codec. And the decoder for AV1 is Dav1d. The decoder for AV2 is Dav2d. The decoder for H.264 is FFH264 inside FFmpeg. The decoder for HEVC is FFHEVC inside FFmpeg.

And there is a next generation codec from the MPEG world: after H.264, H.265, there is one that is called H.266, or also known as VVC.

四、为什么有两个名字？ISO 与 ITU 的政治史

So HEVC is H.265. VVC is H.266. Why is H.266 so much better?

So the question often we have is: why are there two names? Because most of the time it is a conjunct work from the ISO world and the ITU, which is the International Telecommunication Union.

These are two regulatory bodies.

One is a private entity and one is the United Nations.

Which one is the private?

ISO is private.

In theory, H.264 is MPEG-4 Part 10 / H.264 / AVC.

Mhm. And this is the full name.

Nice.

So it's the concatenation of the ISO name and the ITU name.

Yeah.

Even though they work together, this is — this is politics, historical, you know. And for HEVC, it's MPEG-H / H.265 / HEVC.

Got it.

And there is H.266, which is also named VVC.

五、每一代 30%：粗略但真实的总结

Is there a high-level thing to say about the improvement?

30% each generation is the best summary. This is true both for the AV codecs and the H.264/5/6.

So the professionals who are listening to us are going to kill us, because they will say "no, it's 35%, 25%, blah blah," but globally you need to know that HEVC is 30% better than H.264, H.266 is 30% better than H.265 — because there are so many cases, so many scenarios. For example, there are cases — especially for screen recording — where the gains are humongous, because you have the right tool that is done for that. And so for a specific video, a new generation is going to give you 70% gain or 80% gain, right?

But there used to be a ton more codecs, but now the two main codec families for transmission are the H.264 / H.265 / H.266 family and the AV1 / AV2 family.

六、核心差异：编码成本与专利费

And I guess the major difference would be the cost of encoding.

Yes. And the royalty of the patents. And this is the reason why you see the AV version of codecs — because they try to be as royalty-free (which means no cost for the patents) as much as possible.

Because what you need to know — and we've not talked about that so far — is that multimedia is what we call a "patent minefield." There are two places where you have the most patents: it's everything related to 3G, 4G, 5G, RF, and multimedia. Because it's very mathematical, and you can get great gains and so on.

So Google and Meta and Netflix wanted something where it was royalty-free. There are people who say that they have patents outside, but they are fringe patents, right? So it's mostly true that it is patent-free. Patent checking was done as part of the standardization process in AV1, AV2 — whereas patents are not even discussed in the MPEG world. Patents are off-topic completely.

七、专利的运作方式：MPEG-LA 与"专利联盟"

Can you educate me on the patent side? So usually MPEG does a format, right, and then everyone comes around and says "well, I have all those patents on this format," and they usually form a union called MPEG-LA — MPEG Licensing Association — and you put all your patents in, and then you ask everyone who's using this format to pay for it.

Wait, can you elaborate? What does it mean to have a patent of a codec? Why are there many patents?

Imagine I'm doing something where, instead of doing blocks which are square, I'm going to do rectangles, right.

Oh, so every idea — yes, somebody patents it.

Yes.

Oh, man.

Yes.

Oh, man. And how many lawyers — I mean, it pays for a lot of lawyers, right?

Like, the biggest issue is the following, right? Because at the time of H.264, the patents were — let's call it — "sane." But there was so much money in that, that for HEVC, there were a ton of things that were pushed inside the specification which are not useful in 99.9% of the time, but just so one could add a patent on it.

And so it became that for HEVC licensing, there was MPEG-LA plus another patent pool called HEVC Advance, plus — I think Nokia was outside of the patent pool. A few of them are outside, and some others. And so it was impossible to license, right.

八、真实代价：HP 与上亿美元的授权费

And I think that several months ago, HP decided that they were going to remove support for HEVC in their Windows laptops, because the cost was increasing of those patents, and it arrived at a point where there was an uncapped pad. And so for YouTube or Netflix, we could talk about hundreds of millions of dollars of licensing for patents per year. And they said, "you know what, at 100 million per year, I could create my own codec." And this is what they did. And so that's why we have the Open Media Alliance — Alliance for Open Media — that we are part of, that created AV1 and creates AV2. We create also audio codecs.

九、绕开专利的"双重创造力"

So the main difference would be that — and because you need to work around the patents, or go do some things that are not patented, a lot of things are different, right. The basic things that were done in MPEG-2 30 years ago are out of patents. But so for example, there are things like a golden frame, an S frame, or different types of —

these are all patented ideas?

Yeah. No, it's "I can't believe it's not butter." "I can't believe it's not a B-frame." I mean, it's kind of what it is in some ways. It's like a —

Oh, so it's a different variant of a B-frame.

Yeah. That's to try and sidestep things like that.

And so you need to have double creativity, right? Creativity in terms of being more efficient, but creativity of being sure that you don't infringe existing patents. And so for example, VVC has all the patents of HEVC plus new ones, right — while AV2 tries to be as royalty-free as possible.

十、FFmpeg、VLC 与法国对软件专利的拒绝

To what degree do FFmpeg and VLC have to think about this kind of stuff?

We don't. And one of the reasons why VLC was in France is that France rejects software patents. So most of those patents are illegal in France. Because I once made the calculus that if I had to pay all the licensing fees for VLC, I needed to pay more than €200 per user, right? The same in dollars.

But most of those patents are invalid in Europe, because those are called — it's basically mathematical patents or idea patents — and they are not valid in Europe.

观看原视频 →

## 一、专利地雷阵：编解码器其实是一场"数学的圈地运动"
最反直觉的观察是：**视频编解码器的演化，本质上不是技术史，而是法律史**。当 Jean-Baptiste 提到 HEVC 里"99.9% 的场景都用不到"的特性被故意塞进规范——只为多挂一个专利——你会突然意识到：H.266 的规范文件不是一份工程文档，而是一张**专利地契图**。每个"矩形分块"代替"方形分块"的小创意，都是有人在数学的虚空中插了一面小旗子说"这块地是我的"。
而 AV1/AV2 的真正创新，**不是压缩算法的创新，而是"如何在已被插满旗帜的草地上跳舞而不踩到任何一面旗子"的创新**。这就是为什么 AV 系列里出现了"golden frame"、"S frame" 这种看似古怪的概念——它们是 B 帧的"替身演员"，在功能上几乎一样，只是为了**法律上的不一样**。所谓 "I can't believe it's not butter"（人造黄油）的玩笑，恰恰道出了整个开源编解码器世界的悲喜剧。

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Bronze Age radically altered Our DNA – David Reich
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最让人意外的发现：现代化反而"关闭"了选择压力

最反直觉的一点不是"青铜时代发生了选择"，而是最近2000年几乎没有任何对认知相关基因的自然选择信号。我们习惯把工业革命、识字率爆炸、城市化想象成"智力筛选器"——但数据说的恰恰相反：当人类开始建造学校、发明印刷术、进入复杂分工的时候，基因层面的方向性选择反而停了。

这暗示一个被低估的真相：文化进化一旦启动，就会"接管"生物进化的工作。当社会发明了替代基因的外部脚手架——文字、教育、专业分工——基因就不再需要被选择了。换句话说，文明可能不是大脑进化的产物，而是大脑进化的终点站。

二、跨领域连接：青铜时代是人类的"寒武纪大爆发"

把这个发现和经济学、生态学放在一起会出现一个奇妙的对应： - 复利的临界质量：Reich 自己点破了——3000年的复利和300年的复利是不同的物种。这其实和 Eric Beinhocker 在《财富的起源》里描述的经济复杂性涌现机制一模一样：当连接数量和时间窗口同时跨过阈值，系统会从线性增长跳到非线性爆发。 - 生态位构建（niche construction）：青铜时代不仅是金属冶炼，更是人类首次大规模改造自己所处的选择环境——定居农业、阶层社会、财富继承、长距离贸易。这些新环境创造了前所未有的选择梯度，就像加拉帕戈斯群岛对达尔文雀做的事一样。 - 与色素沉着同步的"认知选择"：这一点几乎没人提——肤色变浅和认知多基因评分的最强选择窗口完全重叠。这暗示驱动两者的可能是同一个生态—社会变量：高纬度定居+复杂社会需要的可能不是"两个独立适应"，而是一种综合的'温带定居者表型'。

三、隐藏的方法论革命：把进化当成"群岛实验"

很多人会忽略 Reich 描述的研究方法本身有多颠覆性。他们没有在做一个"全人类大平均"，而是把数据切成了 "时空小岛屿"——4000年前的不列颠、2000年前的匈牙利、1500年前的意大利……每一个都是一个独立的"自然实验"。

这其实是把面板数据计量经济学搬进了古基因组学：用固定效应（fixed effects）剔除迁徙带来的混淆，只看在祖源相对稳定的窗口里基因频率是否系统性偏移。

更深一层的洞见：这种方法暗示"人类"从来不是一个统一的进化单元，而是一个不断拆分、合并、再拆分的网络。单一的"人类进化叙事"可能本身就是个错觉——真正发生的是几千个小群体在各自的局部最优里漂移，偶尔被大迁徙搅成新的牌局。这和复杂系统理论里的"准物种（quasi-species）"概念惊人地吻合。

啊哈时刻：如果"近2000年没有选择"是真的，那意味着我们今天讨论的"先天禀赋"——其遗传基础几乎全部是青铜时代铸造的。你身体里那个所谓的"自然倾向"，可能不是来自原始狩猎采集者，而是来自一个手握青铜剑、骑马、酿啤酒、相信祖先神灵的远祖。我们都是青铜时代的孩子。

📝 转录文本

青铜时代：被低估的人类塑造期

The Bronze Age is not 300 years, it's 3,000 years. It's the power of compound interest, and you have enough time to begin to see a strong effect. But this really, really, really does seem to be a very impactful time in terms of human history.

And you can see it in our complex traits.

色素沉着：最强的选择信号

So for example, if you look at pigmentation — which is the strongest signal of selection for a complex trait in our data set. So you look at genetic mutations that are known to affect pigmentations. You add up their effect across all of the DNA — so there's dozens or hundreds of them — and you look to see in what time the natural selection is strongest.

And the time period is really 2,000 to 4,000 years ago. And for some of these other traits as well, you see again the time period over which the selection is strongest is 2,000–4,000 years ago.

认知相关基因：一个奇怪却真实的信号

So for example, if you look at genetic variants that affect measures of cognitive performance — for example, performance on intelligence tests in white British people today. So this is, of course, a very strange trait to measure in the past, because there were no intelligence tests and there was no school. But it is a predictor today, and you could look at how it's changed in the past.

And we see very strong natural selection for this combination of genetic variants that predicts people's performance on IQ tests, and also is highly correlated to the predictor that predicts the number of years of school or the household wealth of people. All crazy traits in the past, because there was no wealth in the past, there was no school in the past. But if you look at the predictors today, there is a strong, there is a strong movement in a systematic direction — a large effect, about a standard deviation on the scale of modern variation.

用"2000年滑动窗口"测量选择强度

And then we can do this trick of looking to see whether there's periods of time when this natural selection has occurred more intensely or less intensely. What we do is we drag a 2,000-year window through our data, and we repeat our whole analysis — not on 18,000 years, but just on a short 2,000-year window. And we can measure the strength of selection in each of these 2,000-year windows.

And what you see when you look at intelligence is: you see that this maxes out in the Bronze Age, between 5,000–4,000–3,000–2,000 years ago. And the impact in the last 2,000 years is almost nothing. There's no evidence of natural selection at all.

反直觉：近2000年几乎没有选择信号

You might think — your bias coming into this, my bias perhaps — if there's any signal of natural selection on this trait at all, might be that it would be unusually strong in the last 2,000 years. Maybe this is a time of industrialization, maybe this is a time of greater need for this particular trait. But in fact, there's no evidence of natural selection at all in the last 2,000 years.

But there's very strong evidence in between 2,000 and 4,000 years ago, where instead of a one standard deviation strength of selection, it's a two standard deviation strength sort of averaged over this time period.

方法论澄清：标准差指什么？

And the standard deviation here is how much the polygenic score for the trait itself moves, or…

How much the polygenic score trait moves over a 10,000-year period — got it — within a population that is held constant in terms of its ancestry. Got it.

Because what we're actually doing is we're looking in our data set at a kind of heterogeneous group of people. There's, you know, southern Europeans and northern Europeans, and hunter-gatherers and farmers, and at different times in the past those groups are more or less represented. So the whole strength of the methodology Eliakbari developed is it corrects for that changing ancestry over time.

"时空群岛"：把人类切成无数个小自然实验

And as I mentioned before, really what's being asked here is: we've divided up our whole data set into an archipelago of little populations in different places in space and time. And we're asking, in each place in space and time — a little pocket of people in Britain from 4,000 years ago to 3,500 years ago, a little pocket of people in Hungary, a little pocket of people in Italy from 2,000 years ago to 1,500 years ago — in each of these places, where the ancestry is relatively similar without being too disrupted in that short period by migrations, we watch to see if the genetic changes blow in the same direction.

And what we're doing here is we're measuring the strength of selection at each point in time after correcting for the big population changes that have occurred.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最让人意外的发现：现代化反而"关闭"了选择压力
最反直觉的一点不是"青铜时代发生了选择"，而是**最近2000年几乎没有任何对认知相关基因的自然选择信号**。我们习惯把工业革命、识字率爆炸、城市化想象成"智力筛选器"——但数据说的恰恰相反：当人类开始建造学校、发明印刷术、进入复杂分工的时候，基因层面的方向性选择反而停了。
这暗示一个被低估的真相：**文化进化一旦启动，就会"接管"生物进化的工作**。当社会发明了替代基因的外部脚手架——文字、教育、专业分工——基因就不再需要被选择了。换句话说，文明可能不是大脑进化的产物，而是大脑进化的**终点站**。
## 二、跨领域连接：青铜时代是人类的"寒武纪大爆发"
把这个发现和经济学、生态学放在一起会出现一个奇妙的对应： - **复利的临界质量**：Reich 自己点破了——3000年的复利和300年的复利是不同的物种。这其实和 Eric Beinhocker 在《财富的起源》里描述的经济复杂性涌现机制一模一样：当连接数量和时间窗口同时跨过阈值，系统会从线性增长跳到非线性爆发。 - **生态位构建（niche construct

item.title: [Lex Clips] Rust vs C vs Assembly programming languages - FFmpeg developer explains | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：Rust 的"安全承诺"在多媒体领域是一场幻觉

最让人意外的不是"重写很难"，而是Rust 在 FFmpeg/VLC 这类项目里根本无法兑现它的核心承诺。原因藏在一个大多数 Rust 布道者从未直面的事实里：当你处理 H.264、AV1 这类编解码器时，性能瓶颈处的代码 99% 是手写汇编。你把 C 层重写成 Rust，就像在金库门上加了三道密码锁，却发现金库的后墙是纸糊的——汇编可以跳到内存任何位置。Kieran 提出的"secure assembly"（编译时检查的汇编）才是真正的洞见：安全模型的强度，等于其最弱链条的强度，而 Rust 社区一直在加固已经最强的那一环。

跨领域连接：Sinclair C5、世界语、JavaScript 框架——同一种"乌托邦病"

这段对话里有个绝妙的三重类比，把 Rust 和三个看似无关的事物连了起来： - Sinclair C5 电动车（1985）：技术先进但忽视真实需求 - Esperanto 世界语（1887）：逻辑完美但社会拒绝接受 - JavaScript 框架军备竞赛：每个新框架都声称解决一切

它们共享一个深层模式——"工程师乌托邦综合症"：聪明人发明了一个在抽象层完美的解决方案，然后困惑于世界为何不立即采纳。这其实揭示了一个被忽视的系统性真相：技术替代不是"更好就赢"，而是"边际迁移成本 < 边际收益"才赢。Rust 在新项目里碾压 C++，恰恰因为新项目的迁移成本是零。

隐藏的认知不对称：为什么"重写冲动"是一种结构性错觉

对话里那句"写代码比读代码容易一个数量级"，看似平淡，实则是整个软件工程史的暗线。它解释了： - 为什么 LLM 擅长生成代码却不擅长理解遗留系统 - 为什么 Perl 程序员"六个月后看不懂自己的代码" - 为什么每代工程师都觉得前辈的代码是垃圾

这其实和考古学的"记录偏差"是同构的：考古学家挖出陶罐，但永远挖不出制作者的意图、当时的禁忌、邻里的纠纷。代码也一样——你能读到 if/else，读不到当年那个凌晨三点修复生产事故时的权衡。所谓"祖传代码的智慧"，本质是已被遗忘的失败经验的化石。重写，就是把这些化石全部丢弃，然后等着自己重新踩一遍坑。

更深一层的洞见是：LLM 时代会让"重写冲动"变得更危险，因为它把生成代码的成本压到接近零，但理解代码的认知成本几乎没变。这意味着我们正在进入一个"重写诱惑指数级膨胀，重写代价不变"的不对称时代——这恰恰是 Rust 社区当下面临的诱惑放大器。

📝 转录文本

关于 Rust：一个"世界语式"的乌托邦？

Q: Uh, what do you think about the Rust programming language? Cuz that's a bit of a meme.

We have very different opinions with Kieran. I think it's valuable what they're doing in terms of memory safety as a concept.

Q: Can they achieve some of the speed up that assembly achieves?

Uh, not assembly by hand, no. I think that that's a given. C, potentially. But I see it — it has a very big Esperanto vibe about it. It's like, "We're going to solve this, and we're doing this in a particular way."

Q: Meaning it's a bit too utopian?

There's a lot of focus on the self-importance rather than solving real-world problems. It reminds me of the Sinclair C5. Sir Clive Sinclair of Sinclair Computers built a car. And he said, "Oh, everyone will be traveling around in one of these electric cars." It first reminds me of that — where I think the community doesn't quite understand that in order to get people to move, you have to build something that's as good as, if not better than, what you have now.

最后 1% 决定一切

Yes, people are doing Rust rewrites, but if they only do 85–90% of the feature set of what we need — like things like core utils — that last 1% takes 99% of the time. To use Elon's famous quote: "Prototypes are easy." Like this kind of stuff is easy. But to get a real electric car, you have to make a car as good as, if not better than, what we have now. And Rust isn't in that stage yet.

I don't think anyone would object to seeing Rust code in FFmpeg, but it needs to work as well and support the same unit testing as everything else. It needs to be flawless. It can't just randomly break. They can't just randomly break ABI when they want to. It needs to have — I think it only still has only one compiler implementation. Yes. So it's got to be as good as, if not better. And saying, "Hey, here's my utopia of memory safety," isn't enough — even though we probably all agree that that's the goal.

VLC 的 Rust 实践经验

So, I've done a ton of Rust. The two major topics I had were adding Rust modules inside VLC. One of the reasons VLC got popular — and which was one of the main architectural decisions — is that VLC is a very small core and a ton of modules. And so you can write modules in C, C++, in Objective-C, and anything that is basically interoperable with C. And so we did some Rust modules. So I have experience on that, and I wrote some of it. And also, my new startup called Kyber is an open-source project mainly done in Rust.

What Rust is extremely good in, in the sense that, it's a better C++ that cares about memory and allows you to do things about memory ownership that no one else can do so far. However, it's great when you start a new project from scratch and you do everything in Rust. But it's very not good when you interop with existing parts.

"重写一切"的诱惑与陷阱

And some part of the Rust community believes that they need to rewrite everything, and everything will be better with Rust. And the answer is, "No." Like, I'm almost always — in all my years of being engineer, manager, CTO of startup, and so on — "Don't rewrite."

Q: Right? Is that the initial instinct for a lot of people when they show up to a codebase, probably before the LLMs — like probably because they don't understand the wisdom of the way things have been done in the past — to say, "Well, we need to rewrite it"? Hence why there's a thousand JavaScript frameworks.

But the reason is the following, and this is very important to understand: it is an order of magnitude easier to write code than read code. Yeah. And you see that also with LLMs. They can write code; analyzing is a lot more difficult.

And so when you arrive to a very complex piece of code, you don't understand it. Because it's so much more effort to understand the code from someone else, because you don't have the thought process. And often I joke about some languages — mostly Perl, for example, which has very complex syntax. Imagine I am at my maximum intellectual efficiency in programming, and I do write the best code ever. I will not be able to understand myself 6 months later. Because reading code is more difficult.

So very often you arrive, you don't understand all the wisdom, all the business logic, the reasons that were done that is maybe not documented. And you say, "Well, I'm going to rewrite it." And the thing is, no, you don't. Because, as Kieran said, "I'm going to rewrite core utils in Rust." And then of course, you arrive very quickly at 80%, then 90% takes a bit more time, and then you got the last ones, right? On the other side.

Rust 真正的甜蜜区

So, for new projects, it's great. Everything related to parsing files, networking — because of the memory checker, borrow checker — it's amazing, and there is nothing else.

多媒体领域的特殊困境：当汇编破坏一切安全模型

To answer a bit differently for us: imagine I take a piece of software like David or H.264, which has a ton of runtime in assembly. I rewrite the C part in Rust — so it's more secure. Yes. But then you arrive into the assembly, and you can jump anywhere in the memory, because we are doing handwritten assembly.

So even if I rewrite the C part in Rust for security reasons, you break all the security when you write handwritten assembly, because we can jump anywhere.

真正的方向：Secure Assembly

So, in my opinion, we need to do something that is secure assembly — which is compile-time-checked assembly, which is similar to the "check assembly" projects that we are doing on David and H.264 with VideoLAN: to start instrumenting your assembly at compile time to check that it's not jumping anywhere in the memory. Because else, you might rewrite a part of C in Rust, but if you want to have the same performance, you're going to have inline assembly, and so you destroy your whole security model.

Um, so that's a bit what I think about Rust.

观看原视频 →
